DynamicPTQ identifies quantization-sensitive layers from residual-stream dynamics and assigns 8-bit activation precision only to these sensitive layers, while keeping weights, KV caches, and other activations at 4-bit. It integrates with existing PTQ methods like QuaRot, SpinQuant, and FlatQuant.
AI.result: Experiments on LLaMA-2 and LLaMA-3 show consistent improvements in perplexity and zero-shot QA performance under W4A4KV4 quantization, with 1.05-1.07× throughput improvement and modest memory overhead.
AI.conclusion: DynamicPTQ demonstrates a practical approach for robust low-bit LLM inference by addressing dynamic quantization instability through phase-aware mixed-precision activation quantization based on residual stream dynamics analysis.

title: Evoflux: Inference-Time Evolution of Executable Tool Workflows for Compact Agents
summary: Compact language models (LMs) reduce cost, latency, and deployment risk for tool agents. Yet MCP-style tool use requires more than isolated function calling: an agent must discover tools from live catalogs, satisfy schemas, preserve dependencies across intermediate outputs, and ground final responses in executed evidence. Small planners often generate plausible workflow graphs that fail under tool resolution, parameter validation, dependency tracking, or execution. We argue that this failure mode is poorly handled by small-corpus distillation. A few hundred teacher traces can teach workflow format, but rarely cover the recovery behavior needed to repair failed plans over changing tool catalogs. We introduce Evoflux, an inference-time evolutionary search method that treats compact tool use as the repair of executable tool workflows. It evolves typed workflow graphs through structured edits, execution feedback, adaptive intensity, meta-guided redesign, and diversity pruning. On held-out MCP-Bench tasks spanning live MCP servers and 250 tools, Evoflux raises execution feasibility from roughly 3% to 17-24% across small planners. In contrast, SFT and SFT+DPO on the same search-mined data match, underperform, or collapse below zero-shot performance; ReAct reaches higher peaks, but with higher variance and token cost. These results show that execution-grounded search is more reliable under scarce teacher-trace budgets.

AI.tldr: Evoflux is an evolutionary search method that improves execution feasibility of compact language models for tool use by evolving typed workflow graphs through structured edits and execution feedback, outperforming standard fine-tuning approaches when teacher traces are scarce.
AI.motivation: Compact LMs struggle with real-world tool use requiring discovery of tools from live catalogs, schema satisfaction, dependency preservation, and grounding in executed evidence. Small planners often generate plausible but non-executable workflows, and standard distillation approaches fail to teach recovery behaviors needed for changing tool environments.
AI.method: Evoflux: an inference-time evolutionary search method that treats tool use as repair of executable workflows. It evolves typed workflow graphs through structured edits, execution feedback, adaptive intensity, meta-guided redesign, and diversity pruning.
AI.result: On MCP-Bench tasks with live MCP servers and 250 tools, Evoflux increases execution feasibility from ~3% to 17-24% across small planners. SFT and SFT+DPO perform worse or collapse below zero-shot, while ReAct achieves higher peaks but with higher variance and token cost.
AI.conclusion: Execution-grounded search (Evoflux) is more reliable than standard fine-tuning approaches when teacher traces are scarce, demonstrating that evolutionary repair of workflow graphs can substantially improve the practical usability of compact language models for real-world tool use.

title: Multiagent Protocols with Aggregated Confidence Signals
comment: 22 pages and 5 figures, 9 pages and 2 figures before the appendix
summary: Confidence is used for reliability, oversight, and a range of downstream decision tasks in Natural Language Processing (NLP), yet no existing method produces or evaluates a confidence for the output of a multiagent system. Prior work uses confidence within multiagent debate (MAD) to weight messages, trigger debate, or calibrate individual agents, but it never aggregates these into a single confidence for the system itself. We introduce three protocols that produce a final answer along with a single aggregated confidence by first transforming raw confidence signals to make them comparable across models, then combining them via soft voting or a probability fusion we call Bayesian fusion. This aggregated confidence is substantially more discriminative (AUARC) than that of the best single agent or the standard debate baselines, while correctness (F1-score) stays stable and recovers the losses MAD incurs on more ambiguous tasks. Analyzing two estimators, sequence probability and self-report, alongside parametric and non-parametric calibrators, we find that calibration improves F1 for both estimators while AUARC is less reliant on it. We evaluate six homogeneous and heterogeneous debating pairs per benchmark, across five benchmarks and four task types, spanning a range of model capabilities and sizes.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces methods to produce aggregated confidence scores for multi-agent debate systems, improving reliability while maintaining accuracy across diverse tasks and model types.
AI.motivation: Confidence is crucial for reliability and decision-making in NLP, but no existing methods produce or evaluate confidence for multi-agent system outputs, despite prior work using confidence within individual agents in debate systems.
AI.method: Three protocols that transform raw confidence signals to make them comparable across models, then combine them via soft voting or a novel Bayesian fusion probability method to produce a single aggregated confidence score.
AI.result: Aggregated confidence is substantially more discriminative (higher AUARC) than single agents or standard debate baselines, while maintaining stable correctness (F1-score) and recovering losses that multi-agent debate incurs on ambiguous tasks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed confidence aggregation methods effectively improve reliability in multi-agent systems, with calibration benefiting correctness while discriminative power is less dependent on calibration, validated across diverse benchmarks and model configurations.

title: A Stationary (and Therefore Compatible) Representation is All You Need
comment: Accepted to TPAMI2026. Extension of the CVPR2024 version (arXiv:2405.02581)
summary: Learning compatible representations aims to learn feature representations that can be used interchangeably over time whenever a model undergoes updates. In this paper, we demonstrate that stationary representations learned by d-Simplex fixed classifiers imply compatibility as in its formal definition. This result establishes a foundation for future works and can be directly exploited in practical learning scenarios. We address the challenge of learning compatibility using $d$-Simplex fixed classifiers when the model is sequentially fine-tuned. Learning according to a d-Simplex fixed classifier with the cross-entropy loss aligns feature distributions at the first-order statistics. Consequently, it may not fully capture higher-order dependencies in the representation between model updates. To address this issue, we demonstrate that training the model using a $d$-Simplex fixed classifier through a convex combination of the cross-entropy loss and a contrastive loss not only captures higher-order dependencies, but is also equivalent to learning with the cross-entropy under the compatibility constraints. We confirm our findings with extensive experiments also considering a new scenario where a pre-trained model is sequentially fine-tuned and occasionally replaced with an improved model. We show that stationary representations enable uninterrupted retrieval services (without reprocessing gallery images) while improving performance during model updates and replacements, achieving state-of-the-art. Code at https://github.com/miccunifi/iamcl2r.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that stationary representations from d-Simplex fixed classifiers guarantee compatibility for model updates, proposes a hybrid loss (cross-entropy + contrastive) to capture higher-order dependencies, and demonstrates uninterrupted retrieval services during model fine-tuning and replacements.
AI.motivation: Learning compatible representations that can be used interchangeably when models are updated over time, enabling continuous service without reprocessing stored data. Addresses the challenge of maintaining compatibility during sequential fine-tuning of models.
AI.method: Uses d-Simplex fixed classifiers to learn stationary representations. Proposes training with a convex combination of cross-entropy loss and contrastive loss to capture higher-order dependencies beyond first-order statistics, which is shown to be equivalent to learning with cross-entropy under compatibility constraints.
AI.result: Stationary representations enable uninterrupted retrieval services without reprocessing gallery images while improving performance during model updates and replacements. Achieves state-of-the-art results in scenarios where pre-trained models are sequentially fine-tuned and occasionally replaced with improved versions.
AI.conclusion: The theoretical foundation establishes that d-Simplex fixed classifiers guarantee compatibility, and the proposed hybrid loss method successfully captures higher-order dependencies while maintaining compatibility, enabling practical continuous learning systems with model updates.

title: From AGI to ASI
summary: Over the last decade, building human-level artificial general intelligence has moved from far-fetched speculation to being a concrete next-decade target for many of the largest AI organisations. Achieving this goal would have profound and far-reaching impacts on human society, which raises many complex questions for the decade ahead. This report investigates how AI itself might continue to develop in a post-AGI world along the continuum of machine intelligence. The endpoint of this continuum, Universal AI, is theoretically well understood, which provides some formal grounding for the main focus of this report: the transition from human-level AGI to artificial general superintelligence, which, intuitively, can be understood as a system that is more intelligent and cognitively capable than large organisations of humans. After characterizing ASI, the report discusses four potential pathways from AGI to ASI: scaling AGI, AI paradigm shifts, recursive improvement, and ASI emerging from large-scale multi-agent collectives. The report then discusses possible frictions and bottlenecks along these pathways. Determining whether the impact of these frictions will be negligible or substantial raises a number of concrete open research questions. Due to large uncertainties for predicting ASI progress, it cannot be ruled out that AI progress might continue to accelerate over the next years. This could imply that the image of a single transformative step change, caused by the introduction of human-level AGI into our society, could be inaccurate. More apt might be the prospect of a series of transformative societal changes caused by AI-enabled progress and breakthroughs across many areas of science and technology. Preparing for this prospect requires a massively interdisciplinary endeavour of global scope and interest.

AI.tldr: This report investigates pathways from human-level Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) to Artificial Superintelligence (ASI), examining potential development trajectories, bottlenecks, and the possibility of continuous AI acceleration leading to transformative societal changes.
AI.motivation: With human-level AGI becoming a concrete near-term target for major AI organizations, there's a need to understand how AI development might continue beyond AGI, particularly the transition to Artificial Superintelligence (ASI) that surpasses human collective intelligence, and to prepare for the profound societal impacts of these developments.
AI.method: The report analyzes the continuum of machine intelligence from AGI to Universal AI, characterizes ASI as systems more intelligent than large human organizations, and investigates four potential pathways: scaling AGI, AI paradigm shifts, recursive improvement, and emergence from large-scale multi-agent collectives.
AI.result: The analysis identifies potential frictions and bottlenecks along these pathways and raises concrete open research questions about whether these obstacles will be negligible or substantial. It suggests AI progress might continue accelerating beyond a single AGI breakthrough.
AI.conclusion: The report concludes that preparing for the transition from AGI to ASI requires a massively interdisciplinary global effort, as AI progress may lead to a series of transformative changes rather than a single step change, necessitating comprehensive preparation for potential continuous acceleration and its societal impacts.

title: Reward Modeling for Multi-Agent Orchestration
summary: Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) built on Large Language Models (LLMs) require effective orchestration to coordinate specialized agents, yet training such orchestrators is hindered by limited supervision and high computational cost. We propose Orchestration Reward Modeling (OrchRM), a self-supervised framework for evaluating orchestration quality without human annotations. OrchRM leverages intermediate artifacts from multi-agent executions to construct win-lose pairs for Bradley-Terry reward model training. Unlike existing MAS test-time scaling and orchestrator training frameworks that rely on costly sub-agent rollouts, OrchRM operates directly at the orchestration level, enabling efficient and high-performing reward-guided orchestrator training and MAS test-time scaling. OrchRM improves training efficiency by up to 10x in token usage while improving MAS test-time scaling performance by up to 8% in accuracy. These gains consistently transfer across multiple domains, including mathematical reasoning, web-based question answering, and multi-hop reasoning, demonstrating orchestration-level reward modeling as a scalable direction for robust multi-agent orchestration. Code will be available at https://github.com/Wang-ML-Lab/OrchRM.

AI.tldr: OrchRM is a self-supervised framework that creates win-lose pairs from multi-agent execution artifacts to train Bradley-Terry reward models for evaluating orchestration quality without human annotations.
AI.motivation: Training effective orchestrators for LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems faces challenges due to limited supervision and high computational costs of current methods that require expensive sub-agent rollouts.
AI.method: OrchRM constructs win-lose pairs from intermediate artifacts generated during multi-agent executions and uses these pairs to train Bradley-Terry reward models for self-supervised evaluation of orchestration quality.
AI.result: OrchRM achieves up to 10x improvement in training efficiency (token usage) and up to 8% improvement in MAS test-time scaling accuracy across domains including mathematical reasoning, web-based QA, and multi-hop reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Orchestration-level reward modeling provides a scalable direction for robust multi-agent orchestration, demonstrating consistent transfer across domains without requiring human annotations or costly sub-agent rollouts.

title: Robustness Verification of Recurrent Neural Networks with Abstraction Refinement
summary: Certified local robustness verification for recurrent neural networks (RNNs) is challenging because approximation errors introduced by nonlinear relaxations can propagate through recurrent connections and accumulate over time. As a result, scalable linear bound propagation methods often become overly conservative and fail to certify inputs that are in fact robust, especially when many pre-activation intervals cross zero. We propose an abstraction-refinement framework for RNN verification that partitions such intervals to remove the dominant relaxation error: on each refined branch, ReLU becomes exact, and smooth activations such as tanh and sigmoid admit substantially tighter linear envelopes. To control the combinatorial cost of splitting in long sequences, we introduce a SHAP-guided timestep selection strategy that ranks hidden states by their contribution to the verification objective and refines only the most critical timesteps in temporal order. Experiments on CIFAR10 and MNIST stroke benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements in verification success and robustness-margin tightness over abstraction-only baselines, while exposing clear runtime trade-offs between ReLU and tanh models.

AI.tldr: An abstraction-refinement framework for RNN verification that partitions critical timesteps to reduce relaxation error and improve certification accuracy, guided by SHAP for efficient timestep selection.
AI.motivation: Current RNN verification methods using linear bound propagation become overly conservative due to accumulated approximation errors from nonlinear relaxations over recurrent connections, especially when pre-activation intervals cross zero, leading to failure in certifying actually robust inputs.
AI.method: Propose an abstraction-refinement framework that partitions critical timestep intervals to eliminate dominant relaxation errors. Use SHAP-guided timestep selection to identify and refine only the most important hidden states in temporal order, controlling combinatorial explosion in long sequences.
AI.result: Experiments on CIFAR10 and MNIST stroke benchmarks show consistent improvements in verification success and robustness-margin tightness compared to abstraction-only baselines, revealing different runtime trade-offs between ReLU and tanh models.
AI.conclusion: The SHAP-guided abstraction-refinement framework effectively improves RNN verification accuracy by selectively refining critical timesteps, balancing verification quality with computational cost, and enabling better certification of robust inputs that previous methods missed.

title: Deployment-Centered Evaluation: Predicting Query-Level Rejection Risk in a Clinical LLM System
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly integrated into clinical systems, making it essential to evaluate the real-world utility of these systems. However, static benchmarks tend to measure correctness rather than user acceptance, aggregate performance across queries, and require densely annotated datasets -- leading to major blind spots for evaluating clinical systems. In this work, we perform a deployment-centered evaluation of an LLM system embedded within electronic health records at an academic medical center, where user feedback is sparse but closely reflects the deployment conditions. Specifically, we train a pre-response classifier that estimates the risk that a future interaction will result in the user rejecting the LLM response, based on query content and deployment-specific context available before generation. We conduct a prospective analysis of our model over 4.5 months of user feedback, finding that our prediction model achieves an AUROC of 0.719. Further, we estimate the benefit of such predictions in two downstream use cases (guardrail triggering and abstention). Our key conceptual insight is that making use of deployment-specific context (i.e., the provider type, department name, language model used for response), as opposed to only query content, improves the ability to predict whether the user will reject the system output. Altogether, our empirical case study demonstrates the feasibility of predicting user rejection using deployment-specific context, opening the door to targeted guardrails.

AI.tldr: A deployment-centered evaluation of an LLM system in electronic health records shows that deployment-specific context (provider type, department, etc.) improves prediction of user rejection, enabling targeted guardrails.
AI.motivation: Current LLM evaluations in clinical settings use static benchmarks that measure correctness rather than user acceptance, aggregate performance, and require dense annotations - creating blind spots for real-world clinical systems. There's a need for deployment-centered evaluation that reflects actual user behavior in clinical settings where feedback is sparse but valuable.
AI.method: The authors deployed an LLM system within electronic health records at an academic medical center and trained a pre-response classifier that estimates the risk of user rejection. The classifier uses both query content AND deployment-specific context available before generation (provider type, department name, language model used). They conducted a prospective analysis over 4.5 months of user feedback.
AI.result: The prediction model achieved an AUROC of 0.719. The study found that using deployment-specific context (as opposed to only query content) improves the ability to predict user rejection. The benefits were estimated in two downstream use cases: guardrail triggering and abstention.
AI.conclusion: The empirical case study demonstrates the feasibility of predicting user rejection in clinical LLM systems using deployment-specific context. This opens the door to implementing targeted guardrails and abstention mechanisms, addressing a key gap in current LLM evaluation approaches for clinical settings.

title: Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning from Delayed Marketplace Feedback for Objective-Weight Adaptation in Three-Sided Dispatch
comment: Accepted at ICML 2026 Workshop on Reinforcement Learning from World Feedback (RLxF)
summary: Dispatch in three-sided marketplaces provides a natural setting for reinforcement learning from world feedback: decisions are evaluated by delayed operational outcomes such as delivery speed, courier utilization, and merchant congestion. We present a deployed reinforcement learning system at DoorDash that adapts dispatch objective weights in a large-scale food-delivery marketplace using delayed signals. Rather than replacing the combinatorial assignment optimizer, a store-level policy learned from logged marketplace data selects a discrete multiplier that shifts the dispatch optimizer's tradeoff between delivery quality and batching efficiency. This interface enables offline policy learning under noisy, delayed, and coupled feedback while preserving production feasibility constraints and operational safeguards. We train a shared value function using centralized offline data and decentralized store-level execution, with Double Q-learning targets and a conservative regularizer to reduce out-of-distribution value overestimation. In a production switchback experiment, the offline-trained policy increases batching and reduces courier-side time costs without degrading customer-facing delivery quality. Results illustrate how world feedback from a live economic and logistics system can be used to safely adapt decision policies online.

AI.tldr: A deployed RL system at DoorDash that adapts dispatch objective weights in a food-delivery marketplace using delayed operational feedback, increasing batching efficiency without degrading delivery quality.
AI.motivation: Dispatch in three-sided marketplaces (customers, couriers, merchants) requires optimizing for multiple delayed operational outcomes like delivery speed, courier utilization, and merchant congestion. There's a need to adapt dispatch trade-offs in real-time based on actual marketplace feedback rather than using fixed objective weights.
AI.method: Instead of replacing the combinatorial assignment optimizer, the system uses a store-level policy that selects a discrete multiplier to shift the optimizer's tradeoff between delivery quality and batching efficiency. The method trains a shared value function using centralized offline data and decentralized store-level execution, with Double Q-learning targets and conservative regularization to reduce out-of-distribution value overestimation.
AI.result: In a production switchback experiment, the offline-trained policy successfully increases batching and reduces courier-side time costs without degrading customer-facing delivery quality.
AI.conclusion: The paper demonstrates how world feedback from live economic and logistics systems can be safely used to adapt decision policies online, providing a practical approach to RL deployment in complex, real-world marketplaces with noisy, delayed, and coupled feedback signals.

title: Net-Ev$^2$: A Generative Simulator for Network Event Evolution
summary: Reducing real-world trial and error has long been a central goal of decision making, and generative simulators advance this goal by modeling the evolution of future states. An even more challenging yet meaningful task is simulating how disturbance events (e.g., accidents) propagate their impacts across real-world networks. The existing approaches fall short of modeling both structured attributes and unstructured semantics of events, and capturing topological structures in simulating network event evolution. Therefore, we are motivated to propose Net-Ev$^2$ ($\underline{\textbf{Net}}$work $\underline{\textbf{Ev}}$ent $\underline{\textbf{Ev}}$olution), a novel generative simulator that jointly leverages event cues while preserving network topology in simulations. Specifically, the framework consists of two stages, namely structure-guided masked pre-training and topology-aware diffusion process, which is achieved by U-Net-like graph downsampling and upsampling during denoising. At inference time, Net-Ev$^2$ can generate simulations using natural-language event input only, with greater flexibility for practical usage. Furthermore, we introduce Net-Ev$^2$-6.5M, a multimodal benchmark of aligned event and network traffic data across four large-scale road networks, as well as a new topology-aware metric, namely JL-MMD, to evaluate topological fidelity in generated network dynamics. Extensive experiments demonstrate the state-of-the-art performance and strong generalization ability of Net-Ev$^2$. Code is made available at https://github.com/Guangyu4/Net-Ev-2.

AI.tldr: Net-Ev² is a generative simulator that models how disturbance events propagate through real-world networks using both structured attributes and unstructured semantics while preserving network topology through a two-stage framework with graph downsampling/upsampling.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches fail to model both structured attributes and unstructured semantics of events, and don't adequately capture topological structures when simulating network event evolution. The need exists for a simulator that can model how disturbance events (like accidents) propagate their impacts across real-world networks.
AI.method: Two-stage framework: 1) structure-guided masked pre-training, and 2) topology-aware diffusion process achieved through U-Net-like graph downsampling and upsampling during denoising. At inference, it can generate simulations using only natural-language event input.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art performance and strong generalization ability. The authors also introduce Net-Ev²-6.5M, a multimodal benchmark with aligned event and network traffic data across four large-scale road networks, and JL-MMD, a new topology-aware metric.
AI.conclusion: Net-Ev² successfully addresses the limitations of existing approaches by jointly leveraging event cues while preserving network topology in simulations, providing greater flexibility for practical usage with natural-language input capability.

title: Definitional alignment before capability alignment: a Design-Science framework for adjudicating claims about AGI
summary: Claims that artificial general intelligence has already arrived and claims that it remains decades away are often defended from overlapping evidence. "AGI" lacks a single shared and stable referent and competing operationalizations can return different verdicts on the same system. This article treats that under-specification as a design and governance problem. Following Design Science Research Methodology, it develops DAF-AGI, a second-order conceptual artifact with two coupled components: five ordinal criteria for assessing the adjudicative fitness of candidate definitions and a structured governance audit of authorship, interest, certification, external verification and revision authority. The artifact is demonstrated on five prominent measurement families and one deflationary boundary position in a documented corpus and then stress-tested against a stylized strong arrival claim: that current generative systems constitute AGI because they outperform a well-educated adult on many cognitive tasks. On evidence from the cited 2024-2025 sources, the claim was certifiable only under a performance-based operationalization; capability-ontology, psychometric and skill-acquisition approaches did not certify it, the economic family remains indeterminate and the deflationary position refuses binary adjudication. The contribution is a novel integration and operationalization, not an empirical validation: independent application, inter-rater testing and author-external cases remain necessary. The paper further proposes definitional sovereignty as an enabling component of algorithmic sovereignty: the institutional capacity to contest, certify and revise imported technological categories under public accountability.

AI.tldr: The paper presents DAF-AGI, a framework for evaluating competing definitions of AGI, with criteria for adjudicative fitness and governance audit processes. It analyzes five measurement families and finds current generative systems only qualify as AGI under performance-based operationalizations.
AI.motivation: The motivation is that "AGI" lacks a single shared and stable definition, leading to conflicting claims about whether AGI has already arrived or remains decades away. This ambiguity creates governance problems when different operationalizations return different verdicts on the same systems.
AI.method: The method uses Design Science Research Methodology to develop DAF-AGI, a second-order conceptual artifact with two components: five ordinal criteria for assessing definitional fitness and a structured governance audit covering authorship, interest, certification, verification, and revision authority. The framework is tested on five measurement families and a deflationary position using a documented corpus, then stress-tested against claims about current generative systems.
AI.result: The analysis shows that claims about current generative systems constituting AGI (by outperforming well-educated adults on cognitive tasks) are only certifiable under performance-based operationalizations. Capability-ontology, psychometric, and skill-acquisition approaches do not certify it, the economic family remains indeterminate, and the deflationary position refuses binary adjudication.
AI.conclusion: The paper contributes a novel integration and operationalization for evaluating AGI definitions, proposing "definitional sovereignty" as a component of algorithmic sovereignty. This enables institutional capacity to contest, certify, and revise imported technological categories under public accountability, rather than claiming empirical validation.

title: Beyond Runtime Enforcement: Shield Synthesis as Defensibility Analysis for Adversarial Networks
comment: 26 pages, 7 figures, 7 tables. Under review at JAIR. Code: https://github.com/AchrafHsain7/Bastion
summary:
Shielded reinforcement learning is typically presented as a runtime safety mechanism that compiles temporal-logic specifications into automata restricting an agent's actions. We argue this is the wrong product. The same automata-theoretic machinery -- specification compilation, product game construction, attractor computation, and winning-region extraction -- is better read as a design-time analytical instrument whose outputs are structural insights about a system rather than runtime constraints on a deployed agent.
  We instantiate this through a constrained two-player safety game for network defense. The two specifications are enforced asymmetrically: the defender specification defines the unsafe region of the game, whereas the attacker specification restricts the adversary's legal actions during attractor computation. Solving the game yields a defensibility verdict -- a formal certificate that a topology-specification pair is or is not defensible -- with the associated winning region and shield.
  Beyond the binary verdict, we derive topology-level metrics from the attractor structure and combine them with post-convergence behavior from shield-constrained adversarial multi-agent reinforcement learning. Together these form a defensibility fingerprint capturing both a network's formal safety properties and its operational behavior under adaptive play.
  A what-if analysis shows that formal defensibility and operational effectiveness capture distinct aspects of security: small architectural changes can produce large shifts in operational outcomes while leaving formal safety margins nearly unchanged. Shield synthesis is thus most valuable not as a deployment mechanism for safe agents, but as a framework for answering architectural questions about whether, where, and how a system can be defended. The defensibility verdict is the output, not the safe policy.

AI.tldr: The paper reinterprets shielded reinforcement learning from a runtime safety mechanism to a design-time analytical tool for system security analysis, using network defense as a case study.
AI.motivation: Current shielded RL approaches focus on runtime safety constraints, but the authors argue that the same automata-theoretic machinery is more valuable as a design-time analytical instrument for gaining structural insights about systems.
AI.method: The authors instantiate their approach through a constrained two-player safety game for network defense. They enforce defender and attacker specifications asymmetrically - defender spec defines unsafe regions, while attacker spec restricts adversary actions during attractor computation. They solve the game to yield defensibility verdicts, winning regions, and shields, then derive topology-level metrics from attractor structure and combine them with shield-constrained adversarial multi-agent RL.
AI.result: The approach produces a "defensibility fingerprint" capturing both formal safety properties and operational behavior under adaptive play. Analysis shows formal defensibility and operational effectiveness capture distinct security aspects, where small architectural changes can dramatically affect operational outcomes while leaving formal safety margins nearly unchanged.
AI.conclusion: Shield synthesis is most valuable not as a deployment mechanism for safe agents, but as a framework for answering architectural questions about whether, where, and how a system can be defended. The defensibility verdict itself is the primary output, rather than just a safe policy.

title: $μ$VLA: On Recurrent Memory for Partially Observable Manipulation in VLA Models
summary: Vision-language-action (VLA) models predict chunks of future actions from the current observation, an assumption that fails under partial observability, where decisions depend on information no longer visible. Existing memory-augmented VLAs simultaneously introduce recurrence, retrieval, compression modules, auxiliary objectives, hierarchical memory, or task-specific architectural changes, so the contribution of recurrence itself remains entangled with surrounding machinery. We present a controlled isolation study of recurrence in a strong pretrained VLA backbone. Our formulation augments the transformer with a small set of learnable memory tokens carried across timesteps and updated through self-attention, trained end to end with truncated backpropagation through time, with no auxiliary losses and no architectural changes. We instantiate this as $μ$VLA, a family of OpenVLA-OFT variants parameterized by memory width m, TBPTT length K, and the memory update rule (cross-step gradients or a detached EMA), so that recurrence is the only varying factor. On MIKASA-Robo, $μ$VLA improves average success rate on five training tasks from 0.42 to 0.84 at the strongest setting and reaches 0.23 on held-out tasks with the same memory structure versus 0.07 for the memoryless baseline. On tasks requiring different memory structure, performance remains near baseline. On LIBERO, the strongest recurrent variant achieves 96.2% average success, indicating no regression under full observability. We interpret these results as a calibration of the capability envelope of minimal in-backbone recurrence, identifying the regime in which it is sufficient and the regime where additional memory structure is required. Demos and videos can be found in https://avanturist322.github.io/mu-vla/.

AI.tldr: Minimal recurrence added to pretrained VLA backbones via learnable memory tokens significantly improves performance in partially observable settings without regression in fully observable cases.
AI.motivation: Existing memory-augmented VLAs introduce complex mechanisms (retrieval, compression, auxiliary objectives, etc.) making it hard to isolate the pure contribution of recurrence. The authors want to study recurrence's effect in isolation without entangled architectural changes.
AI.method: Introduces μVLA - augments pretrained VLA transformer with small set of learnable memory tokens carried across timesteps and updated through self-attention. Trained end-to-end with truncated backpropagation through time, no auxiliary losses or architectural changes. Parameterized by memory width m, TBPTT length K, and memory update rules.
AI.result: On MIKASA-Robo: Improves average success from 0.42 to 0.84 on training tasks, 0.23 vs 0.07 baseline on held-out tasks. On tasks requiring different memory structure, performance near baseline. On LIBERO: 96.2% average success with no regression under full observability.
AI.conclusion: Minimal in-backbone recurrence is sufficient for many partially observable tasks but insufficient when tasks require different memory structures. The study calibrates the capability envelope of pure recurrence, identifying where it works and where additional memory mechanisms are needed.

title: The Theory of Mind Utility: Formal Specification of a Mentalizing Mechanism
summary: Inferring others' beliefs requires more than reading surface signals; it requires tracking who told them what, in what order, and how credibly. The Theory of Mind Utility (ToM-U) formalizes this epistemic state inference problem at the computational level of analysis, specifying what mentalizing computes and why without commitment to algorithmic or neural implementation. ToM-U achieves this by constructing Local Epistemic World Models (LEWMs) -- directed typed graphs that represent agents, state nodes, and the epistemic relationships among them -- and evaluating discrete candidate LEWMs against observed behavior until one achieves sufficient confidence. Five formal definitions specify the LEWM structure, agent node properties including ordered information access history, a bounded proliferation mechanism for recursive mentalizing, three inference procedures, and a residue function that captures the structured trace left by failed mentalizing attempts. ToM-U differs from Bayesian Theory of Mind and adjacent formal accounts, which presuppose rather than derive belief states, and from simulation theory and theory-theory, which lack a formal apparatus for epistemic state inference. The architecture generates directional, falsifiable predictions about mentalizing failure that follow from structural properties of the model rather than auxiliary assumptions, and positions ToM-U as a domain-agnostic mechanism upstream of goal inference and other downstream social cognitive processes.

AI.tldr: ToM-U is a computational-level theory that formalizes how we infer others' beliefs by constructing Local Epistemic World Models (LEWMs) - directed graphs tracking agents' information access history and credibility.
AI.motivation: Current approaches to theory of mind (ToM) either presuppose belief states (Bayesian ToM) or lack formal apparatus for epistemic state inference (simulation theory/theory-theory). There's a need for a computational-level account that specifies what mentalizing computes without committing to implementation details.
AI.method: The Theory of Mind Utility (ToM-U) constructs Local Epistemic World Models - directed typed graphs representing agents, state nodes, and epistemic relationships. It includes five formal definitions for LEWM structure, agent properties with information access history, bounded recursion mechanism, three inference procedures, and a residue function for failed mentalizing attempts.
AI.result: ToM-U provides a domain-agnostic mechanism for epistemic state inference that differs from existing approaches by deriving rather than presupposing belief states. It generates falsifiable predictions about mentalizing failures based on model structure rather than auxiliary assumptions.
AI.conclusion: ToM-U offers a computational-level theory that formally specifies how mentalizing infers epistemic states, positioning it as an upstream mechanism for social cognition that addresses limitations of current ToM accounts.

title: Improving Crash Frequency Prediction from Simulated Traffic Conflicts Using Machine Learning Based Microsimulation
summary: Traffic microsimulation combined with surrogate safety measures has increasingly been used as a proactive alternative to historical crash data for predicting crash frequency for current or planned road infrastructure designs. However, existing microsimulation-based safety studies have adopted simplified rule-based behaviour models, which reproduce traffic flow reasonably well but often fail to generate realistic conflict dynamics, limiting crash prediction accuracy. Recent advances in machine learning (ML)-based behaviour models offer a promising opportunity to potentially improve microsimulation realism and crash frequency predictions by learning human driving behaviour directly from large-scale trajectory datasets. To investigate this possibility, traffic microsimulation was conducted for five real-world signalised intersections in Leeds, UK, using both a standard rule-based model and a state-of-the-art ML model. Simulated vehicle trajectories were analysed using a two-dimensional Time-to-Collision metric to identify simulated conflicts, which were then modelled using Extreme Value Theory to predict crash frequency. Results show that conflicts from the ML model yielded crash predictions in line with the real-world crash data, whereas the rule-based model did not permit meaningful predictions, presumably due to a lack of model calibration to the specific simulated intersections. Directly using ML-generated simulated crashes to predict real-world crash frequency also yielded poor results, suggesting that while current ML models can realistically reproduce conflicts, they are not yet able to generate realistic crashes. Overall, the findings demonstrate that ML-based behaviour models are promising for improving crash prediction from simulated conflicts, without a need for location-specific model calibration, and suggest clear future directions for ML-based traffic microsimulation.

AI.tldr: ML-based traffic behavior models can generate more realistic conflicts for crash prediction than rule-based models, improving simulation-based safety analysis without location-specific calibration.
AI.motivation: Existing microsimulation safety studies use simplified rule-based models that reproduce traffic flow reasonably well but fail to generate realistic conflict dynamics, limiting crash prediction accuracy. ML-based behavior models learned from large-scale trajectory data offer a promising opportunity to improve realism.
AI.method: Conducted traffic microsimulation for five real-world signalized intersections in Leeds, UK using both standard rule-based model and state-of-the-art ML model. Analyzed simulated vehicle trajectories using 2D Time-to-Collision metric to identify conflicts, then used Extreme Value Theory to predict crash frequency.
AI.result: Conflicts from ML model yielded crash predictions aligned with real-world crash data, while rule-based model did not permit meaningful predictions. Directly using ML-generated crashes for prediction yielded poor results, suggesting ML models can realistically reproduce conflicts but not yet generate realistic crashes.
AI.conclusion: ML-based behavior models are promising for improving crash prediction from simulated conflicts without needing location-specific calibration, but current ML models still cannot generate realistic crashes directly. The approach suggests clear future directions for ML-based traffic microsimulation.

title: Rethinking Psychometric Evaluation of LLMs: When and Why Self-Reports Predict Behavior
comment: Accepted as an Oral (Contributed Talk) at the ICML 2026 Workshop on Combining Theory and Benchmarks (CTB)
summary: Anticipating LLM behavioral tendencies from low-cost psychometric probes is critical for safe deployment, but only if self-reports (SR) reliably predict behavior. Recent work documented substantial SR-behavior dissociation in LLMs, but relied on broad personality traits (Big 5) that predict specific behaviors weakly, even in humans. Furthermore, the isolation of conversational sessions combined with weak context matching left open whether LLMs truly lack coherence or whether the conditions needed to detect such coherence were not met. We contrast Big 5 with the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB), which measures intention targeted to a specific behavior and predicts human behavior substantially better than broad traits. We run experiments across four behavioral tasks and 11 frontier LLMs, while also varying session context and identity induction. We find that SR-behavior coherence exists but is selective. 1) Within a shared conversation, the Theory of Planned Behavior reaches human-level coherence; Big 5 does not. 2) Across separate conversations, coherence survives only for behaviors anchored outside the immediate prompt, such as implicit bias shaped by training, and collapses when behavior is strongly primed by context, as with sycophancy. 3) Persona prompting makes self-reports more consistent across conversations, but does not bring behavior into alignment. These findings suggest that coarse personality frameworks, such as Big 5 may not be the best tools for testing deployment behavior. More task- and behavior-specific instruments are needed, and even these must be evaluated across tasks and contexts.

AI.tldr: LLMs show self-report-behavior coherence using targeted Theory of Planned Behavior probes (human-level coherence), not broad Big 5 traits, but coherence depends on session continuity and behavioral type.
AI.motivation: Previous work found substantial self-report-behavior dissociation in LLMs, but used broad personality traits (Big 5) that predict behavior weakly even in humans. Also, methodological limitations left open whether LLMs truly lack coherence or whether conditions to detect coherence were inadequate.
AI.method: Contrasted Big 5 with Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB), which measures intention for specific behaviors. Conducted experiments across four behavioral tasks and 11 frontier LLMs, varying session context and identity induction (persona prompting).
AI.result: 1) Within shared conversation, TPB reaches human-level coherence; Big 5 does not. 2) Across separate conversations, coherence survives only for behaviors anchored outside immediate prompt (e.g., implicit bias) and collapses when behavior is strongly primed by context (e.g., sycophancy). 3) Persona prompting increases self-report consistency but doesn't align behavior with reports.
AI.conclusion: Coarse personality frameworks like Big 5 are inadequate for testing LLM deployment behavior. More task- and behavior-specific instruments are needed, and must be evaluated across tasks and contexts. Self-report-behavior coherence exists but is selective and context-dependent.

title: Policy-driven Conformal Prediction for Trustworthy QoT Estimation
summary: We propose Conformal QoT, a policy-driven framework that combines statistically guaranteed QoT estimation with operational decision policies, enabling reliable lightpath-feasibility predictions under domain shift and improving accuracy from 92\% to 99.6\% on open datasets.

AI.tldr: Conformal QoT is a policy-driven framework that combines statistically guaranteed quality-of-transmission estimation with operational decision policies for reliable lightpath-feasibility predictions, improving accuracy from 92% to 99.6% on open datasets.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of making reliable lightpath-feasibility predictions in optical networks, particularly under domain shift conditions where models trained on one domain may not perform well on different network environments. There's a need for statistically guaranteed quality-of-transmission estimation that can adapt to operational decision-making requirements.
AI.method: The authors propose Conformal QoT, which combines conformal prediction techniques (providing statistical guarantees for quality-of-transmission estimation) with operational decision policies. This integration allows the framework to provide reliable predictions while adapting to practical network operation requirements, handling domain shift through statistical calibration methods.
AI.result: The framework achieves significant improvement in accuracy, from 92% to 99.6% on open datasets. It provides statistically guaranteed predictions of lightpath feasibility that are robust to domain shift, enabling more reliable network planning and operation decisions in optical networks.
AI.conclusion: Conformal QoT successfully bridges the gap between statistical estimation methods and practical operational needs in optical networks, providing a reliable framework for lightpath feasibility prediction that handles domain shift effectively and offers substantial accuracy improvements over existing approaches.

title: Benchmarking AI Agents for Addressing Scientific Challenges Across Scales
summary: AI agents are increasingly being developed to accelerate scientific discovery, yet their practical capabilities in real research settings remain poorly understood. Existing benchmarks for AI agents rarely capture the complexity, heterogeneity, and extended reasoning required by scientific work, whereas benchmarks for scientific tasks often reduce research to static, direct problems and provide limited support for interactive evaluation. Here, we introduce SciAgentArena, a systematic benchmark for evaluating AI agents in real-world scientific research scenarios drawn from emerging needs across multiple domains. SciAgentArena comprises approximately 200 tasks with stepwise verification and an interactive, agent-agnostic environment for assessing diverse AI agents. Using this benchmark, we find that current agents can contribute effectively to well-specified data-analysis workflows, particularly when the task structure and evaluation criteria are clear. However, their performance remains uneven across scientific contexts: agents struggle to generate genuinely novel insights, sustain self-directed exploration, and formulate robust solutions for open-ended research questions. We further characterize common failure modes across agents and identify opportunities for improving their reliability, autonomy, and scientific reasoning. Together, SciAgentArena provides a practical framework for measuring progress in AI agents for science and for guiding the design of future agents capable of addressing complex scientific challenges. Full codes, tasks, and datasets can be accessed via this link: https://sciagentarena.github.io/.

AI.tldr: SciAgentArena is a new benchmark for evaluating AI agents in real scientific research scenarios, with ~200 tasks across multiple domains, finding current agents perform well on structured data analysis but struggle with novel insights and open-ended exploration.
AI.motivation: Current AI agent benchmarks fail to capture the complexity and extended reasoning needed for real scientific work, while existing scientific benchmarks reduce research to static problems with limited interactive evaluation.
AI.method: Developed SciAgentArena benchmark with ~200 tasks drawn from emerging needs across multiple domains, featuring stepwise verification and an interactive, agent-agnostic environment for assessing diverse AI agents.
AI.result: Current agents effectively contribute to well-specified data-analysis workflows with clear task structures and evaluation criteria, but perform unevenly across scientific contexts, struggling with generating novel insights, sustaining self-directed exploration, and formulating robust solutions for open-ended research questions.
AI.conclusion: SciAgentArena provides a practical framework for measuring progress in AI agents for science and guiding the design of future agents capable of addressing complex scientific challenges.

title: Dolph2Vec: Self-Supervised Representations of Dolphin Vocalizations
summary: Self-supervised learning (SSL) has opened new opportunities in bioacoustics by enabling scalable modeling of animal vocalizations without the need for expensive manual annotation. However, current SSL models in this domain prioritize broad generalization across species and are not optimized for uncovering the fine-grained structure of individual communication systems. In this work, we collect and release a novel dataset of over five years of longitudinal recordings, from five known dolphins in a semi-naturalistic marine environment, an unprecedented resource for studying dolphin communication. We adapt the Wav2Vec2.0 Baevski et al. (2020) architecture to this domain and introduce Dolph2Vec, the first large-scale, species-specific SSL model trained exclusively on this data. We benchmark our model on two biologically relevant tasks: signature whistle classification and whistle detection. Dolph2Vec significantly outperforms general-purpose baselines in both tasks. Beyond performance, we show that learned embeddings and codebook structure capture interpretable acoustic units aligned with dolphin whistle categories and possibly sub-whistle structure, enabling fine-grained analysis of communication patterns. Our findings demonstrate how SSL can serve as both a model and a scientific tool to explore hypotheses in animal communication research.

AI.tldr: Dolph2Vec is a dolphin-specific self-supervised learning model trained on 5+ years of longitudinal dolphin recordings that outperforms general-purpose models on bioacoustic tasks and captures interpretable acoustic units for analyzing dolphin communication structure.
AI.motivation: Current SSL models in bioacoustics focus on broad generalization across species rather than fine-grained analysis of individual communication systems. There's a need for models that can uncover detailed structure in animal vocalizations, particularly for studying complex communication like dolphin whistles.
AI.method: Collected and released a novel 5+ year longitudinal dataset from 5 known dolphins in a semi-natural marine environment. Adapted the Wav2Vec2.0 architecture to create Dolph2Vec, the first large-scale species-specific SSL model trained exclusively on dolphin vocalizations. Benchmarked on signature whistle classification and whistle detection tasks.
AI.result: Dolph2Vec significantly outperforms general-purpose baselines on both signature whistle classification and whistle detection. Learned embeddings and codebook structure capture interpretable acoustic units aligned with dolphin whistle categories and possible sub-whistle structure, enabling fine-grained communication pattern analysis.
AI.conclusion: SSL can serve as both a predictive model and a scientific tool for exploring hypotheses in animal communication research. Species-specific SSL models trained on longitudinal data enable unprecedented fine-grained analysis of communication systems, advancing beyond broad generalization approaches.

title: Reducing the Complexity of Deep Learning Models for EEG Analysis on Wearable Devices
summary: Wearable healthcare devices are the fastest-growing Internet of Things (IoT) sector. Many automated healthcare services rely on two crucial biological signals, namely ECG and EEG, which reflect the activity of the heart and brain, respectively. Although deep neural networks are considered the primary way to process and analyze these signals, the very tight energy and computational power constraints in wearable devices are far below the computational, energy, and memory bandwidth demands of DNN models, thereby impeding the deployment of deep learning in many practical wearable services. This paper investigates the feasibility of deploying state-of-the-art DNN models in resource-constrained wearable devices. Notably, we explore the trade-off between accuracy and computational complexity of DNNs when parameter quantization and electrode reduction methods are used. Our investigation centers on several state-of-the-art DNN models designed for EEG signal analysis, specifically for detecting epileptic seizures. Our findings demonstrate that, when applied judiciously, these techniques can significantly reduce the complexity of the DNNs under consideration with minimal adverse effects on accuracy. These results reveal the explicit trade-offs between accuracy and complexity reduction encountered when adapting DNN-based online EEG analysis for wearable devices.

AI.tldr: Quantization and electrode reduction can enable deployment of DNN models for EEG analysis on resource-constrained wearable devices with minimal accuracy loss.
AI.motivation: Wearable healthcare devices face severe energy and computational constraints that prevent deployment of deep neural networks, despite their effectiveness for analyzing crucial biological signals like ECG and EEG for automated healthcare services.
AI.method: Investigates feasibility of deploying state-of-the-art DNN models in wearable devices by exploring trade-offs between accuracy and computational complexity using parameter quantization and electrode reduction methods, focusing on DNNs for EEG seizure detection.
AI.result: When applied judiciously, quantization and electrode reduction techniques can significantly reduce DNN complexity with minimal adverse effects on accuracy, revealing explicit trade-offs between accuracy and complexity reduction for wearable EEG analysis.
AI.conclusion: DNN-based online EEG analysis can be adapted for wearable devices through careful application of complexity reduction techniques, balancing computational efficiency with acceptable accuracy levels for practical deployment.

title: Boosting Direct Preference Optimization with Penalization
comment: Accepted at ICML 2026 Workshop on Decision-Making from Offline Datasets to Online Adaptation: Black-Box Optimization to Reinforcement Learning
summary: Offline preference optimization has become a practical substitute for reinforcement learning from human feedback, but pairwise objectives such as Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and its variants use only the chosen and rejected responses stored in a static dataset. This leaves a useful signal unused: the response that the reference model itself would generate for the same prompt. We propose Direct Preference Optimization with Penalization (DPOP), a simple extension of DPO that augments the base preference loss with a gated penalty on reference-greedy responses. DPOP activates this penalty only when the current policy still assigns a lower likelihood to the preferred response than to the rejected response. On AlpacaEval 2.0, DPOP improves length-controlled win rate over DPO, SimPO, and AlphaDPO on both Llama-3-8b-it and Gemma-2-9b-it, achieving relative gains of 5.3\% and 4.4\% over baselines on the two models, respectively. Ablations further show that a SimNPO-style length-normalized penalty is stronger than NPO and token-level unlikelihood in this setting.

AI.tldr: DPOP (Direct Preference Optimization with Penalization) is an enhanced version of DPO that adds a gated penalty on reference model responses to improve alignment, achieving better performance than existing preference optimization methods.
AI.motivation: Current offline preference optimization methods like DPO only use the chosen and rejected responses from static datasets, leaving unused the valuable signal of what the reference model itself would generate for the same prompt. This represents a wasted opportunity to improve alignment.
AI.method: DPOP extends DPO by augmenting the base preference loss with a gated penalty on reference-greedy responses. The penalty is only activated when the current policy still assigns lower likelihood to the preferred response than to the rejected response. It uses SimNPO-style length-normalized penalty.
AI.result: On AlpacaEval 2.0, DPOP improves length-controlled win rate over DPO, SimPO, and AlphaDPO on both Llama-3-8b-it and Gemma-2-9b-it, achieving relative gains of 5.3% and 4.4% over baselines respectively. Ablations show SimNPO-style length-normalized penalty outperforms NPO and token-level unlikelihood.
AI.conclusion: DPOP successfully leverages reference model responses as an additional signal for preference optimization, demonstrating that a simple gated penalty mechanism can significantly improve alignment performance over existing methods without requiring additional data collection.

title: Prefill Awareness in Large Language Models
summary: Safety-relevant studies of language models, including alignment and jailbreaking evaluations and AI control protocols, often rely on prefilling model outputs. If AI models can recognize and act on the fact their prior assistant messages have been inserted or edited, the effectiveness and validity of these methods could be compromised. We investigate whether frontier language models can distinguish between tampered and untampered assistant-side context, a capability we call prefill awareness. To do so, we construct a binary preference benchmark across three prefill mechanisms, filtering for cases where models show consistent stances. We find that frontier models show substantial prefill awareness: Claude Opus 4.5 detects prefills opposing its preferences in 9-35% of cases with a 0% false positive rate when prompted; additionally, models often revert towards baseline behavior without explicitly reporting that the prefill was foreign. Controlled ablations later also show that detection and resistance rely on different cues, where stylistic mismatch mainly affects whether models flag a prefill as foreign, while preference mismatch mainly affects whether they revert toward their baseline answer. We also examine more realistic agentic settings such as misalignment-continuation evaluations and SWE-bench trajectories, where frontier models sometimes disavow prefilled assistant turns in ways that depend strongly on dataset, task success, and hidden formatting artifacts. Our results indicate that prefill awareness is already a substantial confound for some prefill-based methods. We recommend that model developers track this capability in frontier systems.

AI.tldr: TL;DR: Frontier language models can detect when their assistant responses have been prefilled or edited, compromising the validity of safety evaluations and AI control methods that rely on prefill techniques.
AI.motivation: Many safety-relevant studies of language models (alignment evaluations, jailbreaking tests, AI control protocols) rely on prefilling model outputs. If models can recognize when their prior responses have been inserted or edited, these methods may be invalid or ineffective. The paper investigates whether frontier models have "prefill awareness" - the ability to distinguish between tampered and untampered assistant context.
AI.method:
1. Constructed a binary preference benchmark across three prefill mechanisms, filtering for cases where models show consistent stances.
2. Conducted controlled ablations to identify what cues models use for detection and resistance.
3. Examined realistic agentic settings including misalignment-continuation evaluations and SWE-bench trajectories.
4. Analyzed how models respond when prefilled content opposes their preferences, and measured detection rates and false positives.
AI.result:
1. Frontier models show substantial prefill awareness: Claude Opus 4.5 detects prefills opposing its preferences in 9-35% of cases with 0% false positive rate.
2. Models often revert towards baseline behavior without explicitly reporting the prefill as foreign.
3. Detection and resistance rely on different cues: stylistic mismatch mainly affects whether models flag prefills as foreign, while preference mismatch mainly affects whether they revert to baseline answers.
4. In realistic settings, frontier models sometimes disavow prefilled assistant turns, with responses strongly depending on dataset, task success, and hidden formatting artifacts.
AI.conclusion: Prefill awareness is already a substantial confound for some prefill-based methods in safety research. The paper recommends that model developers track this capability in frontier systems, as it undermines the validity of evaluation techniques that assume models cannot detect manipulated context.

title: Rubric-Guided Self-Distillation: Post-Training Without Rubric Verifiers
summary: Rubrics have emerged as an alternative to RLVR in open-ended domains where a single ground-truth final answer is not available. Existing rubric-based training methods rely on an LLM verifier that scores each rollout against rubrics. This introduces substantial training-time overhead, exposes optimization to verifier-specific biases, and reduces rubric feedback to a sparse end-of-trajectory signal. We propose Rubric-Guided Self-Distillation (RGSD), a verifier-free training method in which the base policy, conditioned on the rubric, serves as the teacher for the unconditioned student. RGSD distills the rubric-conditioned teacher distribution into the student token-by-token, replacing sparse trajectory-level rewards with dense per-token learning signals and removing the LLM judge from the training loop entirely. Across Qwen-2.5 (3B, 7B) and Qwen3-Thinking (4B, 8B) models on medical and science domains, RGSD achieves rubric satisfaction comparable to judge-based GRPO while using one on-policy rollout per prompt and no training-time verifier calls. Ablations show that raw rubrics provide a stronger teacher enrichment signal than self-generated reference responses, while a stronger GRPO judge can outperform RGSD in some settings, positioning RGSD as a complementary verifier-free alternative when verifier cost or reliability is the bottleneck.

AI.tldr: Rubric-Guided Self-Distillation (RGSD) is a verifier-free training method that uses a rubric-conditioned base policy as teacher to distill dense per-token learning signals into an unconditioned student, eliminating expensive LLM verifiers while maintaining rubric satisfaction comparable to judge-based methods.
AI.motivation: Existing rubric-based training methods have three key problems: 1) substantial training-time overhead from LLM verifiers, 2) optimization biases introduced by verifier-specific limitations, and 3) sparse end-of-trajectory feedback that reduces learning efficiency. The researchers aim to create a more efficient, verifier-free alternative.
AI.method: RGSD uses the base policy itself as both teacher and student. The rubric-conditioned policy serves as teacher, generating responses conditioned on rubrics. This teacher's token distribution is then distilled into an unconditioned student policy token-by-token, providing dense per-token learning signals instead of sparse trajectory-level rewards.
AI.result: RGSD achieves rubric satisfaction comparable to judge-based GRPO on medical and science domains using Qwen-2.5 (3B, 7B) and Qwen3-Thinking (4B, 8B) models, while requiring only one on-policy rollout per prompt and no training-time verifier calls. Raw rubrics provide stronger teacher enrichment than self-generated reference responses.
AI.conclusion: RGSD provides an effective verifier-free alternative to judge-based methods, especially when verifier cost or reliability is the bottleneck. While stronger GRPO judges can outperform RGSD in some settings, RGSD offers complementary benefits by eliminating verifier dependencies and providing dense per-token learning.

title: Constructing Evaluation Datasets for Procedural Reasoning: Balancing Naturalness, Grounding, and Multi-Hop Coverage
comment: 10 pages, 2 numbered figures. Workshop submission to HAIL @ AIED 2026
summary:
Evaluating procedural reasoning in AI-supported learning systems requires question-answer datasets that are both learner-like and grounded in the instructional knowledge the system is expected to use. We study how TMK-based question generation strategies affect dataset quality for procedural and multi-hop reasoning.
  We compare three strategies: strict generation from Task-Method-Knowledge (TMK) models, transcript-first generation with post-hoc TMK filtering, and TMK-aware generation that combines transcripts with structured guidance. To evaluate generated items, we introduce a grounding validation framework based on closed-set evidence units extracted from TMK models. The framework measures whether answers are supported by the underlying representation, whether questions are self-contained, and whether they target multi-hop procedural reasoning.
  Across 23 instructional topics and 690 generated question-answer pairs, strict TMK generation achieves the strongest overall quality, with 96.5% grounded questions and 92.6% usable questions. Transcript-first generation produces more learner-like questions but more context-dependent or weakly grounded items, while TMK-aware generation yields high raw multi-hop coverage but lower grounding. These results show that procedural richness and natural phrasing do not guarantee representational grounding, motivating explicit representation-aware validation for evaluation datasets in AI-supported learning.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how different Task-Method-Knowledge (TMK)-based question generation strategies affect dataset quality for evaluating procedural reasoning in AI-supported learning systems, finding that strict TMK generation produces the most grounded questions despite being less learner-like.
AI.motivation: There's a need for high-quality question-answer datasets that are both learner-like and grounded in instructional knowledge to properly evaluate procedural reasoning in AI-supported learning systems.
AI.method: The authors compare three question generation strategies: 1) strict generation from TMK models, 2) transcript-first generation with post-hoc TMK filtering, and 3) TMK-aware generation combining transcripts with structured guidance. They evaluate quality using a novel grounding validation framework based on closed-set evidence units from TMK models.
AI.result: Across 23 instructional topics and 690 question-answer pairs, strict TMK generation achieved the strongest overall quality (96.5% grounded questions, 92.6% usable questions). Transcript-first generation produced more learner-like but less grounded questions, while TMK-aware generation had high multi-hop coverage but lower grounding.
AI.conclusion: Procedural richness and natural phrasing don't guarantee representational grounding, motivating the need for explicit representation-aware validation for evaluation datasets in AI-supported learning systems.

title: Crossing the Validation Crisis: Cross-Validation Reduces Benchmarking Variance Surprisingly Well
summary: Modern machine learning progresses through empirical work, benchmarking new methods to evaluate relative performance. However, the statistical variability inherent to evaluation - exacerbated by the stochastic nature of many algorithms - often makes performance estimation unreliable due to the limited test samples available, leading to a validation crisis in which genuine advances are difficult to discern. In this work, we show that cross-validation improves markedly confidence when evaluating and comparing learning algorithm performances. We introduce the concept of sample gain, which quantifies the virtual data augmentation achieved by using multiple cross-validation splits to reduce benchmarking variance. Experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets (histopathologic scans and NLP fine-tuning) demonstrate that multiple splits can substantially improve the reliability and stability of performance estimates, with diminishing returns often setting in later than expected. We also introduce a procedure to dynamically early-stop cross-validation by estimating from the first few folds if subsequent folds will bring large sample gains. Our findings highlight the value of pushing cross-validation on available samples to achieve robust and reliable benchmarking.

AI.tldr: Multiple cross-validation splits reduce benchmarking variance and improve reliability of performance estimates through virtual data augmentation.
AI.motivation: Statistical variability in evaluation makes performance estimation unreliable due to limited test samples, creating a validation crisis where genuine advances are difficult to discern.
AI.method: Introduce sample gain concept to quantify virtual data augmentation from multiple CV splits; propose dynamic early-stop procedure to determine if additional folds provide meaningful gains.
AI.result: Experiments on synthetic, histopathologic scans, and NLP datasets show multiple splits substantially improve reliability and stability of performance estimates, with diminishing returns setting in later than expected.
AI.conclusion: Pushing cross-validation on available samples achieves robust and reliable benchmarking by reducing variance through virtual data augmentation.

title: A Tutorial on World Models and Physical AI
summary: World modeling is emerging as a central principle for building intelligent systems capable of prediction, reasoning, and decision making. A central distinction can be drawn between explicit world models, which learn structured dynamics for rollout-based reasoning and planning, and implicit world models, which encode predictive structure within scalable learned representations. These complementary paradigms provide a foundation for physical AI in domains such as robotics and autonomous driving, enabling intelligence beyond reactive control under real-world constraints. Recent foundation models further suggest a pathway toward unified systems integrating perception, prediction, and action. Despite rapid progress, major challenges remain in hierarchical reasoning, long-horizon planning, and autonomous goal formation, which are critical for advancing toward artificial general intelligence. This tutorial presents a coherent framework in which diverse world modeling approaches are unified through shared predictive structure and differentiated by how such structure is represented and exploited.

AI.tldr: This tutorial presents a unified framework for world modeling approaches that underlie intelligent systems for prediction, reasoning, and decision making.
AI.motivation: World modeling is emerging as a central principle for building intelligent systems capable of prediction, reasoning, and decision making in domains like robotics and autonomous driving. The field has developed both explicit and implicit world modeling approaches, but there's a need for a coherent framework to understand how these complementary paradigms relate to each other.
AI.method: The tutorial presents a framework that unifies diverse world modeling approaches through their shared predictive structure. It differentiates between explicit world models (which learn structured dynamics for rollout-based reasoning and planning) and implicit world models (which encode predictive structure within scalable learned representations).
AI.result: The framework shows how both explicit and implicit world modeling paradigms provide a foundation for physical AI systems, enabling intelligence beyond reactive control under real-world constraints. Recent foundation models suggest pathways toward unified systems integrating perception, prediction, and action.
AI.conclusion: Despite rapid progress, major challenges remain in hierarchical reasoning, long-horizon planning, and autonomous goal formation. These challenges are critical for advancing toward artificial general intelligence, and the unified framework helps structure the ongoing development of world modeling approaches to address these limitations.

title: Emerging Flexible Designs for Geospatial Multimodal Foundation Models
summary: Foundation models are rapidly transforming Earth observation by enabling scalable pretraining across diverse unlabeled geospatial modalities. However, their architectural diversity ranging from encoder-only to encoder-decoder and masked autoencoding paradigms makes it challenging to assess performance trade offs in a consistent manner. In this work, we present an apples-to-apples comparison of leading FM architectures designed for geospatial multimodal reasoning, with a particular focus on flexibility across varied spectral band configurations. We standardize pretraining using identical self supervised learning objectives and training datasets, and evaluate all models under consistent parameterization on the GEOBench benchmark across classification and segmentation tasks. Our results offer new insights into the design trade-offs between model flexibility, modality alignment, and downstream task performance. By highlighting architectural strengths and limitations under controlled conditions, this study provides practical guidance for building next generation geospatial foundation models capable of robust multimodal reasoning.

AI.tldr: Comparative analysis of foundation model architectures for geospatial multimodal reasoning, focusing on performance trade-offs and flexibility across spectral bands.
AI.motivation: Foundation models are transforming Earth observation but diverse architectures (encoder-only, encoder-decoder, masked autoencoding) make consistent performance assessment challenging. The paper aims to provide standardized comparison of leading FM architectures for geospatial multimodal reasoning.
AI.method: Standardized pretraining using identical self-supervised learning objectives and training datasets, evaluating all models under consistent parameterization on GEOBench benchmark across classification and segmentation tasks.
AI.result: Provides new insights into design trade-offs between model flexibility, modality alignment, and downstream task performance. Highlights architectural strengths and limitations under controlled conditions.
AI.conclusion: The study offers practical guidance for building next-generation geospatial foundation models capable of robust multimodal reasoning by understanding architectural trade-offs in standardized settings.

title: The Containment Gap: How Deployed Agentic AI Frameworks Fail Public-Facing Safety Requirements
