summary: Agentic large language model systems that autonomously invoke tools, maintain persistent memory, and execute multi-step plans are increasingly deployed in public-facing domains, including government services, healthcare triage, and financial advising. We ask whether the frameworks used to build these systems provide architectural-level structural safety guarantees. Applying six containment principles derived from a compositional model of agentic architectures, we audit three dominant frameworks (LangChain, AutoGPT, and OpenAI Agents SDK) and find no native compliance in any of them. Memory integrity, a defense against one of the most prevalent vulnerability classes, is not observed in any of the three evaluated frameworks. We validate these findings empirically: in a simulated government benefits agent built on LangChain, a single memory-poisoning write induces persistent targeted corruption across all tested seeds and backends, increasing the wrongful denial rate for targeted applicants to 88.9%. Under a complex five-factor policy, the same attack preserves aggregate accuracy while increasing targeted wrongful denials by 3.5x, rendering the corruption difficult to detect through standard monitoring. We then introduce two lightweight containment mechanisms: a memory integrity validator and a policy gate, which eliminate both attack vectors with sub-millisecond overhead (<0.2ms per call). We conclude that the current agentic framework ecosystem may not yet meet secure-by-default expectations for public-facing deployments and outline priority architectural interventions to enable trustworthy deployment in high-stakes, socially impactful applications.

AI.tldr: Current AI agent frameworks lack built-in security safeguards, allowing memory-poisoning attacks that persistently corrupt agent behavior, but lightweight containment mechanisms can fix these vulnerabilities with minimal performance impact.
AI.motivation: As AI agent systems with autonomous tool invocation, persistent memory, and multi-step planning are increasingly deployed in high-stakes public domains (government services, healthcare, finance), there's a critical need to assess whether the development frameworks provide architectural safety guarantees and secure-by-default protection.
AI.method: The researchers applied six containment principles from a compositional model of agentic architectures to audit three major frameworks (LangChain, AutoGPT, OpenAI Agents SDK). They empirically validated findings through a simulated government benefits agent built on LangChain, testing memory-poisoning attacks that induce targeted corruption across different seeds and backends.
AI.result: No framework showed native compliance with containment principles, and memory integrity - a defense against prevalent vulnerabilities - was absent in all three. Memory-poisoning attacks achieved 88.9% wrongful denial rate for targeted applicants and increased targeted wrongful denials by 3.5x under complex policies while preserving aggregate accuracy (making detection difficult). However, two lightweight mechanisms (memory integrity validator and policy gate) eliminated both attack vectors with sub-millisecond overhead (<0.2ms per call).
AI.conclusion: Current agentic framework ecosystem doesn't meet secure-by-default expectations for public-facing deployments. While lightweight containment solutions exist, priority architectural interventions are needed for trustworthy deployment in high-stakes, socially impactful applications.

title: Viral Proteins Reveal Geometry of Protein Language Models
comment: Accepted at ICML 2026 GenBio Workshop and FM4LS Workshop. Code available at https://github.com/MisteFr/viral-proteins-plms
summary: Protein language models are trained on highly imbalanced datasets, raising the question of how they represent underrepresented biological sequences. Using viral proteins as a case study across ESM model families, we identify a dominant nativeness axis in embedding space, aligned with masked reconstruction perplexity, that orders sequences from well-modeled cellular proteins through viral proteins to shuffled and random sequences. Scaling contracts this axis unevenly across viral families. Despite this, protein language model embeddings retain viral-specific signal: viral proteins remain linearly separable beyond zero-shot perplexity and shallow sequence features. Together, these results suggest that pLM representations are structured by a general notion of nativeness while preserving information specific to distinct biological groups.

AI.tldr: Protein language models exhibit a dominant "nativeness axis" in embedding space that orders sequences by how well-modeled they are, with viral proteins showing both scaling contraction effects and retained viral-specific signal despite imbalanced training data.
AI.motivation: Protein language models are trained on imbalanced datasets, raising questions about how they represent underrepresented biological sequences like viral proteins.
AI.method: Used viral proteins as a case study across ESM model families, analyzing embedding spaces, masked reconstruction perplexity, and linear separability of viral vs. cellular proteins.
AI.result: Identified a dominant nativeness axis in embedding space that orders sequences from well-modeled cellular proteins → viral proteins → shuffled/random sequences. Scaling contracts this axis unevenly across viral families, but viral proteins remain linearly separable beyond zero-shot perplexity and shallow sequence features.
AI.conclusion: Protein language model representations are structured by a general notion of nativeness while preserving information specific to distinct biological groups, suggesting they handle underrepresented sequences through this dual mechanism.

title: MLUBench: A Benchmark for Lifelong Unlearning Evaluation in MLLMs
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are trained on massive multimodal data, making data unlearning increasingly important as data owners may request the removal of specific content. In practice, these requests often arrive sequentially over time, giving rise to the challenging problem of MLLM Lifelong Unlearning. However, most existing benchmarks are limited in scale and scope, failing to capture the complexities of MLLM lifelong unlearning. To fill this gap, we introduce the MLUBench, a large-scale and comprehensive benchmark featuring 127 entities across 9 classes under lifelong unlearning requests. We perform extensive experiments using MLUBench and reveal that existing unlearning methods suffer from severe, cumulative degradation. More critically, we further identify the unique challenge of this problem: unlike in unimodal models, MLLM lifelong unlearning is constrained by the need to preserve multimodal alignment. Continually unlearning from one modality could degrade the entire model. To alleviate this challenge, we propose LUMoE, an effective method. Experiments demonstrate that LUMoE significantly mitigates the degradation problem faced by baselines. The source code and the MLUBench dataset are open-sourced in https://github.com/lihe-maxsize/Lifelong_Unlearning_main.

AI.tldr: A large-scale benchmark MLUBench is introduced for multimodal LLM lifelong unlearning, revealing severe degradation in existing methods. The unique challenge is preserving multimodal alignment during sequential unlearning, addressed by proposed method LUMoE which significantly mitigates degradation.
AI.motivation: As MLLMs are trained on massive multimodal data and data owners increasingly request content removal, there's a growing need for effective unlearning methods. These requests often arrive sequentially over time, creating the challenging problem of MLLM Lifelong Unlearning. Existing benchmarks are limited in scale and scope, failing to capture these complexities.
AI.method: The authors introduce MLUBench, a large-scale comprehensive benchmark featuring 127 entities across 9 classes under lifelong unlearning requests. They conduct extensive experiments using MLUBench and identify the unique challenge of preserving multimodal alignment during continual unlearning. They propose LUMoE as an effective method to address this challenge.
AI.result: Experiments reveal existing unlearning methods suffer from severe, cumulative degradation. The unique challenge identified is that MLLM lifelong unlearning requires preserving multimodal alignment, and continually unlearning from one modality can degrade the entire model. LUMoE significantly mitigates the degradation problem faced by baselines.
AI.conclusion: MLLM lifelong unlearning presents unique challenges due to multimodal alignment constraints. The proposed MLUBench benchmark reveals limitations of existing methods, while LUMoE offers an effective solution to mitigate degradation. The work advances the field by addressing the practical sequential nature of unlearning requests in real-world scenarios.

title: The Mathematics of AI Winters: The mathematical Taxonomy of Paradigm Fragility in AI Winter
summary: Two major periods of reduced funding and confidence in artificial intelligence research, commonly called the first and second AI winters, are usually explained through engineering failure, commercial disappointment, and inflated expectations. This article develops a complementary thesis: that the dominant paradigms of those periods also met genuine formal barriers, including limitations of representation, optimisation, computational complexity, statistical learnability, and high-dimensional approximation. The contribution is synthetic rather than archival. We do not claim that particular theorems mechanically caused the winters; rather, we show that several central disappointments of early AI were aligned with mathematically precise bottlenecks. We analyse these bottlenecks through the perceptron impossibility results of Minsky and Papert, the complexity-theoretic hardness of exact neural-network training established by Blum and Rivest, minimax rates for nonparametric estimation in high dimension due to Stone, vanishing-gradient analyses by Hochreiter and by Bengio and collaborators, and classical statistical learning theory in the tradition of Vapnik and Chervonenkis, Valiant, and Blumer and collaborators. We then relate these barriers to the later breakthroughs that mitigated, rather than eliminated, them.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that AI winters (periods of reduced funding/confidence) were caused not just by engineering failures and hype cycles, but also by genuine mathematical limitations of early AI paradigms that met formal theoretical barriers.
AI.motivation: To develop a complementary explanation for AI winters beyond the usual narratives of engineering failure, commercial disappointment, and inflated expectations. The authors want to show that early AI faced mathematically precise bottlenecks that contributed to these periods of reduced confidence and funding.
AI.method: Synthetic analysis of mathematical barriers in early AI, examining five key theoretical limitations: (1) perceptron impossibility results, (2) computational complexity of exact neural network training, (3) minimax rates for nonparametric estimation in high dimensions, (4) vanishing gradient problems, and (5) classical statistical learning theory limitations. The approach is synthetic rather than archival.
AI.result: The authors demonstrate that several central disappointments of early AI were aligned with mathematically precise bottlenecks. They don't claim theorems mechanically caused the winters, but show these theoretical barriers were significant contributing factors that later breakthroughs only mitigated rather than eliminated.
AI.conclusion: AI winters were influenced by genuine mathematical limitations of representation, optimization, computational complexity, statistical learnability, and high-dimensional approximation. Later breakthroughs addressed these barriers to some extent, but they represent fundamental constraints that continue to shape AI research directions and limitations.

title: Teach-and-Repeat: Accurately Extracting Operational Knowledge from Mobile Screen Demonstrations to Empower GUI Agents
comment: 20 pages, 9 figures. Yudong Zhang and Lei Hu contributed equally to this work. Xingyu Liu, Zuojian Wang, and Zhilin Gao are corresponding authors
summary: Understanding the digital world on mobile devices is shifting from static UI perception to dynamic action comprehension. This capability enables models to convert visual state transitions into operational knowledge, defined as short natural-language sentences that describe action types, target UI elements, textual arguments, and execution orders. However, due to the highly diverse and heterogeneous UI designs across applications, existing vision-language models (VLMs) struggle to accurately infer these underlying operations. To bridge this gap, we introduce Teach VLM, a core model designed to translate mobile screen trajectories into step-wise operational knowledge by extracting and analyzing operation-related keyframes from demonstration videos. To address the scarcity of aligned training data, we develop a systematic data flywheel for scalable data acquisition. We further introduce a novel Chinese Mobile Screen Teach Benchmark for fine-grained evaluation. Building upon Teach VLM, we propose the Teach-and-Repeat paradigm, where the generated operational knowledge serves as an interpretable procedural reference to guide downstream screen-based execution agents. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that Teach VLM significantly outperforms strong VLM baselines, achieving state-of-the-art performance in operation semantics prediction. Furthermore, experiments in Android World show that our paradigm yields consistent Task Success Rate improvements for downstream agents. Together, Teach VLM and the Teach-and-Repeat paradigm offer a practical pathway from raw demonstrations to reusable task automation.

AI.tldr: Teach VLM translates mobile screen demonstration videos into step-by-step operational knowledge, enabling interpretable task automation through a Teach-and-Repeat paradigm.
AI.motivation: There's a shift from static UI perception to dynamic action comprehension for mobile automation, but existing vision-language models struggle with diverse UI designs and accurately inferring underlying operations.
AI.method: Introduces Teach VLM that extracts operation-related keyframes from demonstration videos to translate screen trajectories into operational knowledge. Develops a systematic data flywheel for scalable training data acquisition and creates a Chinese Mobile Screen Teach Benchmark for evaluation.
AI.result: Teach VLM significantly outperforms strong VLM baselines, achieving state-of-the-art performance in operation semantics prediction. The Teach-and-Repeat paradigm yields consistent Task Success Rate improvements for downstream agents in Android World experiments.
AI.conclusion: Teach VLM and the Teach-and-Repeat paradigm provide a practical pathway from raw demonstrations to reusable task automation by generating interpretable procedural references that guide execution agents.

title: Evaluation of AutoML Frameworks for IDS under Imbalanced Data Conditions of the NSL-KDD Dataset
summary: This work investigates the impact of severe class imbalance on the performance of automated machine learning (AutoML) frameworks for multiclass network intrusion detection using the NSL-KDD dataset. Unlike previous studies that simplify the problem through binary classification or minority-class removal, we preserve the original five-class distribution, including highly underrepresented attacks such as R2L and U2R, enabling a realistic evaluation of imbalance-sensitive learning behavior. Nine open-source AutoML frameworks were analyzed under a unified and reproducible experimental protocol, considering differences in architectural design, ensemble strategies, validation procedures, hyperparameter optimization, and imbalance-handling mechanisms. The results demonstrate that frameworks incorporating ensemble learning and imbalance-aware optimization achieve better minority-class discrimination. PyCaret obtained the best overall performance, reaching 66\% macro-F1, followed by AutoGluon with 55\%, whereas frameworks lacking native balancing support exhibited significant degradation in minority-class detection capability. The analysis further shows that accuracy-oriented optimization alone is insufficient for highly imbalanced IDS scenarios, since high-weighted metrics may coexist with poor generalization on rare attack categories. As a contribution, this work establishes a standardized benchmark for AutoML-based intrusion detection under severe multiclass imbalance, highlighting current architectural limitations and the need for native integration of imbalance-aware optimization, resampling, and stratified evaluation strategies into automated learning pipelines. The source code is publicly available.

AI.tldr: AutoML frameworks struggle with severe class imbalance in network intrusion detection, with PyCaret performing best (66% macro-F1) due to ensemble learning and imbalance-aware optimization.
AI.motivation: Previous studies simplify multiclass intrusion detection by converting to binary classification or removing minority classes, which doesn't reflect real-world severe class imbalance scenarios. There's a need for realistic evaluation of AutoML frameworks under authentic five-class distribution including rare attacks like R2L and U2R.
AI.method: Analyzed nine open-source AutoML frameworks using NSL-KDD dataset with original five-class distribution. Implemented unified reproducible experimental protocol examining architectural design, ensemble strategies, validation procedures, hyperparameter optimization, and imbalance-handling mechanisms.
AI.result: PyCaret achieved best overall performance with 66% macro-F1, followed by AutoGluon with 55%. Frameworks with ensemble learning and imbalance-aware optimization showed better minority-class discrimination. Frameworks lacking native balancing support exhibited significant degradation in detecting rare attack categories.
AI.conclusion: Accuracy-oriented optimization alone is insufficient for highly imbalanced intrusion detection systems. The work establishes a standardized benchmark highlighting the need for native integration of imbalance-aware optimization, resampling, and stratified evaluation strategies into AutoML pipelines.

title: GeoNatureAgent Benchmark: Benchmarking LLM Agents for Environmental Geospatial Analysis Across Frontier and Open-Weight Foundation Models
comment: Preprint. 10 pages, 8 figures. Submitted to ACM SIGSPATIAL 2026
summary: Environmental scientists spend disproportionate effort on data wrangling rather than analysis, and AI agents that automate geospatial workflows remain unvalidated: no benchmark evaluates agents operating through structured tool calling against real APIs. We introduce the GeoNatureAgent Benchmark, the first benchmark for environmental analysis agents that operate via structured tool calls to a production-style geospatial API. It comprises 93 tasks across 18 categories, covering municipality analysis, multi-turn conversation, spatial reasoning, cross-indicator synthesis, error handling and recovery, ranking, comparison, multilingual understanding, habitat analysis, and task rejection. Tasks are evaluated against an open, self-hostable API serving three environmental indicators across Spain and Portugal via sixteen tools. We evaluate seven LLMs (Claude Sonnet 4, DeepSeek V3.2, GLM-5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Qwen3-235B, GPT-OSS-120B, Llama 4 Scout) under three temperature-1.0 seeds, reporting capability and per-case cost as orthogonal axes. We find: (1) Claude Sonnet 4 leads at 60.8% +/- 0.8%, followed by DeepSeek V3.2 at 56.3% +/- 3.1%, with no other model above 51%; (2) the cost-accuracy Pareto frontier is occupied mostly by open-weight models, with DeepSeek V3.2 offering 93% of Claude's capability at 11x lower cost ($0.011/case); (3) comparison tasks remain universally unsolved (0% on close-value comparisons), exposing systematic reasoning limits; and (4) structured tool calling against a real API is more discriminative than general-purpose GIS benchmarks, with accuracies 25-35 points lower. We further show extensibility by integrating BigEarthNet V2 land cover for Portugal alongside Spanish CO2 and erosion indicators. The benchmark, harness, and self-hostable API are publicly available.

AI.tldr: GeoNatureAgent Benchmark is the first benchmark for environmental analysis agents using structured tool calls to real geospatial APIs, evaluating 7 LLMs across 93 diverse environmental tasks and revealing significant performance gaps while identifying cost-effective open-weight alternatives.
AI.motivation: Environmental scientists waste too much time on data wrangling instead of analysis, and existing AI agents for geospatial workflows lack proper validation with real API benchmarks. There's no benchmark that evaluates agents using structured tool calls against production-style geospatial APIs.
AI.method: Created GeoNatureAgent Benchmark with 93 tasks across 18 categories covering diverse environmental analysis scenarios. Implemented open, self-hostable API serving three environmental indicators across Spain/Portugal via 16 tools. Evaluated 7 LLMs under three temperature-1.0 seeds, measuring both capability and cost-effectiveness.
AI.result: Claude Sonnet 4 leads with 60.8% accuracy, DeepSeek V3.2 follows at 56.3%. Cost-accuracy Pareto frontier dominated by open-weight models, with DeepSeek offering 93% of Claude's capability at 11x lower cost. Comparison tasks universally unsolved (0% on close-value comparisons). Structured tool calling benchmark is 25-35 points more discriminative than general-purpose GIS benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark successfully addresses the validation gap for environmental AI agents, demonstrates the feasibility of cost-effective open-weight models, identifies systematic reasoning limitations in current LLMs, and provides a extensible framework for future environmental analysis agent development.

title: Towards Provably Fair Machine Learning: Bayesian Approaches For Consistent and Transparent Predictions
summary: ML classifiers deployed in high-stakes domains produce predictions whose quality varies systematically across subgroups. For granular subgroups defined by intersections of multiple features, predictions are often inconsistent with the observed data: the model's outputs contradict the evidence available for that subgroup. This problem is exacerbated by regularisation, which improves aggregate performance by collapsing small subgroups into larger groups, disproportionately affecting demographic minorities. We define two requirements for consistent prediction: determinism (identical individuals receive identical predictions) and statistical consistency (we cannot reject, at significance level alpha, the hypothesis that the predictions for a subgroup were drawn from the Bayesian optimal target distribution inferred for that subgroup). From these requirements we derive the Fair Bayesian classifier, which enforces both across every group and subgroup simultaneously and abstains whenever no consistent deterministic prediction is possible. On three benchmark datasets (Adult, COMPAS, and Bank Marketing), standard classifiers produce statistically inconsistent predictions for a substantial proportion of subgroups. Our classifier achieves zero consistency error by construction while exceeding baseline accuracy and multicalibration on every dataset tested. Statistical consistency provides a principled foundation for prediction quality with direct implications for algorithmic fairness. Minority demographics are disproportionately concentrated in small subgroups, precisely where frequentist inference is least reliable; addressing this inference problem is therefore a necessary step toward fair ML. By enforcing Bayesian consistency at the finest resolution the data supports, the our classifier demonstrates that exhaustive subgroup fairness with principled abstention is achievable in practice.

AI.tldr: Fair Bayesian classifier enforces deterministic and statistical consistency across all subgroups simultaneously, achieving zero consistency error while maintaining competitive accuracy by abstaining when no consistent prediction exists.
AI.motivation: ML classifiers in high-stakes domains produce predictions with systematically varying quality across subgroups, particularly affecting granular intersectional groups. Regularization exacerbates this by collapsing small subgroups, disproportionately harming demographic minorities. Current approaches fail to ensure that predictions are consistent with observed data for every subgroup.
AI.method: The authors define two requirements for consistent prediction: determinism (identical individuals get identical predictions) and statistical consistency (predictions can't be rejected as coming from Bayesian optimal target distribution for each subgroup). From these, they derive the Fair Bayesian classifier that enforces both requirements across all groups and subgroups simultaneously, with abstention when no consistent deterministic prediction is possible.
AI.result: On three benchmark datasets (Adult, COMPAS, and Bank Marketing), standard classifiers produce statistically inconsistent predictions for substantial proportions of subgroups. The Fair Bayesian classifier achieves zero consistency error by construction while exceeding baseline accuracy and achieving multicalibration on every tested dataset.
AI.conclusion: Statistical consistency provides a principled foundation for prediction quality with direct implications for algorithmic fairness. By enforcing Bayesian consistency at the finest data-supported resolution, the classifier demonstrates that exhaustive subgroup fairness with principled abstention is practically achievable. This addresses the fundamental inference problem that disproportionately affects minority demographics concentrated in small subgroups.

title: Topical Phase Transitions in Artificial Intelligence Research: Large-Scale Evidence and an Early-Warning Signature for Emerging Topics
summary: Do research topics in artificial intelligence grow gradually, or do they advance through abrupt, detectable jumps? Analyzing 80,814 accepted main-track papers from five premier AI conferences (ACL, CVPR, ICLR, ICML, NeurIPS) spanning 2017 to 2025, we show major AI topics advance through topical phase transitions: remaining marginal for years, then surging across venues within one to three years. Large language models became the dominant cross-venue topic by 2025, diffusion models rose with comparable abruptness, and language-model methods crossed into computer vision via vision-language models, whereas reinforcement learning compounded smoothly, distinguishing genuine phase transitions from ordinary growth. This structure is our primary contribution: a large-scale, cross-venue characterization of how AI research reorganizes. We then ask whether a transition leaves a detectable footprint before it peaks. We define an early-warning signature, four publication-dynamics criteria frozen on 2017-2021 data, and evaluate it out of sample on 2023-2025 transitions, obtaining a precision of 27% and recall of 63% against a 13.5% base rate. Applied to 2025 data, the signature flags reasoning and test-time compute, agentic AI, multimodal LLMs, retrieval-augmented generation, and world models as topics to monitor over 2026-2028. The source code is also publicly available on GitHub at https://github.com/KurbanIntelligenceLab/ai-phase-transitions.

AI.tldr: AI research topics don't grow gradually but advance through sudden "topical phase transitions" - remaining marginal for years then surging across conferences within 1-3 years, with measurable early-warning signatures.
AI.motivation: To understand whether AI research topics evolve gradually or through abrupt, detectable jumps, and to characterize how the field reorganizes itself over time through large-scale analysis.
AI.method: Analyzed 80,814 accepted main-track papers from five premier AI conferences (ACL, CVPR, ICLR, ICML, NeurIPS) from 2017-2025, identifying topical phase transitions and developing an early-warning signature with four publication-dynamics criteria.
AI.result: Major AI topics advance through abrupt phase transitions: LLMs became dominant by 2025, diffusion models rose similarly, language-model methods crossed into CV via VLMs, while RL grew smoothly. Early-warning signature achieved 27% precision and 63% recall on 2023-2025 transitions. Applied to 2025 data, it flags reasoning/test-time compute, agentic AI, multimodal LLMs, RAG, and world models as topics to monitor.
AI.conclusion: AI research reorganizes through detectable phase transitions rather than gradual evolution, with measurable early-warning signatures that can identify emerging topics years before they peak, providing a framework for tracking field evolution.

title: Bag of Dims: Training-Free Mechanistic Interpretability via Dimension-Level Sign Patterns
summary: We show that the standard basis of transformer hidden states already provides a training-free, architecture-general feature basis. Individual dimensions encode semantic content via their signs and confidence via their magnitudes, functioning as independent binary registers. We validate this Bag of Dims framework across three model families (Qwen 3.5-4B, Gemma 3-4B, Mistral 7B) through four progressive experiments. Sign patterns alone carry predictive content: replacing all magnitudes with unity achieves 72-93% top-5 next-token accuracy through the LM head, and pure Hamming scoring without any decoder reaches 80-90% top-4096. These sign patterns organize into semantic features: using a single-token type cache (one forward pass per vocabulary token, no context), we discover 175 categories via per-dimension sign consistency (mean AUC 0.80) from 50 anchors with zero training. A trained probe adds only +0.018 AUC and converges to axis-aligned weights, confirming negligible cross-dimension structure. This structure extends to attention: all 175 categories remain discoverable in K and V projections. On the write side, static FFN weight inspection links 20% of features to individual writer neurons (>0.70 agreement; random controls: 0%), with top-200 neuron coalitions achieving >0.70 agreement on 99.9% of prototypes via majority vote. Fully unsupervised discovery (random seeds, no labels) scales to 1500 features at 100% yield and 99% sparsity across all three models, with pairwise MI of 0.0014 bits confirming low inter-dimension coupling. These results establish that the standard basis already suffices for feature reading throughout the transformer compute pathway, requiring no training, no optimization, and no GPU-days beyond a single forward pass per vocabulary token.

AI.tldr: Transformer hidden states naturally encode semantic features in their standard basis dimensions, with signs representing content and magnitudes representing confidence, forming a 'Bag of Dims' feature basis requiring no training.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to demonstrate that the standard basis of transformer hidden states already provides a sufficient feature basis for semantic understanding, challenging the assumption that complex transformations or specialized training are needed to extract meaningful features from these representations.
AI.method: Uses four progressive experiments across three model families (Qwen 3.5-4B, Gemma 3-4B, Mistral 7B): 1) analyzing predictive content in sign patterns alone, 2) discovering semantic categories via single-token type cache with per-dimension sign consistency, 3) examining attention projections (K and V), and 4) linking features to FFN writer neurons.
AI.result: Sign patterns alone achieve 72-93% top-5 next-token accuracy, pure Hamming scoring reaches 80-90% top-4096 accuracy, discovered 175 semantic categories with mean AUC 0.80, extended findings to attention projections, linked features to individual writer neurons (>0.70 agreement), and scaled unsupervised discovery to 1500 features with 99% sparsity and low inter-dimension coupling (0.0014 bits MI).
AI.conclusion: The standard basis of transformer hidden states provides a training-free, architecture-general feature basis where dimensions function as independent binary registers, with signs encoding semantic content and magnitudes encoding confidence. This suggests transformer features are already well-structured without additional optimization.

title: Fantastic Scientific Agents and How to Build Them: AgentBuild for Rietveld Refinement
summary: As scientific workflows shift from deterministic executables to LLM-based agents, the development practices on offer, such as fine-tuning, reinforcement learning, and prompt-and-go, bury the scientist's judgment. We propose treating agent construction as a workflow stage and introduce AgentBuild, which builds a scientific agent from a contract the scientist authors. The contract is a version-controlled rubric, a difficulty-graded curriculum, and a curated external knowledge base. A rubric-driven judge gates a meta-optimizer coding agent that edits the agent within a declared boundary, so the build compiles the agent, not the scientist's judgment. We instantiate this for Rietveld refinement of X-ray diffraction data through GSAS-II behind MCP and A2A, where a blank-harness construction run progresses through a lithium lanthanum zirconium oxide (LLZO) signal-to-noise ladder, reaches the 4 hour scan as a frontier case, and exposes the workflow-scope limits that remain. The same rubric that rewards credible fits also scores trajectory scope, making the frontier a contract failure rather than a pattern-fitting failure. As base models evolve, re-running AgentBuild is a re-tune, not a rebuild, and the scientist's authored contract remains the durable asset.

AI.tldr: AgentBuild is a system that builds scientific agents from contracts authored by scientists, using a rubric-driven approach to preserve scientific judgment while automating agent construction, demonstrated on Rietveld refinement of XRD data.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-based scientific agent development practices (fine-tuning, reinforcement learning, prompt-and-go) bury the scientist's judgment, creating a need for approaches that preserve scientific expertise while automating agent construction.
AI.method: Treat agent construction as a workflow stage where scientists author contracts containing version-controlled rubrics, difficulty-graded curricula, and curated knowledge bases. A rubric-driven judge gates a meta-optimizer coding agent that edits the agent within declared boundaries.
AI.result: Successfully instantiated for Rietveld refinement of X-ray diffraction data through GSAS-II, progressing through lithium lanthanum zirconium oxide signal-to-noise ladder and reaching 4-hour scan frontier cases, exposing workflow-scope limits while maintaining scientific judgment.
AI.conclusion: AgentBuild enables durable scientific contracts that persist across model evolution (re-tuning rather than rebuilding), making the scientist's authored contract the durable asset and ensuring frontier challenges appear as contract failures rather than pattern-fitting failures.

authors: Juan Pablo De la Cruz Weinstein
title: Keep Policy Gradient in Charge: Sibling-Guided Credit Distillation for Long-Horizon Tool-Use Agents
comment: 13 pages, 4 figures, 7 tables. Submitted to EMNLP 2026 Industry Track
summary:
Long-horizon tool-use reinforcement learning can learn from outcome verification, but its
  trajectory-level advantage is broadcast across many reasoning, API, and answer tokens.
  Self-distillation promises a denser signal by reusing a policy's own rollouts or a privileged
  teacher. We show, however, that direct token-level self-distillation can silently destroy tool use:
  it rehearses teacher behavior without knowing which actions the verifier rewards, so useful skills
  and harmful shortcuts are amplified together. We introduce Sibling-Guided Credit Distillation
  (SGCD), which uses distillation for credit assignment rather than as a competing actor loss.
  Dynamic sampling produces mixed successful and failed sibling rollouts; an external LLM summarizes
  their contrast into a training-only stepwise credit reference; dense teacher/student divergence
  drives credit reassignment; and bounded detached credit weights reshape GRPO token advantages. The
  deployed student sees no external LLM, sibling evidence, or oracle. Across AppWorld and
  $τ^3$-airline, SGCD improves over matched GRPO comparators: AppWorld TGC $42.9 \to 45.6$ on
  test_normal and $24.7 \to 27.0$ on test_challenge, and $τ^3$-airline pass@1 $0.583 \to 0.602$.

AI.tldr: SGCD (Sibling-Guided Credit Distillation) is a new RL method that uses sibling rollouts and contrastive LLM summaries for better credit assignment in long-horizon tool-use tasks, preventing harmful shortcuts in token-level distillation.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon tool-use RL suffers from sparse trajectory-level reward signals. Direct token-level self-distillation can destroy tool use by amplifying both useful skills and harmful shortcuts together since it doesn't know which actions the verifier actually rewards.
AI.method: SGCD uses distillation for credit assignment rather than as a competing actor loss. It involves: 1) Dynamic sampling of mixed successful/failed sibling rollouts, 2) External LLM summarizing their contrast into stepwise credit reference, 3) Dense teacher/student divergence driving credit reassignment, 4) Bounded detached credit weights reshaping GRPO token advantages.
AI.result: SGCD improves over matched GRPO comparators: AppWorld TGC improves from 42.9 to 45.6 on test_normal and from 24.7 to 27.0 on test_challenge; τ³-airline pass@1 improves from 0.583 to 0.602.
AI.conclusion: SGCD successfully addresses the credit assignment problem in long-horizon tool-use RL by using contrastive sibling rollouts and LLM-generated credit references, enabling effective token-level training without requiring external resources during deployment.

title: (Human) Attention Is (Still) All You Need: Human oversight makes AI-assisted social science reliable
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used for tasks once reserved for trained researchers, including hypothesis generation, specification choice, and drafting conclusions. We argue that the reliability of AI-assisted research depends not only on model capability, but also on how cognitive labour is structured between humans and machines. We study this problem through Human-in-the-Loop Economic Research (HLER), a decision architecture based on pre-commitment, decision sequencing, accountability, and attention allocation. In a pre-specified 2*4 factorial experiment with 280 complete research runs across four datasets, an unconstrained multi-agent baseline produced critical failures in 72% of runs. Using the same underlying model, the same agent decomposition, and identical prompts for the shared reasoning agents, HLER reduced the failure rate to 16% by imposing three architectural commitments: LLMs reason but do not execute data work, data and estimation are handled deterministically, and three human decision gates bind the workflow. Fisher's exact test rejects equality of failure rates at p<0.001. Reliability gains were largest on the least publicly represented dataset, a Qing-dynasty population register, consistent with a task-based production model with Frechet-distributed output quality. An 80-run ablation suggests that deterministic computation and human gates contribute independently, with exploratory evidence of complementarity. We interpret HLER as a research harness rather than an autonomous AI scientist: it sharply reduces failures, makes residual weaknesses more visible, and prevents unreliable claims from being advanced as publication-ready outputs.

AI.tldr: HLER (Human-in-the-Loop Economic Research) is a decision architecture that reduces critical failures in AI-assisted research from 72% to 16% by structuring cognitive labor between humans and machines through pre-commitment, sequencing, and accountability mechanisms.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used for research tasks like hypothesis generation and conclusion drafting, the reliability of AI-assisted research depends not only on model capability but on how cognitive labor is structured between humans and machines. Current unconstrained approaches lead to high failure rates.
AI.method: The paper introduces HLER with three key architectural commitments: 1) LLMs reason but don't execute data work, 2) data and estimation are handled deterministically, and 3) three human decision gates bind the workflow. Tested in a 2*4 factorial experiment with 280 complete research runs across four datasets, comparing HLER against an unconstrained multi-agent baseline.
AI.result: Unconstrained baseline produced critical failures in 72% of runs, while HLER reduced failures to 16% using the same underlying model. Fisher's exact test shows p<0.001 for equality of failure rates. Largest reliability gains on least publicly represented dataset (Qing-dynasty population register). Ablation study suggests deterministic computation and human gates contribute independently with possible complementarity.
AI.conclusion: HLER serves as a research harness rather than autonomous AI scientist, sharply reducing failures, making residual weaknesses more visible, and preventing unreliable claims from being advanced as publication-ready outputs. The structure of cognitive labor is crucial for reliable AI-assisted research.

title: The Metric Picks the Winner: Evaluation Choice Flips Model Rankings for Drug-Response Prediction in Unseen Chemistry
summary:
Predicting how a cell's transcriptome responds to a drug it has never seen is a core, hard problem in computational cell biology: recent benchmarks show complex models often fail to beat trivial baselines once test compounds are held out by chemistry. We study one cell line and assay, THP-1 cells profiled by DRUG-seq, scored by the active-compound weighted MSE(wMSE) of the VCPI prediction contest. We propose a staged approach: dumb baselines (untreated control and mean training-compound response) that the field keeps failing to beat; non-parametric retrieval (a Tanimoto-weighted average of a held-out compound's nearest training compounds); and a fusion stage combining a frozen chemistry embedding with retrieval-support features to predict the residual over the mean, with an uncertainty head and gene programs. On the released VCPI THP-1 drug-seq data (14,026 training compounds), under a Bemis-Murcko scaffold split, the model ranking inverts depending on the metric. Under an inverse-variance per-gene proxy, a regularized linear regression on Morgan fingerprints appears to win over the deep models, retrieval, and ChemBERTa -- the textbook "simple baselines win" result. But under the contest's true active-set metric (per-(gene, compound) Mejia weights, validated against the official scorer; mean baseline 0.535 vs the organizers' 0.507 reference), that reverses: the deep models win, our fusion decoder significantly beats the linear fingerprint baseline (-0.012 wMSE, paired bootstrap p < 10^-4), and the proxy's winner becomes the worst chemistry-aware predictor. Picking the metric picks the winner -- to our knowledge the first demonstration on real held-out drug chemistry of the metric-calibration effect established largely on genetic perturbation. We release a reproducible pipeline
  wired to the official scorer that emits a valid submission over the real 1064 x 12,995 grid.

AI.tldr: In drug response prediction, winner ranking flips depending on evaluation metric: linear fingerprint models win under standard metrics, but deep learning models win under contest's active-compound weighted MSE metric on held-out chemistry.
AI.motivation: Predicting transcriptomic response to unseen drugs is a hard problem where complex models often fail to beat simple baselines when test compounds are held out by chemistry. The field struggles with metric selection that properly evaluates performance.
AI.method: Proposed staged approach: dumb baselines (untreated control, mean training response), non-parametric retrieval (Tanimoto-weighted average of nearest training compounds), and fusion stage combining frozen chemistry embedding with retrieval-support features to predict residual over mean, with uncertainty head and gene programs. Evaluated on VCPI THP-1 drug-seq data under Bemis-Murcko scaffold split.
AI.result: Model ranking inverts depending on metric. Under inverse-variance per-gene proxy, regularized linear regression on Morgan fingerprints wins. But under contest's true active-set metric (per-(gene, compound) Mejia weights), deep models win, fusion decoder significantly beats linear fingerprint baseline (-0.012 wMSE, p < 10^-4). Metric selection determines winner.
AI.conclusion: Metric calibration crucially determines which model appears best - first demonstration of this effect on real held-out drug chemistry. Simple baselines don't always win; proper metric selection reveals deep learning advantages for predicting transcriptomic responses to novel compounds.

title: WISE: A Long-Horizon Agent in Minecraft with Why-Which Reasoning
summary: Rapid advances have been made in developing general-purpose embodied agent in environments like Minecraft through the adoption of LLM-augmented hierarchical approaches. Despite their promise, low-level controllers often become performance bottlenecks due to repeated execution failures. We argue that a key limitation is not only the lack of episodic memory, but also the decoupling of \textit{what-where-when} memory from \textit{which-why} reasoning. To address this, we propose \textbf{WISE} (Which-Why Informed Semantic Explorer), a long-horizon agent framework with an enhanced low-level controller equipped with a Causal Event Graph that augments episodic memory with explicit causal structure linking observations to task relevance. Unlike prior work such as MrSteve, which relies on feature similarity for retrieval, WISE enables robust recall under viewpoint changes and supports opportunistic task reordering through causal reasoning. Building on this memory, we propose an Opportunistic Task Scheduler that dynamically re-prioritizes subtasks when causally relevant opportunities are detected. We further equip WISE with a multi-scale progressive exploration strategy to provide spatially comprehensive observations for downstream reasoning. Experiments show that WISE largely improves task success and efficiency on long-horizon sparse tasks, particularly in settings requiring adaptive decision-making.

AI.tldr: WISE is a hierarchical embodied agent framework with causal event graphs and opportunistic task scheduling that improves long-horizon task performance in Minecraft by addressing low-level controller failures through enhanced memory and reasoning integration.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-augmented hierarchical agents in environments like Minecraft suffer from performance bottlenecks due to repeated execution failures at the low-level controller. The key limitation is not just lack of episodic memory, but the decoupling of "what-where-when" memory from "which-why" reasoning, preventing robust recall under viewpoint changes and adaptive decision-making.
AI.method: Proposes WISE framework with: 1) Causal Event Graph that augments episodic memory with explicit causal structure linking observations to task relevance, 2) Opportunistic Task Scheduler that dynamically re-prioritizes subtasks when causally relevant opportunities are detected, 3) Multi-scale progressive exploration strategy for spatially comprehensive observations.
AI.result: Experiments show WISE significantly improves task success and efficiency on long-horizon sparse tasks, particularly in settings requiring adaptive decision-making. It demonstrates robust recall under viewpoint changes and supports opportunistic task reordering through causal reasoning.
AI.conclusion: The integration of causal reasoning with episodic memory through Causal Event Graphs and opportunistic scheduling mechanisms effectively addresses the limitations of current hierarchical embodied agents, enabling better long-horizon task performance in complex environments like Minecraft.

title: Individual Control Barrier Functions-Guided Diffusion Model for Safe Offline Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
comment: Accepted to the 23rd IFAC World Congress, 2026
summary: Offline reinforcement learning allows control policies to be learned directly from data without online interaction, making it suitable for safety-critical tasks. Recent studies have applied diffusion models to offline reinforcement learning to leverage their strong capacity for modeling complex data distributions. However, existing approaches primarily focus on single-agent settings, leaving the safety challenges in multi-agent environments largely unexplored. In this work, we propose a safe offline multi-agent reinforcement learning algorithm that embeds neural individual control barrier functions into the diffusion model to enhance safety during trajectory generation, with control policies recovered through inverse dynamics. We evaluate our algorithm across diverse benchmarks, demonstrating substantial safety improvements while maintaining competitive rewards.

AI.tldr: Proposes safe offline multi-agent RL algorithm using diffusion models with neural control barrier functions for trajectory safety.
AI.motivation: Existing offline RL approaches using diffusion models focus on single-agent settings, leaving multi-agent safety challenges unexplored despite offline RL's suitability for safety-critical tasks.
AI.method: Embeds neural individual control barrier functions into diffusion models to enhance safety during trajectory generation, with control policies recovered through inverse dynamics.
AI.result: Demonstrates substantial safety improvements while maintaining competitive rewards across diverse benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach successfully addresses multi-agent safety challenges in offline RL by integrating neural control barrier functions with diffusion models, achieving both safety and performance.

title: DailyReport: An Open-ended Benchmark for Evaluating Search Agents on Daily Search Tasks
summary: Search Agents (SAs) typically leverage large language models (LLMs) to support complex information-seeking tasks by autonomously exploring web sources and synthesizing information into comprehensive responses. For SAs evaluation, prior benchmarks mainly focus on specialized tasks that are unlikely to arise in real-world user scenarios. Moreover, their reliance on coarse task-level rubrics often limits evaluation interpretability. To bridge this gap, we introduce DailyReport, an open-ended benchmark to evaluate SA capabilities on daily search tasks. It contains 150 open-ended tasks with 3,546 associated rubrics, capturing widely discussed and timely information demands of real-world users. Each task is decomposed into subtasks and evaluated with cascade rubrics across disentangled dimensions. Through cascade performance attribution and user-centric aggregation, we derive highly interpretable scores for each dimension, along with a user preference score. Our results on 17 agentic systems show that current systems still fall short of users' expectations. To facilitate future research, our dataset and code are made publicly available at https://github.com/AGI-Eval-Official/DailyReport.

AI.tldr: DailyReport is a benchmark for evaluating search agents on real-world daily search tasks with fine-grained rubrics for interpretable assessment, showing current systems still fall short of user expectations.
AI.motivation: Current search agent evaluation benchmarks focus on specialized tasks not reflective of real-world user scenarios and use coarse task-level rubrics that limit evaluation interpretability.
AI.method: Introduced DailyReport benchmark with 150 open-ended daily search tasks, each decomposed into subtasks with 3,546 associated cascade rubrics across disentangled dimensions, using cascade performance attribution and user-centric aggregation.
AI.result: Evaluation of 17 agentic systems shows current systems still fall short of users' expectations despite the comprehensive assessment framework.
AI.conclusion: DailyReport provides a realistic, interpretable benchmark for search agent evaluation that reveals current system limitations and is publicly available to facilitate future research.

title: TEDD: Robust Detection of Unstable Temporal Features
summary: When working with real-world temporal data, it is common to encounter features whose distribution is changing over time. The naive employment of Machine Learning models on this unstable data might lead to rapidly degrading performance, especially if the new distribution is much different from what was previously seen during training. In order to cope with this problem, it is critical to automatically identify features that are changing over time. With these features detected, data scientists and other practitioners will be able to mitigate the issue (for instance, by applying data transformations), deploying more robust models that retain high performance for longer periods of time. In this paper, we describe which temporal changes a feature should not suffer from, and propose TEDD, a technique to a) identify when a dataset might lead to an unstable Machine Learning model and b) automatically detect which features cause such lack of robustness. In order to achieve it, we leverage a regression model to highlight which features contribute to a good prediction of an instance's timestamp. We compare our approach to other methods in real and synthetic data, testing their detection capability on all simple change patterns. We show that our method: detects all types of basic changes, both for numerical and categorical features; can detect multivariate drifts; returns a comparable value measuring the amount of change of each feature; requires no parameter tuning; and is scalable both on number of features and instances of the dataset.

AI.tldr: TEDD is a parameter-free technique that detects feature distribution changes over time to help maintain ML model performance by identifying unstable features causing temporal drift.
AI.motivation: Real-world temporal data often has features with changing distributions over time, which causes ML model performance degradation when distributions differ from training data. Manual detection of such unstable features is challenging but critical for deploying robust models.
AI.method: Proposes TEDD technique that leverages regression models to predict instance timestamps, using feature importance analysis to identify which features contribute most to timestamp prediction, indicating distribution changes. The method requires no parameter tuning.
AI.result: TEDD detects all basic change patterns for numerical and categorical features, can identify multivariate drifts, provides comparable values measuring feature change amounts, works without parameter tuning, and scales well with dataset size and feature count.
AI.conclusion: TEDD effectively identifies temporal distribution changes in features, enabling data scientists to mitigate model degradation by focusing transformation efforts on unstable features, leading to more robust ML models that maintain performance over time.

title: HarnessBridge: Learnable Bidirectional Controller for LLM Agent Harness
summary: Large language models are increasingly deployed as agents for long-horizon tasks, yet their performance is shaped not only by model capability and environment design, but also by the harness that mediates agent--environment interaction. Existing harnesses are largely manually engineered, making them difficult to scale as trajectories grow longer and interactions become more complex. In this work, we ask whether harness can be generated by a learnable plug-in module that can be trained in an end-to-end fashion. We introduce HarnessBridge, a lightweight learnable harness controller that parameterizes the agent--environment interface as a bidirectional projection. HarnessBridge learns two bidirectional projections: observation projection, which distills raw trajectories into compact, decision-relevant states, and action projection, which converts proposed actions into executable transitions or trajectory-grounded rejections. We train HarnessBridge on a harness supervision dataset via unified instruction tuning. On Terminal-Bench~2.0 and SWE-bench Verified, HarnessBridge matches or surpasses strong specialized harnesses while substantially reducing token usage and trajectory length, and generalizes from smaller generators to larger commercial models.

AI.tldr: HarnessBridge is a learnable plug-in module that generates bidirectional projections for agent-environment interaction, reducing token usage and trajectory length while matching specialized harnesses performance.
AI.motivation: Existing harnesses for large language model agents are manually engineered, making them difficult to scale as trajectories grow longer and interactions become more complex. The paper questions whether harness can be generated by a learnable module trained end-to-end.
AI.method: Introduces HarnessBridge, a lightweight learnable harness controller that parameterizes agent-environment interface as bidirectional projection. It learns: 1) observation projection to distill raw trajectories into compact, decision-relevant states, and 2) action projection to convert proposed actions into executable transitions or trajectory-grounded rejections. Trained on harness supervision dataset via unified instruction tuning.
AI.result: On Terminal-Bench 2.0 and SWE-bench Verified, HarnessBridge matches or surpasses strong specialized harnesses while substantially reducing token usage and trajectory length. It also generalizes from smaller generators to larger commercial models.
AI.conclusion: HarnessBridge demonstrates that learnable harnesses can effectively mediate agent-environment interactions, offering improved scalability and efficiency compared to manually engineered approaches for long-horizon tasks with complex interactions.

title: Physics-Aware Auxiliary Losses Improve Out-of-Distribution Generalization of a GNN Synthesizability Filter
summary: Machine-learning drug-discovery pipelines increasingly rely on generative models that propose molecules far from the data used to train downstream synthesizability filters. Existing filters (SAScore, SCScore, RAscore, DeepSA) are purely statistical and degrade in exactly this out-of-distribution (OOD) regime. We ask whether cheap, closed-form physical priors, used as auxiliary supervision on a graph neural network (GNN), improve OOD generalization. We add two auxiliary losses to a GINE backbone: a topological complexity regression supervised by the Bertz index, and a strain-energy soft penalty supervised by MMFF94 force-field energy. On a 65,177-molecule corpus (HIV, Tox21, COCONUT) labeled by SAScore thresholds we reproduce a strong in-distribution baseline, then evaluate a 4-way ablation (baseline / +complexity / +strain / +both) on a single-source OOD split (train on drug-like HIV+Tox21, test on COCONUT natural products), repeated over 5 seeds with paired bootstrap confidence intervals. All three physics-aware variants give a small but statistically significant OOD improvement over the baseline (mean OOD AUC 0.9774): +complexity Delta = +0.0060 (95% CI [+0.0023, +0.0102]), +strain Delta = +0.0032 ([+0.0008, +0.0052]), +both Delta = +0.0066 ([+0.0038, +0.0093]); every interval excludes zero, and the combination is best. The variants are indistinguishable in-distribution, so the effect is visible only under OOD evaluation. We are explicit that the effects are modest, and we report a cautionary methodological finding: a single-seed version of this experiment produced a qualitatively different (non-monotone) story that did not survive multi-seed evaluation.

AI.tldr: Physics-informed GNN with Bertz index and MMFF94 auxiliary losses improves OOD generalization for molecular synthesizability prediction.
AI.motivation: Existing statistical synthesizability filters degrade in out-of-distribution (OOD) regimes when generative models propose molecules far from training data.
AI.method: Add two auxiliary losses to GINE backbone: topological complexity regression supervised by Bertz index, and strain-energy soft penalty supervised by MMFF94 force-field energy. Evaluate 4-way ablation on OOD split with 5-seed bootstrap confidence intervals.
AI.result: All physics-aware variants show small but statistically significant OOD improvement: +complexity Δ=+0.0060, +strain Δ=+0.0032, +both Δ=+0.0066 (best). Effects are visible only under OOD evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Cheap physical priors improve OOD generalization for molecular synthesizability prediction. Single-seed experiments can be misleading - multi-seed evaluation is crucial.

title: The Hidden Power of Scaling Factor in LoRA Optimization
summary: In Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA), the scaling factor $α$ is often treated as a mere complement to the learning rate, yet its role in optimization remains poorly understood. In this paper, we reveal that the scaling factor $α$ and the learning rate function differently, with $α$ emerging as the dominant driver of effective optimization, delivering gains that cannot be replicated by learning rate scaling alone. Through the synergy of extensive empirical analysis and a theoretical Signal-Drift framework, we uncover three findings into LoRA's scaling mechanism: First, LoRA's spectral suppression smooths the optimization landscape, rendering standard hyperparameters overly conservative and creating an optimization gap. Second, when leveraging this smoothness to accelerate convergence, $α$ outperforms the learning rate by amplifying the task signal without increasing the drift ratio. Third, the optimal scaling factor follows a sublinear relationship with the rank, well characterized by a square-root law with an unexpectedly large coefficient, revealing the insufficient scaling of existing rank-tied heuristics. Based on these insights, we propose LoRA-$α$, a minimalist framework that restores $α$ to its principled regime, making LoRA compatible with standard small learning rates. Extensive evaluations across diverse tasks demonstrate that LoRA-$α$ consistently improves performance while streamlining hyperparameter search, unleashing the learning potential of LoRA.

AI.tldr: LoRA scaling factor α, not learning rate, is the main optimization driver; paper reveals α's unique role, proposes LoRA-α framework for better performance with standard learning rates.
AI.motivation: Despite widespread use of LoRA, the scaling factor α has been misunderstood as just complementing learning rate, leaving its optimization role unclear and potentially limiting LoRA's effectiveness.
AI.method: Combines empirical analysis with theoretical Signal-Drift framework to study α's role, reveals three key findings about LoRA's scaling mechanism, then proposes LoRA-α framework to restore α to principled regime.
AI.result: α outperforms learning rate by amplifying task signal without increasing drift ratio; optimal α follows square-root relationship with rank; LoRA-α consistently improves performance across diverse tasks while simplifying hyperparameter search.
AI.conclusion: α is the dominant optimization driver in LoRA, not just complement to learning rate; proper understanding and application through LoRA-α framework unlocks LoRA's full learning potential with standard hyperparameters.

title: Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Chemotherapy Pharmacokinetics: Benchmarking the Clinical Estimator and Exposing Parameter Identifiability
summary:
Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) are an attractive tool for partial-observation problems in biology, where the governing dynamics are known but some compartments cannot be measured. Chemotherapy pharmacokinetics (PK) is a clean instance: drug concentration in plasma is routinely measured, but concentration in tissue -- which determines tumour kill and off-target toxicity -- is not. We benchmark a PINN against the standard clinical baseline (nonlinear least-squares on the analytical biexponential plasma solution, hereafter NLS) and a physics-agnostic neural baseline (a data-only MLP) on two PK problems. On the linear two-compartment problem, NLS is near-optimal; the PINN matches it to within a small constant factor while also producing the tissue curve in a single training pass, whereas the data-only MLP fails on tissue by roughly 10x. On a Michaelis-Menten extension (saturable elimination), the biexponential closed form no longer exists, so NLS is mis-specified and silently returns meaningless rate constants. The PINN instead exposes a deeper fact: the Michaelis-Menten two-compartment model is non-identifiable from plasma alone, and the PINN reports this honestly by converging to a basin with k12 -> 0. Adding two sparse tissue observations largely resolves identifiability: across five seeds the PINN recovers k21 to within 1% of truth and Vmax, Km to within one standard-deviation bar, while k12 moves in the correct direction (0.02 -> 0.82) but remains ~2 sigma below truth -- a recovery the closed-form NLS estimator cannot attempt at all, because its biexponential ansatz describes only plasma. Our claim is not that PINNs beat NLS. It is that PINNs offer a uniform recipe that ties the textbook estimator on the textbook problem, exposes structural identifiability that
  the textbook estimator hides, and absorbs heterogeneous measurements within a single loss.

AI.tldr: PINNs provide a unified approach for pharmacokinetic modeling that matches standard clinical methods on linear problems while exposing structural identifiability issues in nonlinear problems that traditional methods miss.
AI.motivation: Partial-observation problems in biology where governing dynamics are known but some compartments cannot be measured present a challenge for traditional methods. Specifically in chemotherapy pharmacokinetics, drug concentration in plasma is measurable but tissue concentration (which determines treatment effectiveness and toxicity) is not, creating a need for more robust inference methods.
AI.method: Uses Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) benchmarked against two baselines: 1) clinical standard nonlinear least-squares on analytical biexponential plasma solution, and 2) physics-agnostic data-only MLP. Tested on linear two-compartment PK problem and Michaelis-Menten extension with saturable elimination.
AI.result: On linear problem: PINNs matched near-optimal NLS results while also producing tissue curve in single training pass. On Michaelis-Menten problem: PINNs exposed model non-identifiability from plasma alone (NLS silently gives meaningless results), and with sparse tissue observations recovered parameters accurately (k21 within 1%, Vmax/Km within 1σ, k12 direction correct but 2σ below truth).
AI.conclusion: PINNs offer a uniform framework that matches textbook estimators on simple problems, reveals structural identifiability issues hidden by traditional methods, and can incorporate heterogeneous measurements in a single loss function, making them particularly valuable for partial-observation biological systems.

title: Zero-source LLM Hallucination Detection with Human-like Criteria Probing
summary: Large language models (LLMs) often hallucinate by generating factually incorrect or unfaithful content, posing significant risks to their safe use. Detecting such hallucinations is particularly challenging under the zero-source constraint, where no model internals or external references are available, and detection must rely solely on the textual query-answer pair. In this paper, we propose Human-like Criteria Probing for Hallucination Detection (HCPD), a paradigm that emulates the multi-faceted reasoning of human evaluators. Its core is a Human-like Criteria Probing (HCP) mechanism, in which a LLM agent adaptively decomposes its judgment into a weighted set of interpretable criteria and aggregates criterion-specific scores into a final truthfulness measure. To achieve this adaptive capability, we introduce a reward-based alignment scheme using only weak supervision from semantic consistency. At inference, we employ a multi-sampling aggregation strategy to ensure robust decisions while preserving full interpretability. We further provide theoretical analysis supporting the reliability of our approach. Extensive experiments show that HCPD consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, offering an effective and explainable solution for zero-source hallucination detection. Code is available at https://github.com/TRISKEL10N/HCPD.

AI.tldr: HCPD is a zero-source hallucination detection method that mimics human evaluators by adaptively decomposing judgments into interpretable criteria and aggregating them, outperforming SOTA methods with explainable results.
AI.motivation: LLMs often hallucinate factually incorrect content, posing safety risks. Detecting hallucinations is challenging under zero-source constraints where no model internals or external references are available, requiring detection from textual query-answer pairs alone.
AI.method: Human-like Criteria Probing for Hallucination Detection (HCPD): uses a LLM agent to adaptively decompose judgments into weighted, interpretable criteria and aggregates criterion-specific scores. Features reward-based alignment with weak semantic consistency supervision and multi-sampling aggregation at inference.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show HCPD consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines. The method provides effective and explainable solutions for zero-source hallucination detection with theoretical analysis supporting its reliability.
AI.conclusion: HCPD offers an effective, explainable solution for zero-source hallucination detection by emulating human evaluator reasoning, addressing safety concerns in LLM deployment while maintaining interpretability.

title: A Zero-shot Generalized Graph Anomaly Detection Framework via Node Reconstruction
summary: Cross-domain graph anomaly detection (GAD) aims to identify abnormal nodes in unseen target graphs, showing strong potential in real-world applications with heterogeneous graph data. However, existing methods often depend on dataset-specific feature semantics and structural patterns, which limits their ability to generalize across different domains. To address this challenge, we propose AlignGAD, a zero-shot generalized graph anomaly detection framework. Our framework is built upon three key components: a Global Unification Module that aligns heterogeneous node features and normalizes graph signals in the spectral domain; a Clustering Module that constructs cluster-aware graph views to capture group-level abnormal patterns; and a Node Discrepancy Scoring Module that measures reconstruction discrepancy and aggregates anomaly evidence from different graph views. Experiments on multiple real-world datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of AlignGAD under the zero-shot GAD setting.

AI.tldr: AlignGAD is a zero-shot graph anomaly detection framework that works across different domains by aligning features, clustering nodes, and scoring discrepancies without requiring target domain training data.
AI.motivation: Existing graph anomaly detection methods struggle to generalize across different domains because they rely on dataset-specific feature semantics and structural patterns, limiting their practical application in real-world scenarios with heterogeneous graph data.
AI.method: Three key components: (1) Global Unification Module aligns heterogeneous node features and normalizes graph signals in spectral domain; (2) Clustering Module constructs cluster-aware graph views to capture group-level abnormal patterns; (3) Node Discrepancy Scoring Module measures reconstruction discrepancy and aggregates anomaly evidence from different graph views.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple real-world datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of AlignGAD under the zero-shot graph anomaly detection setting, showing it can generalize across domains without requiring target domain training.
AI.conclusion: AlignGAD provides a promising solution for cross-domain graph anomaly detection by addressing feature heterogeneity and domain shift through unified alignment, cluster-aware modeling, and discrepancy-based anomaly scoring, enabling zero-shot generalization to unseen target graphs.

title: MDForge: Agentic Molecular Dynamics Pipeline Design under Sparse Simulator Feedback
summary: Molecular dynamics (MD) is the canonical in-silico method for atomistic molecular science, simulating molecular behavior from first-principle physics. Designing an MD pipeline for a new system requires substantial expert knowledge: running it on even one molecule is expensive, ruling out trial-and-error. We automate this expert pipeline-design process with an LLM agent. Unlike existing MD agents that orchestrate a predefined tool set, we treat pipeline design as open-ended code generation in which the agent's behavior is reshaped online by verbal reward. Specifically, we build MDForge, an LLM agent whose in-context update rule densifies the sparse reward via a multi-agent debate among physics experts. On three SAMPL host-guest binding free-energy benchmarks, MDForge automatically designs MD pipelines competitive with human experts. Deployed on a library of unseen candidate guests, its CB[7] pipeline discovers a novel binder that wet-lab competition NMR confirms is a high-affinity, picomolar CB[7] binder. Our data and code are available at https://github.com/Zehong-Wang/MDForge.

AI.tldr: An LLM agent called MDForge automates molecular dynamics pipeline design through open-ended code generation and online verbal reward shaping, achieving expert-level performance and discovering novel high-affinity binders.
AI.motivation: Molecular dynamics (MD) pipeline design requires substantial expert knowledge and is computationally expensive, making trial-and-error approaches infeasible. Existing MD agents are limited to orchestrating predefined tools rather than true design automation.
AI.method: MDForge uses LLM agents for open-ended code generation with online verbal reward shaping. It employs a multi-agent debate among physics experts to densify sparse rewards, allowing the agent's behavior to be reshaped through in-context updates rather than fixed tool orchestration.
AI.result: On three SAMPL host-guest binding free-energy benchmarks, MDForge automatically designs MD pipelines competitive with human experts. When deployed on unseen candidate guests, it discovered a novel CB[7] binder that wet-lab NMR competition confirmed to be a high-affinity, picomolar binder.
AI.conclusion: MDForge demonstrates that LLM agents with open-ended code generation and online reward shaping can effectively automate complex scientific pipeline design, achieving expert-level performance and enabling novel discoveries in molecular science.

title: Fed-FBD: Federated Functional Block Diversification for Isolation, Privacy, and Surgical Unlearning
summary: Federated learning (FL) enables collaborative model training without sharing raw patient data, but standard approaches such as FedAvg treat each client as a black box and provide no mechanism for isolating an adversarial contributor, auditing per-client influence, or honoring a departed participant's right to be forgotten. We present Fed-FBD (Federated Functional Block Diversification), a modular federated architecture that decomposes a ResNet backbone into six functional blocks (the stem, four residual groups, and the classification head) and maintains a warehouse of N color variants, each assembled from independently tracked and contributor-stamped blocks. Fed-FBD provides three capabilities absent in FedAvg: (i) architecturally guaranteed block-level isolation, so that an adversarial or mislabelled client cannot contaminate the clean colous; (ii) privacy-by-design, where membership inference advantage is already indistinguishable from chance before any privacy mechanism is applied; and (iii) surgical machine unlearning of a departed participant's contribution at sub-second cost and without retraining. Experiments on six MedMNIST-2D datasets, PathMNIST at 224x224, and CIFAR-10 show that Fed-FBD trades a modest 0.3%-3.1% IID accuracy gap on the adequately sized datasets for these guarantees, remains within 0.8%-4.0% of FedAvg at Dirichlet alpha=1.0 on three of four datasets, and confines all six adversarial attacks we study to the poisoned client's own blocks with at most +/-0.01 AUC drift on the clean colors.

AI.tldr: Fed-FBD: A modular federated learning architecture that decomposes ResNets into functional blocks with color variants, providing adversarial isolation, privacy-by-design, and surgical unlearning capabilities.
AI.motivation: Standard federated learning (FedAvg) treats clients as black boxes without mechanisms for isolating adversarial contributions, auditing per-client influence, or honoring departed participants' right to be forgotten. These limitations are critical in sensitive domains like medical data.
AI.method: Fed-FBD decomposes ResNet backbones into six functional blocks (stem, four residual groups, classification head) and maintains a warehouse of N color variants assembled from independently tracked, contributor-stamped blocks. This modular architecture enables block-level isolation and tracking.
AI.result: Fed-FBD achieves: 1) Architecturally guaranteed block-level isolation containing adversarial attacks to poisoned client's blocks, 2) Privacy-by-design with membership inference advantage indistinguishable from chance without additional mechanisms, 3) Surgical unlearning of departed participants in sub-second time. Experimental results show modest 0.3%-3.1% IID accuracy gap compared to FedAvg, with strong performance on six MedMNIST-2D datasets, PathMNIST, and CIFAR-10.
AI.conclusion: Fed-FBD provides a practical federated learning framework that offers strong adversarial isolation, privacy guarantees, and efficient unlearning capabilities with only modest accuracy trade-offs, making it suitable for sensitive applications like medical data analysis where these properties are essential.

title: Iterating Toward Better Search: A Two-Agent Simulation Framework for Evaluating Agentic Search Architectures in E-Commerce
summary: We present a modular two-agent simulation framework for evaluating conversational shopping assistant architectures. An independent buyer agent, configured with personas, missions, and patience levels, is paired with an interchangeable responder that integrates with a real e-commerce search API. Holding the buyer constant across experiments enables controlled comparison of responder designs on identical scenarios. Using 2011 conversations across 14 persona buckets, we establish four empirical findings. First, rolling-window memory outperforms intent-extraction memory on all quality metrics while being 35% faster per query.
