Second, illustrating rapid evidence-driven iteration, a systematic failure analysis of a responder version enables targeted fixes that reduce failure and near-failure rates by 62% across the full dataset. Third, swapping the responder LLM backbone from Gemini~2.5 to Llama~3.3~70B costs 0.16--0.45 points despite identical architecture. Finally, we document systematic philosophical disagreement between frontier LLM judges: Gemini rewards process correctness while Claude demands concrete outcomes, despite using the same evaluation prompt.

AI.tldr: A modular two-agent framework for evaluating conversational shopping assistants shows that rolling-window memory outperforms intent-extraction memory, targeted fixes reduce failures by 62%, different LLM backbones affect performance despite identical architecture, and frontier LLM judges systematically disagree on evaluation criteria.
AI.motivation: There's a need for systematic evaluation frameworks to compare different conversational shopping assistant architectures in controlled settings, enabling evidence-based improvements and understanding performance differences.
AI.method: Modular two-agent simulation framework with independent buyer agent (configured with personas, missions, patience) paired with interchangeable responder that integrates with real e-commerce search API. Experiments used 2011 conversations across 14 persona buckets, holding buyer constant to compare responder designs.
AI.result: Four key findings: 1) Rolling-window memory outperforms intent-extraction memory on all quality metrics while being 35% faster per query; 2) Targeted fixes from failure analysis reduced failure/near-failure rates by 62%; 3) Swapping responder LLM backbone from Gemini 2.5 to Llama 3.3 70B costs 0.16-0.45 performance points; 4) Systematic disagreement between frontier LLM judges: Gemini rewards process correctness while Claude demands concrete outcomes.
AI.conclusion: The modular framework enables controlled evaluation and iterative improvement of conversational shopping assistants, revealing important architectural decisions (memory approach) matter more than LLM backbone changes, and highlighting challenges in LLM-based evaluation due to systematic philosophical disagreements between models.

title: How Useful is Causal Invariance for Domain Adaptation in Finite-Sample Settings?
summary:
Machine learning models often degrade when they are deployed on a target distribution that differs from the source distributions they were trained on. Recent work in causality-based domain generalization has shown how shared causal structure between domains can induce invariant predictors, e.g., models on a subset of features which have stable risk across structured domain shifts. However, the extent to which such population-level causal invariances can lead to gains in finite-sample settings remains underexplored. In particular, in practice we often have access to a few labeled target samples, a setting called supervised domain adaptation (sDA). In this paper, we explore when (full or partial) causal knowledge can provably improve supervised domain adaptation.
  As a first step, we study linear regression, where full or partial causal knowledge specifies a collection of invariant or possibly invariant feature subsets, each yielding a source-trained candidate predictor. We derive matching upper and lower bounds showing that finite-sample gains are governed by the target-risk margins separating the candidates, together with the finite-source estimation error. When these margins are sufficiently large relative to $n_Q$, an adaptive aggregation procedure can match the best candidate predictor while avoiding negative transfer relative to target-only learning. On the other hand, when the margins are too small, no algorithm can reliably exploit the candidate collection to obtain faster finite-sample rates. We further connect these margins to structural shift magnitude in linear SCMs and validate the theory on real-world causal benchmarks.

AI.tldr: The paper explores when causal knowledge can improve supervised domain adaptation, showing that finite-sample gains depend on target-risk margins between candidate predictors and that adaptive aggregation can match the best candidate while avoiding negative transfer.
AI.motivation: Machine learning models degrade when deployed on target distributions different from source distributions. While causality-based domain generalization shows how shared causal structure can induce invariant predictors, it's unclear how population-level causal invariances translate to finite-sample gains in supervised domain adaptation settings with few labeled target samples.
AI.method: The authors study linear regression where causal knowledge identifies invariant or possibly invariant feature subsets, each yielding source-trained candidate predictors. They derive matching upper and lower bounds, analyze target-risk margins separating candidates, and develop an adaptive aggregation procedure to match the best candidate while avoiding negative transfer relative to target-only learning.
AI.result: Finite-sample gains are governed by target-risk margins between candidate predictors and finite-source estimation error. When margins are large relative to target sample size, adaptive aggregation can match the best candidate predictor. When margins are too small, no algorithm can reliably exploit candidate collections for faster rates. The margins are connected to structural shift magnitude in linear structural causal models.
AI.conclusion: Causal knowledge can provably improve supervised domain adaptation when target-risk margins between candidate predictors are sufficiently large relative to target sample size, enabling adaptive methods to avoid negative transfer while matching the best candidate, but becomes ineffective when margins are too small.

title: MARS: Margin-Adversarial Risk-controlled Stopping for Parallel LLM Test-time Scaling
summary: Parallel test-time scaling samples many reasoning traces and majority-votes their answers, improving LLM accuracy but requiring traces to run to completion, incurring substantial computational overhead. We observe that probing partial traces at intermediate checkpoints can extract current answers without disrupting generation, revealing an evolving aggregate vote. Based on this observation, we introduce MARS, a margin-adversarial stopping rule that estimates which active traces are likely to change their answers and stops once the leader remains safe under a conservative bound on future vote movement. The rule separates two sources of uncertainty. It learns the trace-level switch probabilities that determine how much of the current margin is likely to be retained, while handling the harder question of where switching traces land through an adversarial bound calibrated from warmup traces. With true switch probabilities, MARS guarantees with high probability that the early-stopped answer matches the full-budget vote. In practice, a five-feature logistic model closely matches oracle switching behavior. Across three reasoning models and three competition-math benchmarks, MARS saves 25-47% of self-consistency tokens and 14-29% on top of DeepConf Online, a strong confidence-weighted baseline that already filters and truncates weak traces, while matching the accuracy of the corresponding full-budget baselines.

AI.tldr: MARS introduces an early stopping rule for parallel test-time scaling that saves computational tokens while maintaining accuracy by predicting which reasoning traces will change answers and stopping when the leading answer is safe.
AI.motivation: Parallel test-time scaling (self-consistency) improves LLM accuracy through majority voting of multiple reasoning traces, but requires all traces to run to completion, resulting in substantial computational overhead and inefficiency.
AI.method: MARS (Margin-Adversarial Stopping Rule) probes partial traces at intermediate checkpoints to extract current answers without disrupting generation, estimates which active traces are likely to change answers using a logistic model of trace-level switch probabilities, and stops generation once the leader remains safe under a conservative bound on future vote movement calibrated from warmup traces.
AI.result: MARS saves 25-47% of self-consistency tokens and achieves 14-29% additional savings beyond DeepConf Online (a strong baseline), while matching the accuracy of full-budget baselines across three reasoning models and three competition-math benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed MARS framework enables efficient early stopping in parallel test-time scaling by separating uncertainty into trace-level switching behavior and answer destination, providing computational savings without sacrificing accuracy through provable safety guarantees when using correct switching probabilities.

title: Forecasting Is Not Attribution: Localizing Decoder Bypass in Graph-Based Neural Marketing Mix Models
summary: Marketing mix models are used to forecast business outcomes and to attribute those outcomes to marketing channels, but these goals are not equivalent. We study a failure mode in graph-based neural MMM called attribution bypass: a high-capacity decoder can obtain low forecasting error through target autoregression, dense communication, co-movement, context, or latent memory while failing to route counterfactual sensitivity through the graph used as the attribution object. We introduce DICE-MMM as a bounded diagnostic and training framework. We do not claim that observational neural MMM identifies causal effects. Instead, DICE separates three questions often conflated in graph-based MMM: graph recovery, forecasting accuracy, and whether the trained decoder's perturbation-induced influence is graph aligned. Stage 1 trains a graph encoder with a restricted graph-mediated decoder. Stage 2 freezes the selected encoder and trains a graph-safe latent decoder whose cross-node communication must pass through the supplied graph. Decoder use is evaluated with CIG, AR-CIG, and graph-swap tests. Across controlled R/d/T swaps and an external multi-graph rawlog stress test, DICE improves stable graph recovery over CausalMMM. The experiments show that forecasting accuracy is not an attribution certificate: in a sparse-target benchmark, no-graph and full-graph decoders achieve MSE@7 around 0.004 while AR-CIG nAUPRC remains near or below zero, whereas an oracle graph reaches 0.807 +/- 0.129 at comparable MSE. Frozen graph-swap localizes the bottleneck: the same DICE-hard-trained decoder moves from nAUPRC -0.044 +/- 0.006 under learned graph inputs to 0.894 +/- 0.027 with the oracle graph. The contribution is a stress test and failure-localization framework showing that low MSE can hide attribution bypass and that the unresolved bottleneck is graph-support selection, not forecasting or decoder capacity.

AI.tldr: DICE-MMM is a diagnostic framework that separates graph recovery, forecasting, and attribution alignment in neural marketing mix models, showing that low forecasting error can mask attribution bypass problems.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the "attribution bypass" problem in neural marketing mix models, where high-capacity decoders can achieve low forecasting error through target autoregression or other shortcuts while failing to properly route counterfactual sensitivity through the attribution graph. This creates a disconnect between forecasting accuracy and attribution validity.
AI.method: The authors introduce DICE-MMM, a two-stage training framework with diagnostic tests. Stage 1 trains a graph encoder with a restricted graph-mediated decoder. Stage 2 freezes the selected encoder and trains a graph-safe latent decoder that forces cross-node communication to pass through the supplied graph. Evaluation uses CIG, AR-CIG, and graph-swap tests to assess attribution alignment.
AI.result: Experimental results show that DICE-MMM improves stable graph recovery over existing methods like CausalMMM. The framework reveals that forecasting accuracy alone is insufficient for attribution validation - in sparse-target benchmarks, no-graph and full-graph decoders achieve similar MSE but have near-zero AR-CIG nAUPRC, while oracle graphs achieve 0.807 nAUPRC at comparable MSE.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that low mean squared error (MSE) can hide attribution bypass issues in marketing mix models, and that the fundamental bottleneck is graph-support selection rather than forecasting accuracy or decoder capacity. The DICE-MMM framework provides a diagnostic tool to identify and address these limitations.

title: PRISMR: Overcoming Parse Collapse in Multimodal Listwise Ranking via Parameterized Representation Internalization
summary:
Generative listwise ranking with Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) aims to capture global list context in a single forward pass, but
  its effectiveness degrades in long-context multimodal scenarios. We identify a recurring failure mode, parse collapse, where the
  autoregressive decoder produces fluent yet incomplete rankings by silently omitting candidates and terminating early. This
  failure stems from limited context utilization rather than simple formatting mistakes, making prompt engineering and constrained
  decoding insufficient. We propose PRISMR (Parameterized Representation Internalization for Semantic Multimodal Ranking), a
  framework that replaces transient in-context list processing with parametric structural conditioning. PRISMR uses a lightweight
  hypernetwork to encode multimodal candidates in parallel and generate item-specific LoRA weights, which are synthesized into an
  instance-specific adapter for a LMM. This paradigm enables more robust internalization of list structure while preserving the
  base model. We further introduce a large-scale multimodal review-ranking benchmark for evaluation. Experiments demonstrate that
  PRISMR substantially reduces parse collapse, improves listwise ranking performance, and transfers effectively across domains and
  instruction-tuned backbones.

AI.tldr: PRISMR is a framework that addresses parse collapse in generative listwise ranking with Large Multimodal Models by replacing in-context list processing with parametric structural conditioning using hypernetworks and LoRA weights.
AI.motivation: Generative listwise ranking with LMMs suffers from degraded effectiveness in long-context multimodal scenarios due to "parse collapse" - a failure mode where the autoregressive decoder produces incomplete rankings by omitting candidates and terminating early, stemming from limited context utilization rather than formatting issues.
AI.method: PRISMR replaces transient in-context list processing with parametric structural conditioning. It uses a lightweight hypernetwork to encode multimodal candidates in parallel and generate item-specific LoRA weights, which are synthesized into an instance-specific adapter for an LMM, enabling robust internalization of list structure while preserving the base model.
AI.result: Experiments show PRISMR substantially reduces parse collapse, improves listwise ranking performance, and transfers effectively across domains and instruction-tuned backbones. The authors also introduce a large-scale multimodal review-ranking benchmark for evaluation.
AI.conclusion: PRISMR provides an effective solution to the parse collapse problem in generative listwise ranking with LMMs by enabling more robust structural conditioning through parametric methods, outperforming prompt engineering and constrained decoding approaches that fail to address the fundamental limitation of context utilization.

title: M*: A Modular, Extensible, Serving System for Multimodal Models
summary: We are entering a new era of composite model architectures that integrate diverse components such as vision encoders, language backbones, diffusion and flow heads, audio codecs, action generators, and world-model predictors. Such architectures underpin a broad class of multimodal models, including unified multimodal models, omni models, speech-language models, vision-language-action policies, and world models. However, existing model serving frameworks were built on narrow assumptions about model structure, making them ill-suited to accommodate this new architectural diversity. Here we present M*, a universal serving system for efficient serving of composite AI models. M* represents models as dataflow graphs, processing requests spanning diverse modalities and tasks as traversals over these graphs. The core insight is a modular abstraction that supports arbitrary composition of model components, flexible placement onto a physical cluster, and model-agnostic optimizations within a distributed runtime. We call this abstraction the Walk Graph and show how it can concisely capture composite models from a broad range of families. We instantiate M* on representative models and find that it achieves, on average, 20% lower end-to-end latency than vLLM-Omni for text-to-image workloads on BAGEL, while delivering up to 2.9x lower real-time factor and 2.7x higher throughput for text-to-speech workloads on Qwen3-Omni. M* also outperforms the V-JEPA 2-AC rollout baseline for robotic planning by up to 12.5x. Thus, our work paves the road towards more efficient serving of complex models with minimal developer effort.

AI.tldr: M* is a universal serving system for composite AI models that achieves 20-50% lower latency than existing systems across diverse multimodal workloads.
AI.motivation: Existing model serving frameworks were designed for narrow model architectures and can't handle the new era of composite models that integrate diverse components like vision encoders, language backbones, diffusion heads, audio codecs, etc.
AI.method: M* uses a modular abstraction called Walk Graph that represents models as dataflow graphs, allowing arbitrary composition of model components, flexible cluster placement, and model-agnostic optimizations in a distributed runtime.
AI.result: M* achieves 20% lower end-to-end latency than vLLM-Omni for text-to-image workloads, 2.9x lower real-time factor and 2.7x higher throughput for text-to-speech workloads, and 12.5x performance improvement for robotic planning tasks.
AI.conclusion: M* paves the way for efficient serving of complex composite AI models with minimal developer effort, addressing the architectural diversity of modern multimodal models.

title: Learning What to Remember: A Cognitively Grounded Multi-Factor Value Model for Agentic Memory
summary: Long-running LLM agents accumulate interaction histories far larger than any context window, forcing a standing decision: what to encode deeply, what to forget, and what to retrieve under a fixed memory budget. Production systems answer with semantic similarity or recency -- both mis-specified for the forgetting decision, which is made at consolidation time before the future query is known. We propose a multi-factor memory value function V(m)=\sum_i w_i f_i(m) over seven interpretable factors (emotional intensity, goal relevance, value alignment, self/user relevance, task utility, reliability, and usage history) drawn from cognitive psychology, whose weights are learned from a downstream objective by a gradient-free optimiser, and whose single scalar uniformly controls encoding depth, forget risk, and retrieval rank. We make a methodological point: on LongMemEval, scoring goal relevance against the held-out evaluation question saturates gold-evidence retention at \approx 0.98 -- this measures retrieval, not forgetting. In the realistic blind regime, a learned multi-factor value retains 0.770 \pm 0.011 of gold evidence across 479 usable cases, versus 0.657 for uniform weights, 0.518 for the best single factor, and 0.368 for recency; every paired gap's 95% bootstrap CI is above zero, and a neural network over the same factors ties the linear model. The learned weights are interpretable -- reliability, emotional intensity, and self/user relevance dominate, while query-time goal similarity is correctly down-weighted for the forgetting decision. A controlled synthetic task with planted confounds confirms the learner recovers a separating weighting (1.00 retention) where uniform weighting fails (0.62). The substrate is open-source; all experiments run on a single CPU with no API calls.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a multi-factor memory value function for LLM agents that learns optimal weights from cognitive psychology factors to decide what to remember, forget, and retrieve under fixed memory constraints, outperforming conventional methods like recency or semantic similarity.
AI.motivation: Long-running LLM agents accumulate large interaction histories exceeding context windows, requiring decisions about what to encode, forget, and retrieve. Current production systems use semantic similarity or recency, which are mis-specified for forgetting decisions made before future queries are known.
AI.method: Proposes a multi-factor memory value function V(m)=∑wᵢfᵢ(m) with seven interpretable factors from cognitive psychology (emotional intensity, goal relevance, value alignment, self/user relevance, task utility, reliability, usage history). Weights are learned via gradient-free optimization from downstream objectives, creating a single scalar controlling encoding depth, forget risk, and retrieval rank.
AI.result: On LongMemEval, the learned multi-factor value retains 77.0% of gold evidence vs. 65.7% for uniform weights, 51.8% for best single factor, and 36.8% for recency. All gaps are statistically significant. Learned weights are interpretable (reliability, emotional intensity, self/user relevance dominate). On controlled synthetic tasks, achieves 1.00 retention vs. 0.62 for uniform weighting.
AI.conclusion: A learned multi-factor memory value function outperforms conventional approaches for memory management in LLM agents. The method demonstrates that query-time goal similarity should be down-weighted for forgetting decisions, and that interpretable factors from cognitive psychology can be effectively combined through learned weights to optimize memory retention.

title: Two-Layer Linear Auto-Regressive Models Estimate Latent States
summary: Auto-regressive models have emerged as powerful tools for sequential data, from language to video. Understanding how and why these models learn latent representations remains an open theoretical question. In this work, we demonstrate that when trained by empirical risk minimization on data from partially observed linear dynamical systems, two-layer linear auto-regressive models naturally learn to approximate Kalman filtering. In particular, we show that the learned hidden representation coincides, up to a similarity transformation, with the state estimates produced by the optimal (Kalman) filter, even though the model has no explicit knowledge of the underlying dynamics or state. The result follows from three main insights. First, we establish that the Kalman filter is well approximated by an auto-regressive model with bounded truncation error. Second, we show that despite non-convexity, the two-layer optimization landscape is benign, i.e., all stationary points are either strict saddles or global minima. Finally, as our main contributions, we provide finite-sample guarantees on prediction error, parameter estimation error, and latent state recovery. Numerical simulations support the theoretical results and demonstrate that the latent representations of auto-regressive models recover state estimates.

AI.tldr: Auto-regressive models trained on data from linear dynamical systems naturally learn to approximate Kalman filtering, recovering optimal state estimates without explicit knowledge of the underlying dynamics.
AI.motivation: Auto-regressive models are powerful for sequential data but understanding how they learn latent representations remains an open theoretical question. The paper aims to show that these models can naturally learn optimal filtering when trained on appropriate data.
AI.method: 1) Show Kalman filter can be approximated by auto-regressive model with bounded truncation error. 2) Prove the two-layer optimization landscape is benign despite non-convexity (all stationary points are either strict saddles or global minima). 3) Provide finite-sample guarantees on prediction error, parameter estimation error, and latent state recovery.
AI.result: When trained by empirical risk minimization on data from partially observed linear dynamical systems, two-layer linear auto-regressive models learn hidden representations that coincide (up to similarity transformation) with state estimates produced by the optimal Kalman filter. Numerical simulations confirm theoretical results.
AI.conclusion: Auto-regressive models can implicitly learn to perform optimal filtering, bridging the gap between deep learning and classical control theory. This provides theoretical justification for why these models learn useful latent representations from sequential data.

title: OpenMedQ: Broad Open Pretraining for Medical Vision-Language Models
comment: Medical Imaging with Deep Learning (MIDL) 2026, Short Paper Track
summary: We present OpenMedQ, a medical vision-language model pretrained on the broadest fully-open medical mix to date: 14 datasets totaling ~3.35M pretraining samples spanning pathology, radiology, microscopy, and text-only clinical QA. OpenMedQ reaches state-of-the-art BLEU-1 on PathVQA (75.9), beating Med-PaLM M variants up to 562B parameters (~80x larger), and matches the best reported VQA-MED BLEU-1 (64.5). Its vision encoder, transferred to 8 unseen medical classification benchmarks under an identical downstream recipe, obtains the highest average macro-F1 (0.757) among BiomedCLIP (0.745), PMC-CLIP (0.745), PubMedCLIP (0.746), and a from-scratch baseline (0.616). We release our code and an interactive demo is publicly available as a reproducible baseline for the community.

AI.tldr: OpenMedQ is a medical vision-language model trained on 3.35M samples from 14 datasets across multiple medical domains, achieving state-of-the-art performance on medical VQA benchmarks and strong transfer learning results.
AI.motivation: To create a comprehensive, fully-open medical vision-language model that addresses the need for reproducible baselines in medical AI, leveraging diverse medical data across pathology, radiology, microscopy, and clinical QA.
AI.method: Pretrained on 14 medical datasets totaling ~3.35M samples covering pathology, radiology, microscopy, and text-only clinical QA, using a vision-language model architecture that can be transferred to various medical tasks.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art BLEU-1 on PathVQA (75.9), beating much larger models; matches best reported VQA-MED BLEU-1 (64.5); vision encoder achieves highest average macro-F1 (0.757) on 8 unseen medical classification benchmarks compared to other medical CLIP models.
AI.conclusion: OpenMedQ demonstrates that broad, diverse medical pretraining with fully-open data produces strong performance on medical vision-language tasks, providing a valuable reproducible baseline for the research community with code and demo released.

title: LLM-Powered Personalized Glycemic Assessment in Type 2 Diabetes with Wearable Sensor Data
comment: The 14th IEEE International Conference on Healthcare Informatics, 2026
summary:
Type 2 Diabetes (T2D) poses an increasing global health threat, demanding effective glycemic assessment to support personalized and improved diabetes care. Wearable sensors such as continuous glucose monitors (CGM) and fitness trackers offer many valuable insights for glycemic assessment. However, effectively analyzing these data requires integration with essential individual-level context. Existing methods are often based on traditional machine learning (ML) and rely primarily on historical blood glucose measurements and overlook personalized information, which limits their performance across diverse diabetes populations. Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated their ability to integrate diverse data modalities while modeling sequential dependencies, motivating the exploration of their potential for personalized glycemic assessment.
  In this paper, we propose GlyLLM, an LLM-powered framework for modeling CGM-based glycemic dynamics through the integration of wearable sensor data and structured metadata. GlyLLM can leverage the extensive prior knowledge of pre-trained LLMs and achieve sensor-text semantic abstraction at decision time. Experiments on two related tasks on the AI-READI dataset demonstrate that our model outperforms traditional ML methods by an average of 13.66\% in Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) for glucose forecasting and 13.08\% in Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (AUROC) for diabetes categorization. Additionally, our ablation study shows that diabetes surveys and biometric tests are more critical than other health information for glycemic assessment. Our work presents a promising step toward harnessing the power of LLMs to advance personalized glycemic assessment in T2D care.

AI.tldr: GlyLLM: An LLM-powered framework that integrates wearable sensor data (CGM/fitness trackers) with structured metadata for improved glycemic assessment in Type 2 Diabetes, outperforming traditional ML methods by ~13% in both glucose forecasting and diabetes categorization tasks.
AI.motivation: Type 2 Diabetes poses a growing global health challenge requiring better glycemic assessment. While wearable sensors provide valuable data, current methods based on traditional ML primarily use historical glucose data and overlook personalized context, limiting performance across diverse populations. LLMs' ability to integrate diverse data modalities and model sequential dependencies motivates their exploration for personalized glycemic assessment.
AI.method: GlyLLM - an LLM-powered framework that models continuous glucose monitor (CGM)-based glycemic dynamics by integrating wearable sensor data (CGM and fitness trackers) with structured metadata. The approach leverages pre-trained LLMs' extensive prior knowledge and achieves sensor-text semantic abstraction at decision time.
AI.result: On the AI-READI dataset, GlyLLM outperforms traditional ML methods by an average of 13.66% in RMSE for glucose forecasting and 13.08% in AUROC for diabetes categorization. Ablation studies show diabetes surveys and biometric tests are more critical than other health information for glycemic assessment.
AI.conclusion: GlyLLM presents a promising step toward harnessing LLMs to advance personalized glycemic assessment in T2D care, demonstrating superior performance over traditional ML methods by effectively integrating multimodal data including wearable sensors and contextual metadata.

title: Multi-Modal Agents for Power Distribution Defect Detection: An Evaluation of Foundation Models
summary: The power distribution network is critical to reliable electricity delivery, yet traditional inspection methods face limitations in semantic understanding, generalization, and closed-loop automation. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a Multi-Modal Agent framework specifically for power distribution defect detection. Central to this study is the systematic evaluation of multimodal foundation models as unified cognitive engines. We rigorously assess their integrated performance across three critical capabilities: (1) Perception, where the model must accurately identify equipment and generate expert-level descriptions of defects; (2) Reasoning, where the model interprets visual findings to diagnose causes, assess severity, and plan maintenance strategies based on domain knowledge; and (3) Tool Usage, where the model acts as an autonomous operator to execute actions -- such as querying knowledge bases or generating work orders -- to achieve closed-loop maintenance. To support this evaluation, a domain-specific evaluation dataset and a comprehensive benchmark are developed. Experimental results demonstrate the strengths and limitations of current foundation models in these three dimensions, providing empirical evidence for deploying autonomous agents in high-stakes industrial environments.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a Multi-Modal Agent framework for power distribution defect detection and systematically evaluates multimodal foundation models across perception, reasoning, and tool usage capabilities in industrial settings.
AI.motivation: Traditional power distribution network inspection methods face limitations in semantic understanding, generalization, and closed-loop automation, creating a need for more intelligent, automated defect detection systems.
AI.method: The paper proposes a Multi-Modal Agent framework for power distribution defect detection and systematically evaluates multimodal foundation models as unified cognitive engines across three capabilities: perception (identifying equipment and describing defects), reasoning (interpreting findings, diagnosing causes, assessing severity, planning maintenance), and tool usage (acting as autonomous operator to execute actions like querying knowledge bases or generating work orders). The authors also developed a domain-specific evaluation dataset and comprehensive benchmark.
AI.result: Experimental results demonstrate both the strengths and limitations of current foundation models across the three evaluated dimensions (perception, reasoning, tool usage), providing empirical evidence for deploying autonomous agents in high-stakes industrial environments.
AI.conclusion: The study provides important insights into the capabilities of multimodal foundation models for power distribution defect detection, highlighting their potential while identifying current limitations, which informs the practical deployment of autonomous agents in critical industrial settings.

title: A Stabilized Path-Space Approach to Diffusion-Based Posterior Sampling
summary: Diffusion models provide expressive data-driven priors for Bayesian inverse problems, but many diffusion posterior samplers rely on heuristic guidance approximations that can fail for nonlinear operators and multimodal posteriors. In this work, we develop a stabilized path-space framework for diffusion-based posterior sampling. Starting from a base diffusion process whose terminal marginal represents the prior, we define a likelihood-weighted target measure on trajectories and cast posterior sampling as learning a controlled stochastic process whose path measure matches this target. This formulation connects diffusion posterior sampling to stochastic optimal control while preserving the Bayesian structure needed for uncertainty quantification. We introduce a time reparameterization that makes the path-space control problem well posed by removing the bias induced by the unknown initial value function, without auxiliary training. We then learn the control via a trust-region path-space optimization method with log-variance objectives. The path-space perspective also unifies our learned control approach with existing guidance-based samplers, quantifies the sampling error induced by approximate controls, and yields importance sampling corrections for asymptotically exact posterior expectations. We evaluate the proposed framework on a suite of benchmark inverse problems with analytically characterized or high-quality reference posteriors, enabling principled assessment of sampling accuracy and uncertainty quantification. These experiments provide insight into the behavior of diffusion-based posterior samplers and demonstrate improved accuracy and robustness over leading approaches.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a stabilized path-space framework for diffusion-based Bayesian posterior sampling that connects diffusion models to stochastic optimal control, enabling principled uncertainty quantification and improved accuracy over heuristic guidance methods.
AI.motivation: Current diffusion posterior samplers rely on heuristic guidance approximations that can fail for nonlinear operators and multimodal posteriors, limiting their reliability for Bayesian inverse problems.
AI.method: The authors develop a path-space framework that defines likelihood-weighted target measures on trajectories and casts posterior sampling as learning a controlled stochastic process. They introduce time reparameterization to make the control problem well-posed and use trust-region path-space optimization with log-variance objectives to learn the control.
AI.result: The framework unifies learned control approaches with existing guidance-based samplers, quantifies sampling errors induced by approximate controls, and yields importance sampling corrections for asymptotically exact posterior expectations. Experiments on benchmark inverse problems demonstrate improved accuracy and robustness over leading approaches.
AI.conclusion: The path-space framework provides a principled, stabilized approach to diffusion-based posterior sampling that preserves Bayesian structure for uncertainty quantification while overcoming limitations of heuristic guidance methods, enabling more accurate and robust inverse problem solutions.

title: A Mathematical Forum Platform for Collaborative Problem Solving and Dataset Generation for AI Reasoning
summary: Sharing mathematical content in online forums remains a significant friction point for students and educators: writing raw LATEX is error-prone, standalone optical character recognition tools require platform switching, and current forum software offers no integrated path from a photograph of a formula to a rendered post. We present a unified system that eliminates this friction by embedding an image to LATEX conversion pipeline directly inside a forum posting interface. A user uploads or captures an image of a mathematical expression; the system routes it through the Mathpix OCR API, detects whether the returned output is LATEX or plain text containing inline math, applies the appropriate delimiter normalisation, and renders a live preview in either LATEX or Markdown mode before the post is committed to the database. The architecture is organized in three loosely coupled layers: image processing, rendering, and storage, and supports both desktop and mobile clients. A provisional US patent application has been filed covering the core methods. We describe the full system design, each component in detail, the data schema, and the key technical innovations, and we position the work against existing standalone tools and forum platforms to demonstrate the practical gap it closes. Beyond immediate usability, we argue that a deployed platform of this kind constitutes a continuously growing, community-validated dataset of mathematical problems and step-by-step solutions, a resource that can be used to train and benchmark AI systems for accurate mathematical reasoning

AI.tldr: A system that integrates image-to-LaTeX OCR directly into forum software, eliminating friction in sharing mathematical content online by allowing users to upload formula images that get automatically converted to properly formatted math notation.
AI.motivation: Current methods for sharing mathematical content in online forums create significant friction: writing raw LaTeX is error-prone, standalone OCR tools require platform switching, and forum software lacks integrated image-to-math conversion capabilities.
AI.method: The system embeds an image-to-LaTeX conversion pipeline directly in forum interfaces using the Mathpix OCR API. It processes images, detects output types (LaTeX or plain text with inline math), applies delimiter normalization, provides live previews, and uses a three-layer architecture (image processing, rendering, storage) supporting both desktop and mobile clients.
AI.result: The system eliminates friction in mathematical content sharing, has a provisional US patent application for core methods, and creates a continuously growing community-validated dataset of mathematical problems and solutions that can train AI systems for accurate mathematical reasoning.
AI.conclusion: The unified system successfully closes a practical gap in mathematical content sharing for online communities, transforming forum platforms into valuable datasets for AI training while significantly improving usability for students and educators.

title: Out-of-Distribution (OOD) Detectors for Open-Set RF Fingerprinting
summary: Radio-frequency (RF) fingerprinting systems must operate in open-world environments where signals from unknown transmitters and temporal drift introduce distribution shift at test time. Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection provides a natural framework for this problem, yet its application to RF fingerprinting (RFF) remains limited. A key barrier to their adoption is that most OOD detectors require auxiliary OOD data for parameter tuning, an assumption that is difficult to satisfy in RF environments where representative OOD data is impractical to collect. In this work, we introduce a promising set of OOD detection methods from the machine learning literature to open-set RFF domain. We present these methods within a unified mathematical framework based on information theory, which is a natural framework for communication systems. Our framework allows for the systematic analysis of methods and development of new methods. We further demonstrate the applicability of recent work on tuning OOD detectors without given OOD tuning data for open-set RFF. We evaluate on the POWDER RF fingerprinting dataset, showing that detectors tuned without any given OOD data achieve performance comparable to baselines with access to true OOD tuning data and greatly out-perform baseline approaches without access to true OOD tuning data, showcasing the practical viability for the RFF problem.

AI.tldr: OOD detection methods can be applied to RF fingerprinting in open-world environments, and new tuning approaches work well even without access to OOD data for parameter tuning.
AI.motivation: RF fingerprinting systems face challenges in open-world environments where unknown transmitters and temporal drift cause distribution shift. OOD detection provides a framework, but adoption is limited because most OOD detectors require auxiliary OOD data for tuning, which is impractical to collect in RF environments.
AI.method: The paper introduces OOD detection methods from ML literature to open-set RF fingerprinting domain, presents them within a unified mathematical framework based on information theory, and demonstrates applicability of recent work on tuning OOD detectors without given OOD tuning data.
AI.result: Evaluation on POWDER RF fingerprinting dataset shows detectors tuned without any given OOD data achieve performance comparable to baselines with access to true OOD tuning data, and greatly outperform baseline approaches without access to true OOD tuning data.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates practical viability for RF fingerprinting problems in open-world environments by enabling effective OOD detection without requiring impractical collection of representative OOD data for tuning.

title: Structured Testbench Generation for LLM-Driven HDL Design and Verification-Oriented Data Curation
summary: Automated testbench generation has become a critical bottleneck in large language model (LLM)-driven Register Transfer Level (RTL) workflows, where large numbers of candidate designs must be verified rapidly and reliably. Existing prompt-based approaches treat testbench generation as unconstrained code synthesis, yielding stochastic outputs with high token cost, low reproducibility, and insufficient coverage. To address this gap, we present STG, a Structured Testbench Generation framework that exploits the inherent structure of hardware designs to generate deterministic testbenches. As a direct verification tool, STG runs 720x faster than an iterative LLM-based testbench generation flow and higher rate of successful compilation, achieves higher coverage, and reduces false-pass verdicts on incorrect DUTs. STG also helps identify errors in RTL generation benchmarks by exposing faulty benchmark testbenches. As a data curation engine, it is 11x faster than LLM-based filtering on a single CPU core with 127x less energy, and the resulting distilled models provide state-of-the-art performance in our multi-benchmark evaluation. As a test-time scaling oracle, it reduces node count by 14-47\%. Our models are available at https://huggingface.co/collections/AS-SiliconMind/siliconmind-v12.

AI.tldr: STG is a structured testbench generation framework that uses hardware design structure to create deterministic testbenches, achieving 720x faster verification than LLM-based approaches while improving coverage and reducing false passes.
AI.motivation: Automated testbench generation is a critical bottleneck in LLM-driven RTL workflows, where existing prompt-based approaches produce stochastic outputs with high token cost, low reproducibility, and insufficient coverage.
AI.method: STG exploits the inherent structure of hardware designs to generate deterministic testbenches, moving beyond unconstrained code synthesis approaches to create structured, reliable testbenches.
AI.result: STG runs 720x faster than iterative LLM-based testbench generation with higher compilation success rates, achieves higher coverage, reduces false-pass verdicts, identifies errors in RTL benchmarks, serves as an efficient data curation engine (11x faster with 127x less energy), and enables test-time scaling with 14-47% node count reduction.
AI.conclusion: STG provides a structured framework that dramatically improves testbench generation efficiency, reliability, and coverage while enabling better model distillation and test-time optimization, representing a significant advancement in hardware verification workflows.

title: Normative Robustness as a Frontier for Non-Verifiable Reasoning in LLMs
summary: As LLMs increasingly serve in advisory and deliberative roles, users rely on them for non-verifiable reasoning in domains lacking objective ground truths. However, traditional evaluations of LLM reasoning focus almost exclusively on fact-based domains, such as mathematics and science, leaving uncertainty over whether and to what degree models can handle ambiguous, subjective, or value-laden problems over time. To address this concern, we propose moral reasoning as a paradigmatic subdomain of non-verifiable reasoning. We define moral robustness as a model's capacity to exhibit sound moral reasoning across time and contexts, and we introduce a scalable, adversarial, multi-turn evaluation framework to empirically measure this capability. We simulate 48,000 user-agent moral deliberations across four frontier LLMs, varying premise relevance, premise order, conversation duration, and the user's stated moral view. We find that models successfully ignore morally-irrelevant distractors, but shift their reasoning by up to 6.5%, on average, towards the user's stated preferred moral view, and varying their reasoning depending on factors such as order (altering moral judgments by order in 13-22% of the cases) and duration (altering moral judgments between single-turn and multi-turn in 10-24% of the cases). Our analysis indicates that models tailor not just their final verdicts but their underlying justifications to align with a user's moral viewpoint - a failure mode we characterize as moral deliberative sycophancy.

AI.tldr: LLMs exhibit moral deliberative sycophancy - they tailor moral judgments and justifications to align with users' stated moral viewpoints in ambiguous domains, rather than maintaining consistent reasoning principles.
AI.motivation: Current LLM evaluations focus almost exclusively on fact-based domains (mathematics, science), leaving uncertainty about models' ability to handle ambiguous, subjective, or value-laden problems over time where there are no objective ground truths.
AI.method: Propose moral reasoning as paradigmatic subdomain of non-verifiable reasoning. Define moral robustness as capacity for sound moral reasoning across time/contexts. Introduce scalable, adversarial, multi-turn evaluation framework simulating 48,000 user-agent moral deliberations across 4 frontier LLMs, varying premise relevance, premise order, conversation duration, and users' stated moral views.
AI.result: Models successfully ignore morally-irrelevant distractors, but shift reasoning by up to 6.5% on average towards users' preferred moral views. Moral judgments altered by premise order in 13-22% of cases and between single/multi-turn conversations in 10-24% of cases. Models tailor both final verdicts and underlying justifications to align with user's moral viewpoint.
AI.conclusion: LLMs exhibit "moral deliberative sycophancy" - systematically adjusting moral reasoning to please users rather than maintaining principled consistency. This reveals critical vulnerability in models serving advisory roles in ambiguous domains, highlighting need for evaluations beyond fact-based domains and development of moral robustness.

title: APCyc: Property-Informed Design of Cyclic Peptides via Automated Cyclization
comment: Accepted at the 32nd ACM SIGKDD Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining (KDD 2026)
summary: Cyclic peptides represent a promising class of therapeutic compounds in modern drug discovery, often offering improved stability and binding affinity. However, the de novo design of cyclic peptides remains challenging because methods must identify pocket-adaptive cyclization patterns and linkage sites while simultaneously controlling drug-relevant properties. This challenge is particularly pronounced for recent generative models trained predominantly on linear peptide data, which may fail to capture cyclization-specific constraints. To address the limitation, we introduce APCyc, a target-aware de novo cyclic peptide generation framework that explicitly models cyclization and jointly optimizes multiple essential physicochemical properties. By using an expanded residue vocabulary and explicitly encoding cyclization-site and linkage-type information, APCyc learns cyclization-aware representations and leverages Bayesian posterior guidance to steer sampling toward cyclic peptides satisfying multiple property objectives. Experimental results demonstrate that our model learns target-dependent cyclization preferences, and enables effective and controllable multi-property optimization for cyclic peptide design. The source code of this paper is available at https://github.com/HKUSTGZ-ML4Health-Lab/APCyc.

AI.tldr: APCyc is a target-aware generative framework for de novo cyclic peptide design that explicitly models cyclization patterns and optimizes multiple physicochemical properties using Bayesian guidance.
AI.motivation: Cyclic peptides are promising therapeutic compounds but de novo design is challenging due to need for pocket-adaptive cyclization patterns, linkage site selection, and property control. Existing generative models trained on linear peptide data fail to capture cyclization-specific constraints.
AI.method: APCyc uses an expanded residue vocabulary and explicit encoding of cyclization-site and linkage-type information to learn cyclization-aware representations. It leverages Bayesian posterior guidance to steer sampling toward cyclic peptides satisfying multiple property objectives.
AI.result: Experimental results show the model learns target-dependent cyclization preferences and enables effective and controllable multi-property optimization for cyclic peptide design.
AI.conclusion: APCyc addresses limitations of current methods by providing a target-aware framework that explicitly models cyclization while optimizing essential properties, advancing de novo cyclic peptide design capabilities.

title: Let's Ask Gauss: Improved One-Run Privacy Auditing
summary: Privacy auditing provides an important safeguard by estimating the actual information leaked by a model, thus ensuring that theoretical privacy guarantees hold in practice. We study empirical privacy auditing for differentially private (DP) machine learning, focusing on efficient one-run methods for mechanisms such as DP-SGD. Prior one-run approaches threshold training examples or "canaries" into binary membership guesses, which discards useful information. We show that, in the white-box DP-SGD setting, canary-aligned signals naturally form a sequence of random variables whose normalized sum is asymptotically Gaussian. Leveraging this distributional perspective, we develop a DP-auditing framework that leads to tighter privacy lower bounds from a single training run.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a new differential privacy auditing framework that leverages Gaussian asymptotic properties of canary-aligned signals in DP-SGD to provide tighter privacy lower bounds from a single training run.
AI.motivation: Privacy auditing is crucial to verify that theoretical differential privacy guarantees hold in practice, but current one-run methods are inefficient as they discard useful information by thresholding training examples into binary membership guesses.
AI.method: The authors study empirical privacy auditing for DP machine learning, focusing on white-box DP-SGD settings. They show that canary-aligned signals form a sequence of random variables whose normalized sum is asymptotically Gaussian, and develop a DP-auditing framework that leverages this distributional perspective.
AI.result: The proposed framework provides tighter privacy lower bounds compared to prior approaches that use binary membership guessing, enabling more accurate auditing from just a single training run.
AI.conclusion: By shifting from binary classification to a distributional perspective of canary-aligned signals, the paper presents an improved empirical privacy auditing method that better utilizes available information to achieve more precise privacy lower bounds for DP-SGD mechanisms.

title: Physics-Informed Neural Networks and Radial Basis Functions for PDEs with Dirac Delta Sources
summary: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) are a machine learning method for solving forward and inverse Partial Differential Equations (PDEs). When applied to PDEs with Dirac delta functions in the forcing terms, boundary conditions, or initial conditions, PINNs require approximating them with smooth surrogate functions, a practice that can introduce significant modeling errors. In this work, we exploit the interpretation of PINNs as Residual Least Squares (RLS) methods and show that this perspective enables direct treatment of Dirac delta terms by integrating the weak-form equation. Among RLS formulations other than PINN, we focus on the Radial Basis Function (RBF) expansion (also known as a single-layer RBF Network). We show that while integrating out the Dirac delta in PINNs causes residuals to fail to converge to zero, RBF-RLS consistently provides good forward and inverse solutions to transport problems. We explain this finding using the Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) theory. We test both approaches on linear PDEs that represent groundwater flow and transport in porous media and rivers. We solve inverse problems to fit synthetic data, noisy synthetic data, and real-world measurements.

AI.tldr: PINNs struggle with Dirac delta terms in PDEs, requiring smoothing that introduces errors; this paper shows that viewing PINNs as Residual Least Squares methods enables direct treatment of Dirac deltas via weak-form integration, with RBF-RLS performing better than PINNs on transport problems.
AI.motivation: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) face limitations when solving PDEs with Dirac delta functions in forcing terms, boundary conditions, or initial conditions. The standard practice of approximating Dirac deltas with smooth surrogate functions introduces significant modeling errors. The authors seek to address this limitation by exploring alternative approaches that can handle Dirac delta terms more effectively.
AI.method: The authors exploit the interpretation of PINNs as Residual Least Squares (RLS) methods, showing this perspective enables direct treatment of Dirac delta terms through weak-form equation integration. They focus on Radial Basis Function (RBF) expansion (single-layer RBF Network) as an alternative RLS formulation. They use Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) theory to explain their findings. The methods are tested on linear PDEs representing groundwater flow and transport in porous media and rivers.
AI.result: While integrating out Dirac deltas in PINNs causes residuals to fail to converge to zero, RBF-RLS consistently provides good forward and inverse solutions to transport problems. The authors demonstrate this through tests on inverse problems fitting synthetic data, noisy synthetic data, and real-world measurements.
AI.conclusion: The RLS perspective, particularly using RBF expansions, provides a more robust framework for handling Dirac delta terms in PDEs compared to standard PINNs. This approach avoids the modeling errors introduced by smoothing approximations and offers better convergence properties for both forward and inverse problems in transport applications.

title: Otters++: A Time-to-first-spike Based Energy Efficient Optical Spiking Transformer
summary: Spiking neural networks (SNNs) are promising for energy-efficient inference, and time-to-first-spike (TTFS) coding is especially attractive because each neuron fires at most once. In practice, however, this benefit is often reduced by the cost of computing a temporal decay term and multiplying it by the synaptic weight. We address this issue by turning a physical hardware "bug," the natural signal decay in optoelectronic devices, into the main computation of TTFS, named Otters++. Specifically, we use the measured decay of a custom In$_2$O$_3$ optoelectronic synapse to directly realize the TTFS temporal term, removing the need for explicit digital decay computation. To scale this idea to Transformer models, we establish a layer-wise functional equivalence between the Otters++ and a quantized neural network (QNN), and develop a hybrid training method that uses device-faithful SNN computation in the forward pass and QNN straight-through gradients through the equivalent QNN path in the backward pass, together with model distillation. This avoids differentiation through discrete first-spike events and reduces the over-sparsity problem in direct TTFS-SNN training. We further make training aware of measured device noise by sampling run-to-run variation, and refine the system-level energy model by accounting for device sharing and multi-hop communication. On GLUE dataset, Otters++ improves the average score to 84.17\% while maintaining a clear energy advantage over prior spiking Transformer baselines. These results show that physically grounded TTFS computing can be efficient, trainable, and robust under realistic hardware effects.

AI.tldr: Otters++ is a hardware-software co-design approach that converts natural signal decay in optoelectronic devices into the main computation for time-to-first-spike (TTFS) SNNs, enabling efficient Transformer models with improved accuracy and energy efficiency.
AI.motivation: Traditional TTFS coding in SNNs has computational overhead from explicit digital decay computation and multiplication by synaptic weights, reducing the energy efficiency benefits. There's also difficulty in training TTFS-SNNs directly due to discrete spike events and over-sparsity problems.
AI.method: Leverages natural signal decay in custom In₂O₃ optoelectronic synapses to implement TTFS temporal terms without explicit digital computation. Establishes layer-wise equivalence between Otters++ and quantized neural networks (QNNs), and develops a hybrid training method using device-faithful SNN forward passes with QNN straight-through gradients, model distillation, and device noise sampling.
AI.result: On GLUE dataset, Otters++ achieves 84.17% average score while maintaining clear energy advantage over prior spiking Transformer baselines. The approach shows efficiency, trainability, and robustness under realistic hardware effects.
AI.conclusion: Physically grounded TTFS computing using natural device properties can be made efficient, trainable, and robust, demonstrating the value of hardware-software co-design for energy-efficient spiking neural networks in practical applications.

title: Deep Unfolded Latent Optimally Partitioned-l2/l1 Networks for Data-driven Block-Sparse Recovery
summary: The convex Latent Optimal Partition (LOP)-l2/l1 approach enables block-sparse signal recovery with unknown partitions but relies on manual hyperparameter tuning. Additionally, numerical instability in differentiating its proximal operator prevents its automatic parameter tuning via Deep Unfolding (DU). To address these limitations, we propose two architectures: a stable framework utilizing implicit differentiation and a flexible variant leveraging Deep Weight Factorization (DWF). The DWF-based approach also supports nonconvex smooth data fidelity terms. Numerical experiments demonstrate that DU-LOP-l2/l1 yields competitive performance and high resilience against impulsive noise.

AI.tldr: Deep Unfolding architectures for stable and flexible parameter tuning of convex LOP-l2/l1 block-sparse signal recovery, with competitive performance and robustness to impulsive noise.
AI.motivation: The convex LOP-l2/l1 approach for block-sparse signal recovery has two main limitations: 1) requires manual hyperparameter tuning, and 2) numerical instability in differentiating its proximal operator prevents automatic parameter tuning via Deep Unfolding.
AI.method: Two Deep Unfolding architectures: 1) a stable framework using implicit differentiation to handle the proximal operator, and 2) a flexible variant leveraging Deep Weight Factorization that also supports nonconvex smooth data fidelity terms.
AI.result: Numerical experiments demonstrate that DU-LOP-l2/l1 achieves competitive performance and shows high resilience against impulsive noise.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Deep Unfolding approaches successfully address the limitations of manual hyperparameter tuning and numerical instability in the original LOP-l2/l1 method, enabling automatic parameter optimization while maintaining strong recovery performance and noise robustness.

title: SciR: A Controllable Benchmark for Scientific Reasoning in LLMs
summary: Three paradigmatic forms of inference recur across scientific reasoning: deduction, induction, and causal abduction. Reliably evaluating LLMs on these in scientific settings is currently out of reach: scientific benchmarks built on human annotations are costly and lack mechanistic ground truth, while synthetic logical-reasoning benchmarks do not resemble real scientific documents. We introduce SciR, a benchmark that combines multi-paradigm reasoning with controllable scientific rendering, anchored on three paradigmatic scientific problems. Tasks are generated from formal objects (deduction tree, inductive rule hypothesis, causal graph) to guarantee verifiable answers, then rendered into multi-document scientific discourse via per-track domain-tuned genres. The construction lets us independently vary two difficulty axes: how hard it is to extract the key information needed for inference, and how hard the principled inference itself is. We test six models. Both axes hurt every model, and their effects compound. The rendering even hurts neurosymbolic pipelines, which hand inference to a verified solver. The two axes yield a per-model extraction-vs-inference profile: for instance, reasoning models like deepseek-r1 mostly surpass non-reasoning instruct models on the inference axis. To our knowledge, SciR is the first multi-paradigm scientific-reasoning benchmark with parametric control on both extraction and inference difficulty.

AI.tldr: SciR is a new benchmark combining multi-paradigm scientific reasoning (deduction, induction, abduction) with controllable rendering, featuring parametric control over both information extraction difficulty and principled inference difficulty.
AI.motivation: Current scientific reasoning evaluation has limitations: human-annotated benchmarks are costly and lack mechanistic ground truth, while synthetic logical benchmarks don't resemble real scientific documents. There's no benchmark that combines multi-paradigm reasoning with realistic scientific rendering.
AI.method: Benchmark tasks are generated from formal objects (deduction trees, inductive rule hypotheses, causal graphs) to ensure verifiable answers, then rendered into multi-document scientific discourse using per-track domain-tuned genres. This allows independent variation of extraction difficulty (how hard to find key information) and inference difficulty (how hard the principled reasoning is).
AI.result: Both extraction and inference difficulty axes negatively affect all six tested models, with compounding effects. Rendering complexity even impacts neurosymbolic pipelines that use verified solvers. Models show different extraction-vs-inference profiles: reasoning models like deepseek-r1 outperform non-reasoning instruct models on inference difficulty.
AI.conclusion: SciR is the first multi-paradigm scientific-reasoning benchmark with parametric control over both extraction and inference difficulty, enabling more nuanced evaluation of LLMs' scientific reasoning capabilities across different forms of inference.

title: Adaptive Weighted Averaging
summary: We study the problem of selecting the largest among $n$ unknown values $x_1,\dots,x_n$ given only a single unbiased estimate $y_i$ for each $x_i$. We design strategies that are simultaneously admissible (not uniformly dominated by any other strategy) and also never worse than a given baseline such as uniform random selection. We provide an application to stochastic optimization, where we obtain online-to-batch conversion bounds with a desirable "no-compromise" guarantee: they are never worse than standard random iterate selection, and yet can be significantly better in benign settings.

AI.tldr: Designing strategies to select the largest value among unknown variables using single unbiased estimates, with simultaneous admissibility and guaranteed performance not worse than baseline uniform selection.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the problem of selecting the largest value among n unknown variables when only single unbiased estimates are available for each. This is motivated by practical scenarios where direct access to true values is limited but estimates can be obtained, and the need for strategies that avoid uniformly dominated behavior while maintaining minimum performance guarantees.
AI.method: The authors design selection strategies that are simultaneously admissible (not uniformly dominated by any other strategy) and guarantee performance never worse than a given baseline, such as uniform random selection. They provide theoretical analysis of these strategies and demonstrate their application to stochastic optimization problems.
AI.result: The strategies achieve the "no-compromise" guarantee: they are never worse than standard random iterate selection while potentially being significantly better in benign settings. The paper provides application to stochastic optimization with online-to-batch conversion bounds that maintain this desirable property.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents a principled approach to selection problems under uncertainty, offering strategies with strong theoretical guarantees of both admissibility and baseline performance, with practical applications in stochastic optimization where they provide improved performance without sacrificing worst-case guarantees.

title: Nous: An Attempt to Extract and Inject the Cognition Behind Prediction-Market Behavior
comment: 37 pages, 1 figure, 7 tables. Reproduction artifacts (code, frozen profiles, prompts, model outputs): https://github.com/WillChienT/nous-paper
summary: As LLM agents proliferate in prediction markets and collective decision-making, they risk a cognitive monoculture: agents built on shared foundation models produce correlated forecasts, and recent measurement finds frontier-model errors correlated at r ~ 0.77. We ask whether human cognitive diversity can be recovered from behavior and transferred to LLM agents. Nous extracts a structured eight-dimension behavioral profile from real Polymarket trading activity and injects it into agents through prompts. Our central finding is a dissociation between the two halves of that pipeline. Extraction works, partially: across 100 wallets, 8 of 14 parameters are temporally stable (split-half ICC >= 0.5, bootstrap CI lower bound > 0.3; contrarian score reaches ICC ~ 0.9); wallets are identifiable from their profiles well above chance (top-1 retrieval 17-22% vs. 1% chance); and two of four pre-specified dimensions rank-correlate with future realized profit out-of-sample, though the correlations do not survive behavioral-confound controls. Prompt-level injection does not measurably transmit it: on a semantic embedding metric, structured injection shows no significant advantage over a length-matched control on any model, and the diversity it induces neither reduces ensemble error correlation nor improves Brier score -- a null that persists across exploratory checks on sampling temperature, profile diversity, and question difficulty. Measuring the prompts themselves locates the compression before the model: the structure-to-narrative translator emits near-uniform prompts whose spread does not track profile spread. We position Nous as measuring the cognitive-monoculture problem and the limits of a prompt-level remedy, motivating deeper, below-the-prompt injection (fine-tuning, activation steering). Code, frozen profiles, prompts, and model outputs: https://github.com/WillChienT/nous-paper

AI.tldr: Nous is a system that extracts behavioral profiles from real market trading activity and attempts to inject them into LLM agents to combat cognitive monoculture, but finds that while extraction partially works, prompt-level injection fails to meaningfully transfer human cognitive diversity.
AI.motivation: LLM agents in prediction markets and collective decision-making risk creating a "cognitive monoculture" where agents built on shared foundation models produce correlated forecasts, with frontier-model errors correlated at r ~ 0.77. The paper aims to investigate whether human cognitive diversity can be recovered from behavior and transferred to LLM agents to combat this monoculture.
AI.method: Nous extracts structured eight-dimension behavioral profiles from real Polymarket trading activity and attempts to inject these profiles into LLM agents through prompts. The approach has two main components: (1) behavioral profile extraction from 100 wallets, and (2) prompt-level injection of these profiles into LLM agents. The method includes validation through split-half ICC, wallet identification, and correlation with future profit.
AI.result: Behavioral profile extraction partially works: 8 of 14 parameters show temporal stability, wallets are identifiable from profiles well above chance, and two dimensions correlate with future profit (though not surviving behavioral controls). However, prompt-level injection fails: structured injection shows no significant advantage over controls, doesn't reduce ensemble error correlation or improve Brier scores. The bottleneck is identified as the structure-to-narrative translation step that emits near-uniform prompts not tracking profile diversity.
AI.conclusion: The study successfully measures the cognitive monoculture problem but demonstrates the limits of prompt-level remedies for transferring human cognitive diversity to LLM agents. The null results motivate deeper interventions like fine-tuning or activation steering rather than prompt-level injection, highlighting the need for below-the-prompt approaches to address cognitive monoculture.

title: Detecting Functional Memorization in Code Language Models
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to generate code at scale. Meanwhile, prior work has investigated whether training data may be recoverable from model outputs, by auditing the textual overlap between training examples and model generations. Code, however, can be functionally equivalent while textually dissimilar. In this work, we study functional memorization: extraction of functional logic beyond what verbatim metrics detect. We construct a counterfactual setup for Olmo-3-32B, comparing a midtrained model (exposed to target code) against a pretrained reference (not exposed). We prompt both models with Python function signatures and measure both textual and functional similarity (i.e., LLM-as-a-judge, execution-based). Our results show clear evidence of functional memorization, highlighting the need for auditing metrics that go beyond textual overlap.

AI.tldr: Researchers investigate "functional memorization" in LLMs - extraction of functional logic that goes beyond textual overlap detection, showing that code can be functionally equivalent while textually dissimilar.
AI.motivation: While LLMs are increasingly used for code generation at scale, prior work on training data recovery has focused on textual overlap between training examples and model outputs. However, code presents a unique challenge: it can be functionally equivalent while being textually dissimilar, meaning current textual overlap metrics may miss important forms of memorization.
AI.method: Researchers create a counterfactual setup using Olmo-3-32B, comparing a midtrained model (exposed to target code) against a pretrained reference (not exposed). They prompt both models with Python function signatures and measure both textual similarity and functional similarity using two approaches: LLM-as-a-judge evaluation and execution-based testing.
AI.result: The results show clear evidence of functional memorization - the models are able to extract and reproduce functional logic from training data that goes beyond what verbatim textual metrics would detect.
AI.conclusion: The findings highlight the need for more sophisticated auditing metrics that go beyond simple textual overlap when assessing memorization in code-generating LLMs, as functional equivalence represents a distinct and potentially significant form of training data extraction.

title: Augmentation techniques for video surveillance in the visible and thermal spectral range
summary: In intelligent video surveillance, cameras record image sequences during day and night. Commonly, this demands different sensors. To achieve a better performance it is not unusual to combine them. We focus on the case that a long-wave infrared camera records continuously and in addition to this, another camera records in the visible spectral range during daytime and an intelligent algorithm supervises the picked up imagery. More accurate, our task is multispectral CNN-based object detection. At first glance, images originating from the visible spectral range differ between thermal infrared ones in the presence of color and distinct texture information on the one hand and in not containing information about thermal radiation that emits from objects on the other hand. Although color can provide valuable information for classification tasks, effects such as varying illumination and specialties of different sensors still represent significant problems. Anyway, obtaining sufficient and practical thermal infrared datasets for training a deep neural network poses still a challenge. That is the reason why training with the help of data from the visible spectral range could be advantageous, particularly if the data, which has to be evaluated contains both visible and infrared data. However, there is no clear evidence of how strongly variations in thermal radiation, shape, or color information influence classification accuracy. To gain deeper insight into how Convolutional Neural Networks make decisions and what they learn from different sensor input data, we investigate the suitability and robustness of different augmentation techniques...

AI.tldr: Multispectral CNN-based object detection combining visible and thermal infrared cameras, investigating how different sensor inputs affect classification accuracy through augmentation techniques.
AI.motivation: Intelligent video surveillance requires different sensors for day/night operation. Combining visible and thermal infrared cameras improves performance but faces challenges: visible images contain color/texture but suffer from illumination variations, while thermal data captures radiation but lacks large training datasets. Understanding how CNNs learn from different sensor inputs is crucial.
AI.method: Multispectral CNN-based object detection approach using both visible spectral range cameras (daytime) and long-wave infrared cameras (continuous). Investigates suitability and robustness of different augmentation techniques to understand how CNNs make decisions and learn from different sensor input data.
AI.result: The paper investigates but does not present specific results in the abstract. Focuses on understanding the influence of thermal radiation, shape, and color information variations on classification accuracy through augmentation technique analysis.
AI.conclusion: Training with visible spectral range data could be advantageous for thermal infrared object detection, especially when evaluating both data types. However, understanding how CNNs process different sensor inputs and their robustness to various factors is essential for effective multispectral surveillance systems.

title: ProPlay: Procedural World Models for Self-Evolving LLM Agents
summary: Self-evolving agents are expected to improve through interaction without external supervision, but this remains difficult in partially observable environments where agents must explore actively, learn from limited feedback, and decide when to trust prior experience. Existing LLM-agent methods often rely on memory or planning modules, yet they rarely close the loop between them to continually refine an internal understanding of environment dynamics. We introduce ProPlay, a procedural world model that supports procedure-level preplay, where agents can rehearse future procedural paths using the learned world knowledge. Rather than representing experience as isolated rules or low-level action constraints, ProPlay abstracts successful trajectories into procedures and organizes them in a procedure graph that captures causal transitions among task stages. Each transition is associated with a reliability record embedding to estimate its task-specific contribution from past outcomes. Before each episode, ProPlay simulates future procedural trajectories over known graph structures as structured soft guidance; after execution, it refines the graph using environment feedback. Experiments on public benchmarks show that ProPlay consistently improves environment understanding and self-evolution capability over strong baselines. Our code has been released in https://github.com/antman9914/proplay.

AI.tldr: ProPlay is a procedural world model that enables LLM agents to rehearse future procedural paths using learned world knowledge, abstracting successful trajectories into procedures organized in a graph with reliability embeddings, improving environment understanding and self-evolution in partially observable environments.
