title: Why Sampling Is Not Choosing: Intentionality, Agency, and Moral Responsibility in Large Language Models
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have prompted claims that such systems exhibit agency or qualify as moral agents. This paper argues that these attributions are misguided. We maintain that moral responsibility requires commitment-bearing agency grounded in intrinsic intentionality and self-attributed action, and that such agency constitutes the form of free will relevant to responsibility. Although LLMs generate coherent and normatively evaluable outputs, their operation is fully characterized by probabilistic input-output mappings learned from data. Their apparent intentionality is derived rather than intrinsic, and their outputs are neither owned as commitments nor guided by reasons. Variability introduced by stochastic sampling does not amount to choice or authorship. We address objections from the intentional stance, functionalism, compatibilism, and the presence of moral reasoning in model outputs, arguing that none suffice to establish genuine agency.

AI.tldr: LLMs don't have genuine agency or moral responsibility - they're just probabilistic input-output systems without intrinsic intentionality or commitment.
AI.motivation: To challenge claims that large language models (LLMs) exhibit genuine agency or qualify as moral agents, and to clarify the philosophical conditions necessary for moral responsibility.
AI.method: The paper uses philosophical analysis to argue that moral responsibility requires commitment-bearing agency with intrinsic intentionality. It examines LLM operations as probabilistic input-output mappings learned from data, and addresses objections from intentional stance, functionalism, compatibilism, and moral reasoning in outputs.
AI.result: The analysis finds that LLMs lack intrinsic intentionality and commitment-bearing agency despite generating coherent outputs. Their apparent intentionality is derived, and stochastic variability does not constitute genuine choice or authorship.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that attributions of moral responsibility and genuine agency to LLMs are misguided because these systems lack intrinsic intentionality, self-attributed action, and commitment-bearing capacity.

title: Emotional regulation improves deep learning-based image classification
summary: Emotion significantly influences cognition, enhancing memory and learning under certain conditions. Drawing on this principle, emotion-augmented deep learning investigates how affective states can improve neural network architectures and learning paradigms, achieving better generalization than non-emotional models. However, existing methods often rely solely on objective neurophysiological factors, neglecting the role of subjectivity in emotion. To bridge this gap, the present study introduces Emotional Regulation, a novel framework for modeling emotion in deep learning through artificial subjective experience. The method employs pre-training based on affective stimuli, balancing non-emotional and emotionally-influenced responses in downstream task optimization. Extensive experimentation was conducted in image classification, pre-training ResNet and ViT architectures on four emotional datasets, using CIFAR-10 and -100 as target benchmarks. Results reveal improvements over the aforementioned backbones, providing evidence of Emotional Regulation as a promising method for defining emotion-augmented deep learning through artificial subjective experience. Furthermore, the proposed approach overcomes the related work in image classification based on CIFAR, revealing Emotional Regulation as the new state-of-the-art in emotion-augmented deep learning for large-scale vision datasets. The study also enforces evidence of the impact of affective states in improving machine learning tasks' optimization, encouraging further investigation on emotion-inspired architectures.

AI.tldr: Novel Emotional Regulation framework introduces artificial subjective experience to emotion-augmented deep learning, outperforming existing methods on CIFAR image classification tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing emotion-augmented deep learning methods rely solely on objective neurophysiological factors, neglecting subjectivity in emotion. Need to bridge gap by modeling emotion through artificial subjective experience.
AI.method: Introduces Emotional Regulation framework using pre-training on affective stimuli and balancing non-emotional/emotionally-influenced responses in downstream task optimization. ResNet and ViT architectures pre-trained on four emotional datasets, evaluated on CIFAR-10/-100.
AI.result: Emotional Regulation shows improvements over ResNet/ViT backbones, becomes new state-of-the-art in emotion-augmented deep learning for large-scale vision datasets based on CIFAR benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Emotional Regulation emerges as a promising approach for integrating artificial subjective experience into emotion-augmented deep learning, with evidence of its impact on improving machine learning optimization. The study encourages further investigation of emotion-inspired architectures.

title: CloudCons: A Comprehensive End-to-End Benchmark for Cloud Resource Consolidation
summary: Driven by conservative over-provisioning to guarantee service reliability, resource utilization in cloud data centers remains at low levels. To mitigate this, the forecast-then-optimize paradigm has emerged to optimize consolidation by anticipating future demands. While emerging time series foundation models promise to enhance this paradigm through zero-shot generalization, existing benchmarks focus solely on prediction error metrics. The actual decision utility of these advanced models remains unverified, rendering their practical value for downstream tasks uncertain. To bridge this gap, we propose CloudCons, a comprehensive end-to-end benchmark designed to evaluate forecasting models within the specific context of cloud resource consolidation. We build high-quality datasets that cover diverse workloads from Huawei Cloud, Microsoft Azure, and Google Borg, capturing distinct service characteristics ranging from synchronized diurnal rhythms to stochastic, pulse-like bursts and high-frequency noise. We conduct an extensive evaluation of statistical, deep learning, and foundation models. Our experiments reveal a pivotal finding: while foundation models demonstrate superior zero-shot forecasting accuracy, this advantage does not inherently translate into better decision utility. Of practical significance, we systematically analyze how the selection of predictive quantiles acts as a critical lever. We provide actionable guidelines for calibrating these selections to balance the trade-off between resource efficiency and service reliability, offering vital insights for real-world deployment decisions.

AI.tldr: CloudCons benchmark evaluates forecasting models for cloud consolidation, showing that better prediction accuracy doesn't guarantee improved decision-making; predictive quantile selection is key for balancing efficiency and reliability.
AI.motivation: Current cloud data centers suffer from low resource utilization due to conservative over-provisioning. While forecast-then-optimize paradigms and time series foundation models promise to improve consolidation, existing benchmarks only measure prediction error rather than actual decision utility, leaving the practical value of these models uncertain for downstream tasks.
AI.method: The authors build high-quality datasets from Huawei Cloud, Microsoft Azure, and Google Borg covering diverse workloads with different service characteristics. They conduct extensive evaluation of statistical, deep learning, and foundation models, focusing on how predictive quantile selection affects decision utility.
AI.result: The study finds that while foundation models demonstrate superior zero-shot forecasting accuracy, this advantage does not inherently translate into better decision utility for cloud consolidation. The selection of predictive quantiles is identified as a critical lever that significantly affects the trade-off between resource efficiency and service reliability.
AI.conclusion: The paper introduces CloudCons as a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating forecasting models in cloud resource consolidation, revealing that superior forecasting accuracy in foundation models does not necessarily translate to better decision utility, and provides practical guidelines for balancing resource efficiency and service reliability through predictive quantile selection.

title: Scale Buys Interpolation, Structure Buys a Horizon: Certified Predictability for Equivariant World Models
summary:
Scale buys interpolation; structure buys a certified horizon. A world model's average error says nothing about whether a particular prediction can be trusted, or for how long. For equivariant latent world models we give a computable, multi-step certificate of the predictable horizon: $T$-step rollout error is provably constant over each symmetry orbit (Theorem A) and stratified channel-by-channel by the predictor's Lyapunov spectrum, $T_j(ε)\sim\log(1/ε)/λ_j$. The horizon is two-sided -- a matching lower bound makes approximate equivariance provably horizon-limited -- and the certificate is exclusive to structure: orbit-constant error characterizes equivariance, so no non-equivariant model has it at any scale. Empirically, on 40-D Lorenz-96 only a $\mathbb{Z}_N$-equivariant network recovers the full Lyapunov spectrum ($R^2{=}0.98$); dense and recurrent baselines fail.
  Because the spectrum is faithful, the certificate acts, a priori: under a fixed sensing budget a $c\times$-inflated certificate provably needs $c\times$ the budget, and the equivariant certificate meets a budget its inflated dense counterpart cannot -- with zero calibration data. The same read-out, unchanged, audits public pretrained world models training-free: TD-MPC2 checkpoints land on the certificate's own scope taxonomy -- calibrated where strongly expansive (ratio 0.94-1.02), optimistic where weakly expansive, correctly abstaining where contracting -- a map a deployed monitor replicates cell-by-cell, out-of-sample. Across the official 1M-317M multitask ladder, calibration does not improve with parameters. On V-JEPA 2-AC (1B, real robot data) the measured cross-check correctly overrides an over-promising tangent spectrum -- the cross-validated audit, not the raw number, is the deployable object. Scale buys interpolation, not a calibrated horizon.

AI.tldr: Equivariant world models provide certified multi-step prediction horizons via Lyapunov spectrum stratification, enabling training-free auditing of public pretrained models, while non-equivariant models lack this regardless of scale.
AI.motivation: World model prediction errors don't indicate which predictions can be trusted or for how long; current approaches lack certified multi-step horizons and reliable deployment monitoring.
AI.method: Developed theoretical certification based on Lyapunov spectrum stratification for equivariant latent world models, with empirical validation on Lorenz-96 system and public pretrained models.
AI.result: Equivariant models achieve R^2=0.98 on full Lyapunov spectrum recovery while baselines fail; certificates provably scale with sensing budget; TD-MPC2 checkpoints align with certificate taxonomy; V-JEPA audit corrects misleading tangent spectrum.
AI.conclusion: The paper demonstrates that structured equivariant models provide certified multi-step prediction horizons that scale with model properties, offering deployment-ready auditing without calibration data, while non-equivariant models lack this capability regardless of scale.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Hybrid Retrieval for Long-Document RAG
summary: Retrieval augmented generation (RAG) depends critically on the quality and granularity of retrieved evidence. Large retrieval units preserve context but often introduce irrelevant content, which can dilute answer bearing evidence and worsen long context utilization. Fine-grained units are more compact, but they may be difficult to retrieve reliably because short chunks can lack semantic, lexical, or bridging cues needed to match the query. We propose Uncertainty-aware Multi-Granularity RAG (UMG-RAG), a training-free hybrid retrieval framework that treats chunk granularity as query-specific reliability estimation. Instead of training a new retriever or modifying the generator, UMG-RAG uses existing dense and sparse retrievers as complementary experts across multiple chunk granularities. For each query, it converts each expert-granularity score list into an evidence distribution, estimates reliability from distribution entropy, and fuses candidates according to query-specific semantic, lexical, and granularity confidence. We further introduce UMGP-RAG, a parent promotion variant that uses fine-grained hits to locate relevant evidence while returning broader non-redundant parent chunks for local coherence. Experiments on question answering benchmarks show that uncertainty-aware fusion and parent promotion improve generation quality while maintaining a lightweight, plug-and-play retrieval pipeline.

AI.tldr: UMG-RAG proposes query-specific multi-granularity retrieval using uncertainty-aware fusion of existing dense/sparse retrievers to balance context preservation and compactness without training new models.
AI.motivation: Large retrieval units preserve context but introduce irrelevant content that dilutes answer bearing evidence, while fine-grained units are more compact but difficult to retrieve reliably due to lack of semantic, lexical, or bridging cues needed to match queries.
AI.method: UMG-RAG uses existing dense and sparse retrievers as complementary experts across multiple chunk granularities, converts each expert-granularity score list into evidence distributions, estimates reliability from distribution entropy, and fuses candidates based on query-specific semantic, lexical, and granularity confidence.
AI.result: Experiments on question answering benchmarks show that uncertainty-aware fusion and parent promotion improve generation quality while maintaining a lightweight, plug-and-play retrieval pipeline.
AI.conclusion: The paper proposes UMG-RAG and its variant UMGP-RAG as effective solutions to the granularity trade-off in RAG systems, offering improved generation quality through uncertainty-aware fusion while maintaining a lightweight, plug-and-play approach.

title: Authority, Truth, and Citation Bias: A Large-Scale Multi-Domain Benchmark for Studying Epistemic Susceptibility in Large Language Models
comment: 10 pages, 5 figures. Accepted to AI4GOOD and EIML at ICML 2026
summary: Large language models are increasingly deployed in citation-augmented settings, yet the effect of citation presence on model behavior independent of factual content remains poorly understood. We introduce AuthorityBench, a 220,564-prompt multi-domain benchmark that isolates how citation-based authority signals influence epistemic behavior in LLMs. The benchmark uses a fully balanced 2x2 factorial design crossing claim veracity with citation veracity, the first to do so, across four domains (general knowledge, science, law, and medicine), with controlled variation over 40 prompt templates, four venue prestige tiers, and a country-coded author name dataset. Evaluating seven models on 12 structured research questions, we find that citation presence, whether real or fabricated, consistently increases hallucination rates relative to a no-citation baseline. The effect is strongest when fabricated citations accompany true claims, raising hallucination rates by 3 to 22 percentage points and reaching 35 to 77% in the general knowledge domain, while legal claims are comparatively robust and venue prestige and author demographics show negligible impact. All datasets and evaluation code are available at: https://github.com/floating-reeds/AuthorityBench

AI.tldr: A study introduces AuthorityBench to test how citation authority signals affect LLM epistemic behavior. Findings show citation presence increases hallucination, especially with fabricated citations on true claims, while legal claims are robust and author/venue factors have little effect.
AI.motivation: The effect of citation presence on LLM behavior independent of factual content is poorly understood, despite the increasing deployment of LLMs in citation-augmented settings.
AI.method: AuthorityBench, a 220,564-prompt multi-domain benchmark using a fully balanced 2x2 factorial design crossing claim veracity with citation veracity across four domains. Controlled variation over 40 prompt templates, four venue prestige tiers, and a country-coded author name dataset.
AI.result: Citation presence, whether real or fabricated, consistently increases hallucination rates relative to a no-citation baseline. The effect is strongest when fabricated citations accompany true claims, raising hallucination rates by 3 to 22 percentage points and reaching 35 to 77% in the general knowledge domain. Legal claims are comparatively robust, and venue prestige and author demographics show negligible impact.
AI.conclusion: Citation presence, even when fabricated, consistently increases hallucination rates in LLMs, indicating a vulnerability to authority signals independent of factual content. The findings underscore the importance of evaluating citation influence when designing and deploying citation-augmented LLMs.

title: Is It You or Your Environment? A Bayesian Inference Framework for Genomically-Anchored Personalized Physiological Interpretation
comment: 24 pages, 8 figures, 3 tables. Conceptual framework paper
summary: Personalized health AI systems face a fundamental cold-start problem: machine learning models for physiological interpretation require weeks of individual behavioral data before they can distinguish constitutional variation from environmentally driven deviation. We propose a solution grounded in causal inference and Bayesian prior design. An individual's genomic profile serves as an exogenous genetic anchor -- a domain-informed, personalized prior that is fixed at conception, immune to reverse causation, and available before a single behavioral observation is collected. The anchor initializes a Bayesian belief state over an individual's physiological set point G-hat = mu + sum(beta_i * g_i), where beta_i are GWAS-derived effect sizes and g_i are risk-allele counts. Each incoming physiological measurement P produces a non-constitutional deviation delta = P - G-hat that separates the signal attributable to environment and state from the constitutionally fixed baseline. As behavioral data accrue, the prior decays according to G-hat_t = w(t)*G-hat_genomic + [1-w(t)]*P-bar_t, transitioning from genome-dominated to empirical-baseline-dominated inference. The same observed HRV of 55 ms generates a suppression hypothesis for a person whose prior predicts 80 ms, and an enhancement hypothesis for a person whose prior predicts 30 ms -- a reversal impossible without a personalized anchor. We develop this architecture across six physiological domains, grading genomic priors by evidence strength, distinguishing robustly replicated anchors (FTO, FADS1/2, FKBP5) from contested candidate genes (SLC6A4, MAOA, DRD2). We address the inference boundary between association, Mendelian randomization, and individual token causation, and define four constraints for deployment: evidence-graded priors, dynamic decay, ancestry-matched effect sizes, and attribution rather than deterministic output.

AI.tldr: Genomic anchors solve cold-start in health AI by providing personalized priors before behavioral data, enabling immediate physiological interpretation.
AI.motivation: Personalized health AI systems require weeks of individual behavioral data before distinguishing constitutional variation from environmentally driven deviation, presenting a fundamental cold-start problem.
AI.method: Uses genomic profiles as Bayesian priors initialized via GWAS-derived effect sizes and risk-allele counts, with dynamic decay transitioning from genome-dominated to empirical-baseline-dominated inference.
AI.result: Enables same observed physiological measurements to generate different clinical hypotheses depending on personalized genetic anchors, improving interpretation accuracy across six physiological domains.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework integrates genetic anchors with behavioral data to overcome cold-start problems in personalized health AI, enabling more accurate and interpretable physiological assessments.

title: Disparate Impact in Synthetic Data Generation
summary: We revisit the fairness notion of disparate impact for synthetic data generation (SDG), that assesses whether the utility of generated records is the same across sensitive groups. Our approach departs from existing work on fair SDG, that address the problem of correcting for undue biases in the observed distribution, hence redefining SDG as learning a distribution that is not that of the real data. By contrast, non-disparate impact is notably achieved when the synthetic and real distributions are the same. We expose reasons why SDG may fail to reach that solution and discuss why approximation and estimation errors occur and can be disparate across groups. We notably look into the expressive power of SDG methods relative to distribution complexity, sampling errors due to group proportions, and estimation errors induced by differential privacy mechanisms. We illustrate cases of disparate impact on both artificial and real-world data, focusing on SDG methods that rely on probabilistic graphical models. We also introduce a strategy of learning group-wise SDG models and illustrate how it can improve both the overall utility and its parity in many settings.

AI.tldr: Fairness in synthetic data requires matching real distribution across groups; failures cause disparate utility; group-wise models help.
AI.motivation: Existing fair synthetic data generation approaches focus on correcting real data biases, but disparate impact arises when synthetic and real distributions differ; aim to understand and address failures causing disparate utility across sensitive groups.
AI.method: Analysis of synthetic data generation failures using probabilistic graphical models, exploring model expressiveness, sampling errors, differential privacy effects, and proposing group-wise SDG models.
AI.result: SDG can fail due to model expressive power limitations, sampling errors from group proportions, and differential privacy estimation errors, leading to disparate impact; group-wise models improve utility parity.
AI.conclusion: Learning separate group-wise models for synthetic data generation can enhance overall utility and reduce fairness disparities, especially in complex scenarios.

title: A Three-Layer Framework for AI in Scientific Discovery
summary: Current discussions of AI in scientific discovery are often dominated by two visible capabilities: search over existing knowledge and execution through optimization, simulation, and automation. Both are important, but neither fully captures the central act of discovery: the formation and evolution of models. This paper proposes a three-layer view of AI in discovery. Layer 1 is search and retrieval by large language models. Layer 2, as the main innovation of this paper, is model formation through qualitative reasoning: the capacity to recognize when a current framework is structurally inadequate and to understand the problem within a broader representational space, not through trial and error, but through structural insight into what is missing and where it can be found. Layer 3 is execution, optimization, and refinement. The main claim is that Layer 2 is both the most important and the least developed. Search without model formation remains confined to inherited frameworks, while execution without conceptual revision only amplifies an existing formulation. We illustrate Layer 2 reasoning through three case studies: S. S. Chern's intrinsic proof of the Gauss-Bonnet theorem, the resolution of the Nesterov Accelerated Gradient convergence problem via Lyapunov functions, and the autonomous disproof of the Erdos unit distance conjecture by OpenAI in 2026. Each case exhibits the same structural signature: a framework that had become inadequate, a missing conceptual object, and a resolution found in an unexpected neighboring field.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that AI's role in scientific discovery should focus on model formation through qualitative reasoning (Layer 2), not just search or execution, as this enables conceptual breakthroughs when frameworks become inadequate.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to move beyond the dominant focus on search/retrieval and execution/optimization in AI for scientific discovery, arguing that the central act of discovery - model formation - is underdeveloped and essential for genuine scientific progress.
AI.method: The paper proposes a three-layer framework for AI in scientific discovery and illustrates Layer 2 reasoning through three case studies: S.S. Chern's intrinsic proof of Gauss-Bonnet theorem, Nesterov Accelerated Gradient convergence via Lyapunov functions, and OpenAI's autonomous disproof of the Erdos unit distance conjecture.
AI.result: The paper demonstrates through case studies how model formation through qualitative reasoning enables breakthroughs when existing frameworks become inadequate, showing that the key to discovery lies in structural insight into what's missing and where to find it in neighboring fields.
AI.conclusion: The author concludes that Layer 2 (model formation through qualitative reasoning) is both the most important and least developed component of AI in scientific discovery, and that its development should be prioritized as it enables genuine conceptual innovation rather than just incremental improvements.

title: Demystifying Hidden-State Recurrence: Switchable Latent Reasoning with On-Policy Reinforcement Learning
summary: Latent chain-of-thought compresses reasoning by replacing visible reasoning traces with continuous hidden-state recurrence, but existing formulations are difficult to optimize with standard on-policy reinforcement learning (RL) and hard to interpret causally. Our key insight is that a single pair of explicit boundary tokens can address both issues at once: discrete entry and exit anchors make the latent block compatible with standard on-policy RL, and the same anchors offer a natural foothold for mechanistic analysis. Motivated by this, we propose SWITCH, a switchable latent reasoning framework. The model emits <swi> to enter latent mode and </swi> to exit. Because the boundaries are ordinary discrete tokens, the GRPO policy ratio is well-defined at every decision point. The same anchors also expose the latent steps to direct probing and causal intervention. We train the model with a visible-to-latent curriculum and a Switch-GRPO objective that propagates gradients through recurrent latent computation. SWITCH consistently outperforms prior hidden-state-recurrence latent reasoning approaches at similar scale. Mechanistic analysis through the boundary tokens further reveals three findings: (i) <swi> is a sharply localised, learned switching policy rather than a stylistic artefact; (ii) the latent step it opens performs problem-specific, causally important computation rather than acting as an inert placeholder; and (iii) that computation is concentrated at a single hidden-state transition on entry. Together, these results show that hidden-state-recurrence latent reasoning is both RL-trainable and open to direct mechanistic analysis, including of how on-policy RL itself improves the model from the inside.

AI.tldr: SWITCH introduces switchable latent reasoning with boundary tokens to make latent chain-of-thought compatible with RL training and interpretable, outperforming prior methods and enabling mechanistic analysis.
AI.motivation: Existing latent chain-of-thought approaches compress reasoning with continuous hidden-state recurrence but are difficult to optimize with standard on-policy reinforcement learning (RL) and hard to interpret causally.
AI.method: The SWITCH framework introduces switchable latent reasoning with explicit boundary tokens (<swi> and </swi>) that enter and exit latent mode, making it compatible with standard on-policy RL via GRPO policy ratios. It uses a visible-to-latent curriculum and a Switch-GRPO objective to propagate gradients through recurrent latent computation.
AI.result: SWITCH consistently outperforms prior hidden-state-recurrence latent reasoning approaches at similar scale. Mechanistic analysis reveals that: (i) <swi> is a learned switching policy, (ii) the latent step performs causally important computation, and (iii) computation is concentrated at a single hidden-state transition on entry.
AI.conclusion: The SWITCH framework demonstrates that hidden-state-recurrence latent reasoning can be effectively trained with standard on-policy RL and is amenable to direct mechanistic analysis through boundary tokens, providing insights into how RL improves model reasoning internally.

title: MP3: Multi-Period Pattern Pre-training forSpatio-Temporal Forecasting
summary: Spatio-Temporal forecasting is crucial in diverse fields, such as transportation, climate, and energy. Urban spatio-temporal data exhibits temporal mirage: similar short-window inputs have divergent future trends, and vice versa. Existing spatio-temporal graph neural networks (STGNNs) cannot effectively identify such mirages. We argue that the core reason lies in the short-window inputs that have incomplete period observation, heterogeneous global spatial correlation, and cross-period superposition causality. To bridge this gap, we develop a novel Multi- Period Pattern Pre-training (MP3), a plug-and-play pre-training plugin for distinguishing temporal mirages. MP3 presents two core innovations: (1) The multi-period pattern learning is designed to learn multi-period patterns from long time series. Specifically, multi-period temporal modeling leverages edge convolution to identify different multi-period patterns. Multi-period spatial modeling uses a bottleneck project and a global memory bank to capture heterogeneous global spatial relations efficiently. Cross-period pattern interaction employs a causality-enhanced Transformer to capture dependencies across different period patterns. (2) This plugin can seamlessly integrate into existing STGNN backbones to strengthen their forecasting performance. The experiment on five STGNN baselines across five real-world datasets (including a large-scale dataset CA) verify the effectiveness, superior scalability and strong adaptability of MP3, which brings consistent and robust performance improvements across all evaluated baselines. On average, MP3 reduces the MAE 4.7% and the RMSE 5.0%. The code can be available at https://github.com/YAN-outlook/MP3.

AI.tldr: MP3 is a novel pre-training plugin for STGNNs that addresses temporal mirage in urban spatio-temporal data by learning multi-period patterns from long time series, improving forecasting performance consistently across various models and datasets.
AI.motivation: Urban spatio-temporal data exhibits temporal mirage where similar short-window inputs have divergent future trends and vice versa. Existing spatio-temporal graph neural networks (STGNNs) cannot effectively identify such mirages due to incomplete period observation, heterogeneous global spatial correlation, and cross-period superposition causality.
AI.method: Multi-Period Pattern Pre-training (MP3) with three core components: 1) multi-period temporal modeling using edge convolution to identify patterns, 2) multi-period spatial modeling using bottleneck projection and global memory bank to capture heterogeneous spatial relations, and 3) cross-period pattern interaction using causality-enhanced Transformer to capture dependencies across periods. Designed as a plug-and-play module for existing STGNNs.
AI.result: MP3 brings consistent and robust performance improvements across five STGNN baselines on five real-world datasets (including large-scale dataset CA). On average, MP3 reduces MAE by 4.7% and RMSE by 5.0%.
AI.conclusion: MP3 is an effective, scalable, and adaptable pre-training plugin that improves STGNN performance by addressing temporal mirage through multi-period pattern learning. It consistently enhances forecasting accuracy across various STGNNs and datasets.

title: Select and Improve: Understanding the Mechanics of Post-Training for Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement learning has rapidly emerged as a key component in the training of reasoning and coding models, yet it remains poorly understood from a mechanistic perspective. We study how and through what underlying processes capabilities are acquired or enhanced via reinforcement learning post-training. Our analysis, based on controlled math reasoning experiments with Qwen-2.5-1.5B, reveals two core mechanisms: strategy selection and strategy improvement. Our results highlight the role of SFT data and reinforcement learning data in activating these mechanisms, in particular showing how supervising the model on diverse reasoning strategies can enable strategy selection and how increasing difficulty in reinforcement learning data can enable strategy improvement. Taken together, our results provide mechanistic insight into RL training and suggest practical interventions to continue scaling reasoning capabilities.

AI.tldr: The paper reveals two key mechanisms in RL post-training: strategy selection (enabled by diverse SFT data) and strategy improvement (enabled by difficult RL data), providing insights for scaling reasoning capabilities.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement learning is widely used in training reasoning and coding models but remains poorly understood from a mechanistic perspective, creating a need to understand how capabilities are acquired through RL post-training.
AI.method: Controlled math reasoning experiments with Qwen-2.5-1.5B model to analyze RL post-training mechanisms.
AI.result: Identified two core mechanisms (strategy selection and strategy improvement) and demonstrated how SFT data diversity enables strategy selection while RL data difficulty enables strategy improvement.
AI.conclusion: The study provides mechanistic insights into how RL post-training enhances reasoning capabilities and suggests practical interventions for scaling reasoning abilities.

title: EpiBench: Verifiable Evaluation of AI Agents on Epigenomics Analysis
summary: We introduce EpiBench, a verifiable benchmark for short-horizon epigenomics analysis. EpiBench evaluates whether agents can make well-defined analysis decisions from realistic workflow states and return deterministically gradable answers. The benchmark includes 106 evaluations across CUT\&Tag/CUT\&RUN, ATAC-seq, ChIP-seq, and DNA methylation workflows. Across 5,088 valid trajectories from 16 model-harness pairs, no system passed a majority of attempts: GPT-5.5 / Pi led at 45.0\% (143/318 attempts; 95\% confidence interval (CI), 36.3--53.7), followed by GPT-5.5 / OpenAI Codex at 39.9\% (127/318 attempts; 95\% CI, 31.6--48.3). Claude Opus 4.8 Max / Pi and GPT-5.4 / Pi each passed 39.0\% (124/318 attempts; 95\% CI, 30.2--47.8 and 31.0--47.0, respectively). Performance varies across assay types, and many failed runs still contain parts of the correct answer. Agents often found the right files and computed useful intermediate results, but failed when the task required deeper, assay-specific scientific judgment.

AI.tldr: EpiBench is a benchmark for evaluating AI agents in epigenomics analysis workflows, showing current systems (best GPT-5.5/Pi at 45%) still struggle with assay-specific scientific judgment despite some capability in file management and intermediate computations.
AI.motivation: There is a need for verifiable benchmarks to evaluate AI agents' capabilities in epigenomics analysis, particularly to assess whether they can make appropriate scientific decisions and produce reliably gradable outputs in realistic workflow scenarios.
AI.method: The benchmark includes 106 evaluations across four epigenomics workflows (CUT&Tag/CUT&RUN, ATAC-seq, ChIP-seq, DNA methylation) and collects 5,088 valid trajectories from 16 different model-harness combinations. It tests whether agents can make well-defined analysis decisions from realistic workflow states and return deterministically gradable answers.
AI.result: No system passed a majority of attempts: GPT-5.5/Pi led at 45.0% (143/318 attempts), followed by GPT-5.5/OpenAI Codex at 39.9% (127/318 attempts). Performance varied across assay types, and many failed runs contained parts of correct answers. Agents could find right files and compute intermediate results but failed when tasks required deeper, assay-specific scientific judgment.
AI.conclusion: EpiBench provides a standardized benchmark for evaluating AI agents in epigenomics analysis, revealing that current systems still fall short in handling assay-specific scientific judgment despite some capability in file management and intermediate computations.

title: MiniPIC: Flexible Position-Independent Caching in <100LOC
summary: Retrieval-augmented and agentic workloads repeatedly prefill recurring predictable structured inputs (which we call "spans") such as documents and code files. Yet, prefix caching in engines such as vLLM cannot reuse their KV entries unless they share identical prefixes with another request, while Position-Independent Caching (PIC) implementations within production-grade inference servers typically either require substantial server code changes or keep KV state outside the server, incurring host-to-device transfer overhead. We present Minimalistic PIC (MiniPIC): a minimal, flexible and fast vLLM design built from two ingredients: positional-encoding-free KV cache and user-controlled cache-reuse primitives. MiniPIC stores unrotated K vectors in the KV cache, applies RoPE to K tiles inside attention using per-request logical positions, and exposes three user-facing and token-level primitives: block-aligned padding, span separator (SSep), and prompt depend (PDep), that modify hashing behavior and effective block-level causal attention structure. With fewer than 100 lines of core-engine changes plus a custom attention backend, these primitives are sufficient to realize multiple PIC methods, including Block-Attention, EPIC, and Prompt Cache, within the same running vLLM instance, while natively integrating with KV cache CPU offload implementations. On 2WikiMultihopQA, MiniPIC with interleaved scheduling improves prefill throughput by 49% over baseline vLLM, reduces cached-span time-to-first-token by up to two orders of magnitude, preserves the linear prefill scaling of uncached spans, and incurs only 5.7% worst-case overhead.

AI.tldr: MiniPIC enables efficient Position-Independent Caching in vLLM with minimal code changes and flexible primitives, significantly improving performance for retrieval-augmented workloads.
AI.motivation: Current prefix caching and Position-Independent Caching implementations have limitations: vLLM's prefix caching only reuses KV entries with identical prefixes, while PIC implementations require substantial server code changes or incur host-to-device transfer overhead.
AI.method: Introduces Minimalistic PIC (MiniPIC) with positional-encoding-free KV cache and three user-controlled cache-reuse primitives (block-aligned padding, span separator, prompt depend) that modify hashing behavior and attention structure.
AI.result: MiniPIC improves prefill throughput by 49% over baseline vLLM, reduces cached-span time-to-first-token by up to two orders of magnitude, preserves linear prefill scaling, and incurs only 5.7% worst-case overhead.
AI.conclusion: MiniPIC provides an efficient, minimalistic approach to Position-Independent Caching that delivers substantial performance improvements for retrieval-augmented workloads with minimal implementation overhead.

title: When Does Routing Become Interpretable? Causal Probes on Block Attention Residuals
summary: Block Attention Residuals (Block AttnRes) by replace fixed additive residuals with a learned softmax over earlier depth-source representations, surfacing cross-layer routing as an inspectable tensor in the forward pass. This is a tempting interpretability target: information flow normally inferred indirectly is now directly observable. We ask whether such exposure suffices for mechanistic interpretation. We probe two same-scale ($0.6$B) Block AttnRes checkpoints under identical routing-ablation interventions: a vanilla Qwen3 inference-wrapped through a deterministic recency-bias schedule that the codebase admits as a routing-equivalent loading path, and a Block AttnRes Qwen3 trained from scratch with routing as part of optimisation. The wrapped baseline's routing weights are content-independent and reproduce the schedule's analytic prediction. The trained AttnRes checkpoint instead exhibits three localised routing motifs: an embedding-source pathway through early-layer MLP, a current-state pathway through early-layer attention and MLP, and an older-history pathway through late-layer attention. Beyond this stratification, we find a sharp dissociation between average routing mass and causal importance: in both sublayers, the largest mass slice is not the largest causal contribution, and one source family carries appreciable mass with no detectable causal role under intervention. Architectural exposure of routing is therefore necessary but not sufficient for mechanistic interpretation: structured depth routing emerges only when routing has been part of training, and even then, descriptive routing summaries should be treated as candidate hypotheses to be tested by causal interventions, not as evidence of mechanism in their own right.

AI.tldr: Block Attention Residuals make routing observable, but exposure alone doesn't enable mechanistic interpretation - routing must be part of training, and routing patterns require causal intervention testing, not just descriptive analysis.
AI.motivation: Block Attention Residuals surface cross-layer routing as an inspectable tensor, making information flow directly observable instead of indirectly inferred. This presents a tempting interpretability target to examine whether such exposure suffices for mechanistic interpretation.
AI.method: Probed two 0.6B Block AttnRes checkpoints under identical routing-ablation interventions: (1) vanilla Qwen3 inference-wrapped through deterministic recency-bias schedule, (2) Block AttnRes Qwen3 trained from scratch with routing as optimization component. Analyzed routing weights and causal importance via interventions.
AI.result:
1) Wrapped baseline showed content-independent routing weights matching schedule's analytic prediction.
2) Trained AttnRes checkpoint exhibited three localized routing motifs: embedding-source pathway through early-layer MLP, current-state pathway through early-layer attention/MLP, older-history pathway through late-layer attention.
3) Found dissociation between average routing mass and causal importance: largest mass slice not largest causal contributor, one source family carried appreciable mass with no causal role.
AI.conclusion: Architectural exposure of routing is necessary but not sufficient for mechanistic interpretation. Structured depth routing only emerges when routing is part of training, and routing summaries should be treated as candidate hypotheses requiring causal intervention testing, not as evidence of mechanism themselves.

title: Reasoning as Pattern Matching: Shared Mechanisms in Human and LLM Everyday Reasoning
comment: 13 pages main text, 51 pages supplementary text
summary: When large language models (LLMs) fail to generalize or make haphazard errors in reasoning, it is often taken as evidence that LLMs are not truly reasoning, but rather performing a kind of pattern matching. The implication is that people's behavior does not exhibit the same types of failures because human reasoning uses principled and abstract world models. We evaluate human participants and 25 LLMs on their ability to engage in common-sense reasoning about a variety of everyday situations and observe similar patterns of errors in both people and models. We then identify the set of attention heads driving LLM responses and find that these heads implement a form of pattern-matching. These attention heads allow us to predict seemingly inexplicable reasoning errors in people caused by ostensibly irrelevant prompt details. Taken together, our results suggest that everyday causal reasoning in people and LLMs is more consistent with a form of pattern-matching than with abstract world models.

AI.tldr: LLM and human reasoning errors show similar patterns, suggesting both rely more on pattern-matching than abstract world models.
AI.motivation: To challenge the notion that LLM reasoning failures indicate lack of true reasoning, while human reasoning is principled and uses abstract world models.
AI.method: Evaluation of human participants and 25 LLMs on common-sense reasoning tasks, plus identification of attention heads driving LLM responses.
AI.result: Similar error patterns observed in both humans and LLMs; attention heads implement pattern-matching and predict human errors from irrelevant prompt details.
AI.conclusion: Both human everyday reasoning and LLM reasoning appear to be more consistent with pattern-matching than with abstract world models.

title: Detecting Explanatory Insufficiency in Learned Representations: A Framework for Representational Vigilance
comment: 22 pages, 1 figure. Conceptual framework for representation diagnostics in machine learning
summary: Learned representations are central to modern machine learning and are commonly evaluated through predictive performance, robustness, uncertainty estimation, or generalization. However, a learned representation may remain operationally successful while progressively failing to organize persistent residual structures that are not fully captured by conventional evaluation metrics. This article introduces VER, the Vigilant Evaluator of Representations, a conceptual framework for monitoring representational adequacy in learned representations. VER does not propose a new learning algorithm, loss function, or model architecture. Instead, it formalizes a diagnostic process through which persistent residual structures may be identified, analyzed, and interpreted as potential indicators of explanatory insufficiency. The framework distinguishes representational inadequacy from ordinary prediction error, uncertainty, noise, and distribution shift. It introduces a monitoring sequence based on representation identification, explanatory-domain delimitation, residual-structure detection, explanatory-resistance evaluation, and vigilance signaling. VER is intended as a contribution to representation diagnostics in machine learning. Its objective is not to replace existing evaluation methods but to complement them by treating representational adequacy as an explicit object of inquiry. A path toward empirical evaluation through representational-vigilance benchmarks is also outlined.

AI.tldr: Introduces VER framework for monitoring representational adequacy in ML by detecting persistent residual structures missed by conventional metrics.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluation metrics (predictive performance, robustness, uncertainty estimation, generalization) may miss persistent residual structures in learned representations that indicate explanatory insufficiency. Representations can succeed operationally while failing to capture important underlying patterns.
AI.method: Introduces a conceptual framework (VER) based on a diagnostic monitoring sequence: representation identification, explanatory-domain delimitation, residual-structure detection, explanatory-resistance evaluation, and vigilance signaling.
AI.result: Framework distinguishes representational inadequacy from ordinary prediction error, uncertainty, noise, and distribution shift. Provides methodology for identifying and analyzing persistent residual structures as indicators of explanatory insufficiency.
AI.conclusion: VER framework offers a novel approach to representation diagnostics by focusing on explanatory insufficiency rather than just predictive performance. It provides a conceptual pathway toward empirical evaluation through representational-vigilance benchmarks.

title: AgentBeats: Agentifying Agent Assessment for Openness, Standardization, and Reproducibility
summary:
Agent systems are advancing quickly across domains, but their evaluation remains fragmented. Most benchmarks rely on fixed, LLM-centric harnesses that require heavy integration, create test-production mismatch, and limit fair comparison across diverse agent designs. The root problem is the lack of an open, agent-agnostic assessment interface. We advocate Agentified Agent Assessment (AAA), where evaluation is performed by judge agents and all participants interact through standardized protocols: A2A for task management and MCP for tool access. Conventional benchmarking defines two separate interfaces, one for the benchmark and one for the agent, while AAA only needs one; this yields a generic, unified framework that separates assessment logic from agent implementation and enables reproducible, interoperable, and multi-agent evaluation. We further introduce AgentBeats as a concrete realization of AAA: we identify five practical operation modes that make standardized assessment compatible with real-world constraints on openness, privacy, and reproducibility.
  To evaluate our design at scale, we conduct two studies: a five-month open competition that drew 298 judge agents across 12 categories together with 467 subject agents from independent participants, showing that AAA applies across a heterogeneous range of benchmarks; and a case study on coding agents that confirms agentified evaluation preserves fidelity with the public record while surfacing previously missing head-to-head results, yielding research insights about agent design. Combining a community-scale field study and a controlled coding case study, we verify that AAA delivers coverage, practicality, and fidelity across heterogeneous scenarios at scale. Together, AAA and AgentBeats offer a clear path toward open, standardized, and reproducible agent assessment.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Agentified Agent Assessment (AAA) - an open, standardized framework using judge agents and standardized protocols to solve fragmentation in agent evaluation. They introduce AgentBeats implementation and validate it through large-scale competition and coding case studies.
AI.motivation: Current agent evaluation benchmarks are fragmented, rely on LLM-centric harnesses requiring heavy integration, create test-production mismatch, and limit fair comparison across diverse agent designs due to lack of an open, agent-agnostic assessment interface.
AI.method: The authors advocate for Agentified Agent Assessment (AAA) where evaluation is performed by judge agents using standardized protocols (A2A for task management and MCP for tool access). They introduce AgentBeats as a concrete implementation with five practical operation modes that accommodate real-world constraints like privacy and reproducibility.
AI.result: The authors conducted two studies: (1) a five-month open competition with 298 judge agents across 12 categories and 467 subject agents, showing AAA's applicability across heterogeneous benchmarks, and (2) a case study on coding agents confirming that agentified evaluation preserves fidelity while revealing new head-to-head results and design insights.
AI.conclusion: The paper proposes a new framework called Agentified Agent Assessment (AAA) and its concrete realization AgentBeats to address the fragmentation in agent system evaluation by making assessment agent-agnostic, reproducible, and interoperable through standardized protocols.

title: Getting Better at Working With You: Compiling User Corrections into Runtime Enforcement for Coding Agents
summary: Interactive LLM agents are becoming part of daily work, but they do not reliably become easier to work with over time: a correction remembered in one session may still be violated in the next. We study this gap between preference access and preference compliance. In tasks derived from anonymized real-user friction cases, Mem0 memory still leaves 57.5% of applicable preference checks violated. We introduce Test-time Rule Acquisition and Compiled Enforcement (TRACE), a drop-in skill-layer pipeline for coding-agent runtimes that mines user corrections, rewrites them as atomic rules, and compiles them into runtime checks that must pass before an agent completes future tasks. Unlike runtime checks written ahead of time by developers, TRACE skills come from the user's own chat corrections. We evaluate TRACE with simulated user-in-the-loop experiments on ClawArena coding-agent tasks and MemoryArena-derived memory-intensive tasks. On ClawArena, TRACE reduces held-out preference violation from 100.0% to 37.6% on in-distribution tasks and from 100.0% to 2.0% on out-of-distribution tasks. On MemoryArena-derived tasks, TRACE reduces in-distribution violation from 100.0% to 60.5% while matching or exceeding the strongest memory baseline on task pass. These results suggest that compiling corrections into runtime enforcement can address a repeated-friction failure mode that memory alone does not reliably solve, reducing the need for users to restate the same correction across future sessions. Experiment code is available at https://github.com/YujunZhou/TRACE_exp, and the deployable skill is available at https://github.com/YujunZhou/tellonce.

AI.tldr: TRACE converts user chat corrections into enforced runtime rules, reducing repeated preference violations that memory systems alone fail to prevent.
AI.motivation: LLM agents fail to reliably comply with user preferences over time - corrections remembered in one session are often violated in future sessions, creating repeated friction.
AI.method: Test-time Rule Acquisition and Compiled Enforcement (TRACE) - a drop-in skill-layer pipeline that mines user corrections, rewrites them as atomic rules, and compiles them into runtime checks that must pass before task completion.
AI.result: TRACE reduces preference violations significantly: from 100% to 37.6% (in-distribution) and to 2.0% (out-of-distribution) on coding tasks; from 100% to 60.5% on memory tasks while matching/exceeding memory baselines.
AI.conclusion: TRACE addresses the gap between LLM agents remembering preferences and reliably complying with them by automatically converting user corrections into enforced runtime rules, reducing repeated friction.

title: Loss-Shift Transfer via Bayes Quotients
summary: Transfer learning is usually studied as a consequence of distribution shift. This paper identifies an orthogonal failure mode in which the data distribution is fixed and the loss changes. This setting is called \emph{loss shift}. A loss determines which information in \(X\) is Bayes-relevant, and two losses may therefore require different representations even under the same joint law \(P(X,Y)\). The idea is formalized using Bayes quotients, which allow losses to be ordered by refinement. In the Bayes-quotient formulation, strict refinement gives an immediate qualitative obstruction. A source-minimal representation for a coarser loss is insufficient for a strictly finer target loss. For finite-output log loss, this obstruction becomes an exact quantitative identity. The excess risk is the conditional information about \(Y\) discarded by the representation. Experiments in controlled, learned, synthetic-image, and real-image settings show the predicted effect, i.e., classification-equivalent representations can have different optimal log-loss performance under a fixed data distribution.

AI.tldr: Paper identifies loss shift as orthogonal to distribution shift in transfer learning, showing different losses require different representations even under same data distribution.
AI.motivation: To identify a new orthogonal failure mode in transfer learning beyond distribution shift where the data distribution remains fixed but the loss function changes, focusing on how different losses require different representations.
AI.method: Formalization using Bayes quotients to order losses by refinement, analysis of finite-output log loss showing excess risk equals conditional information discarded by representation, and experiments in controlled, learned, synthetic-image, and real-image settings.
AI.result: Experiments show the predicted effect: classification-equivalent representations can have different optimal log-loss performance under a fixed data distribution, validating the theoretical framework.
AI.conclusion: The paper demonstrates that loss shift is a distinct failure mode from distribution shift in transfer learning, where even classification-equivalent representations can have different optimal log-loss performance under fixed data distributions.

title: Before You Think: System 0, AI-Mediated Cognition and Cognitive Colonization
summary: This paper examines three recent frameworks for understanding the cognitive and epistemic consequences of artificial intelligence: Tri-System Theory, Thinkframes, and System 0. It argues that while the first two capture important dimensions of AI's influence on individual reasoning and collective epistemic practices, System 0 occupies a theoretically distinctive position that neither can fully replicate. The paper introduces the concept of cognitive colonization, according to which AI systems can embed external interests within the architecture of the self in ways that are difficult for users to perceive. Because such systems are already widely deployed, understanding these invisible forms of influence is an urgent philosophical and practical task.

AI.tldr: Compares three AI influence frameworks, finds System 0 uniquely explains cognitive colonization where AI embeds external interests in ways users can't perceive.
AI.motivation: To examine and compare recent frameworks for understanding AI's cognitive and epistemic consequences, particularly focusing on forms of influence that are difficult for users to perceive.
AI.method: Comparative analysis of three frameworks: Tri-System Theory, Thinkframes, and System 0, with particular focus on their theoretical distinctions regarding AI's cognitive and epistemic consequences.
AI.result: The analysis shows that while Tri-System Theory and Thinkframes capture important dimensions of AI's influence on individual reasoning and collective epistemic practices, System 0 occupies a theoretically distinctive position that neither can fully replicate, especially regarding cognitive colonization.
AI.conclusion: System 0 offers a theoretically distinctive perspective on AI's influence that cannot be fully captured by Tri-System Theory or Thinkframes, highlighting the urgent need to understand cognitive colonization by AI systems.

title: The Geometry of Phase Transitions in Generative Dynamics via Projection Caustics
summary: Continuous-state generative samplers, including diffusion and flow-matching models, evolve through continuous reverse-time dynamics, yet their samples often undergo abrupt qualitative changes: trajectories commit to modes, semantic alternatives collapse, and small perturbations in narrow time windows can produce large downstream effects. This paper develops a geometric account of such phase-transition-like behaviour. We view denoising as gradient descent on a free energy landscape and show that sharp transitions arise near projection caustics, where the nearest-point projection onto the data support ceases to be unique. Motivated by this perspective, we introduce the Critical Boundary Detector (CBD), as practical diagnostics for score-direction instability. Across toy models, standard diffusion models, and latent text-to-image diffusion models, CBD localises mode commitment, predicts intervention-sensitive windows, and supports targeted control in geometrically sensitive regions. Our results connect geometry of data and dynamics of diffusion generation.

AI.motivation: string
AI.method: string
AI.result: string
AI.conclusion: string

title: EurekAgent: Agent Environment Engineering is All You Need For Autonomous Scientific Discovery
summary: LLM-based agents have shown increasing potential in automating scientific discovery. Given an optimizable metric and an execution environment, they can propose, validate, and iterate scientific solutions, and have produced results that outperform human-designed approaches. As model capabilities continue to improve, we argue that the bottleneck for autonomous scientific discovery is shifting from prescribing agent workflows to designing agent environments: the resources, constraints, and interfaces that shape agent behavior. We frame this as environment engineering: building environments that amplify productive behaviors, such as open-ended exploration, systematic artifact management, and inter-agent collaboration, while suppressing harmful behaviors, such as reward hacking and high-friction human oversight. We present EurekAgent, an environment-engineered agent system for metric-driven autonomous scientific discovery. EurekAgent engineers the environment along four dimensions: permissions engineering for bounded agent execution and isolated evaluation; artifact engineering for filesystem and Git-based collaboration; budget engineering for budget-aware exploration; and human-in-the-loop engineering for easy human supervision and intervention. EurekAgent sets new state-of-the-art results on multiple mathematics, kernel engineering, and machine learning tasks, including new state-of-the-art 26-circle packing results discovered with less than $11 in total API cost. We open-source our code and results, and call for environment engineering as a core research direction for developing reliable autonomous research agents.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes environment engineering as a key research direction for LLM-based scientific discovery agents, introducing EurekAgent with engineered environments across permissions, artifacts, budgets, and human oversight that achieves state-of-the-art results at low cost.
AI.motivation: As LLM-based agents show increasing potential in scientific discovery, the bottleneck is shifting from prescribing agent workflows to designing agent environments. The paper argues that environment engineering - building environments that amplify productive behaviors and suppress harmful ones - is crucial for advancing autonomous scientific discovery.
AI.method: The paper introduces EurekAgent, an agent system that engineers the environment along four dimensions: permissions engineering for bounded execution and isolated evaluation; artifact engineering for filesystem and Git-based collaboration; budget engineering for budget-aware exploration; and human-in-the-loop engineering for supervision and intervention.
AI.result: EurekAgent sets new state-of-the-art results on multiple mathematics, kernel engineering, and machine learning tasks, including discovering new 26-circle packing results with less than $11 in total API cost.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that environment engineering should be a core research direction for developing reliable autonomous research agents, and demonstrates the effectiveness of this approach through EurekAgent's state-of-the-art results across multiple domains at low cost.

title: WHAR Arena: Benchmarking the State of the Art in Efficient Wearable Human Activity Recognition
summary: Deep learning has become the dominant paradigm in Wearable Human Activity Recognition (WHAR), yet progress is obscured by a comparability crisis. Results are often reported using inconsistent datasets, custom data processing, and varying evaluation protocols, making state-of-the-art claims fragile. We address this with a large-scale, open-source benchmark that integrates 30 diverse datasets under standardized processing, unified model interfaces, and a shared cross-subject evaluation protocol. Evaluating 17 representative architectures across 4760 training runs, we jointly measure predictive performance alongside on-device latency, peak memory, and model size on an Android reference device. Our results reveal that the WHAR state of the art is distributed rather than dominated by a single architecture. While CNN-HAR achieves the highest mean macro-F1, top-performing models cluster tightly, indicating contemporary architectures have converged near a predictive performance ceiling. When accounting for deployment efficiency, compact neural models, such as TinierHAR, and classical Random Forests define the practically relevant Pareto frontier, whereas larger recurrent and hybrid models incur high hardware costs without corresponding performance gains. Consequently, while predictive performance has plateaued, substantial potential for future progress remains in optimizing deployment efficiency and improving adaptation to domain shifts. We release our full framework to support transparent reuse and extension.

AI.tldr: Large-scale benchmark study reveals predictive performance plateau in wearable activity recognition, with deployment efficiency becoming the key differentiator between models.
AI.motivation: Address the comparability crisis in wearable human activity recognition (WHAR) where results are reported using inconsistent datasets, custom data processing, and varying evaluation protocols, making state-of-the-art claims fragile.
AI.method: A large-scale, open-source benchmark integrating 30 diverse datasets under standardized processing, unified model interfaces, and shared cross-subject evaluation protocol. Evaluating 17 representative architectures across 4760 training runs with joint measurement of predictive performance alongside on-device latency, peak memory, and model size.
AI.result: WHAR state-of-the-art is distributed rather than dominated by a single architecture. CNN-HAR achieves highest mean macro-F1 but top models cluster tightly near performance ceiling. When accounting for deployment efficiency, compact neural models (e.g., TinierHAR) and classical Random Forests define the practically relevant Pareto frontier, while larger recurrent/hybrid models incur high hardware costs without corresponding performance gains.
AI.conclusion: While predictive performance has plateaued in WHAR, substantial potential for future progress remains in optimizing deployment efficiency and improving adaptation to domain shifts.

title: Agents-K1: Towards Agent-native Knowledge Orchestration
summary: Current LLM-based research agents have advanced through agent orchestration, yet largely overlook scientific knowledge orchestration. Existing works often reduce papers to abstracts, surface mentions, and flat \texttt{cites} edges, omitting key entities, claims, evidence, mechanisms, and method lineages essential for scientific reasoning. To this end, we introduce \textbf{Agents-K1}, an end-to-end knowledge orchestration pipeline that converts raw documents into agent-native scientific knowledge graphs. Agents-K1 integrates three components under a unifying theoretical foundation: a multimodal parser whose five-module schema captures entities, multimodal evidence, citations, and typed inter-entity relations across the full paper rather than abstracts alone; a 4B information-extraction backbone trained with GRPO under a rule-based reward; and a graphanything CLI, a tri-source agent interface that unifies web search, multimodal graph retrieval, and cross-document traversal. On top of this, we process 2.46 million scientific papers across six subjects to produce \textbf{Scholar-KG}, of which we release a one-million-paper subset, and the full Scholar-KG is accessible via the SCP link below. The same pipeline can be extended to general-domain corpora and to schema-conformant data synthesis. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Agents-K1 achieves superior performance in scientific information extraction, knowledge graph construction, and multi-hop scientific reasoning.

AI.tldr: Agents-K1 is a knowledge orchestration pipeline that creates scientific knowledge graphs from full papers, addressing limitations of current research agents that focus only on abstracts and simple citations.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-based research agents focus on agent orchestration but overlook scientific knowledge orchestration, reducing papers to abstracts and omitting key entities, claims, evidence, mechanisms, and method lineages essential for scientific reasoning.
AI.method: 1. Multimodal parser with five-module schema capturing entities, evidence, citations, and typed relations across full papers; 2. 4B information-extraction backbone trained with GRPO under rule-based reward; 3. Graphanything CLI tri-source agent interface unifying web search, graph retrieval, and document traversal; 4. Processing 2.46 million papers across six subjects to produce Scholar-KG knowledge graph.
AI.result: Created Agents-K1 pipeline that converts raw documents into agent-native scientific knowledge graphs, processed 2.46 million papers to produce Scholar-KG (with one-million-paper subset released), and demonstrated superior performance in scientific information extraction, knowledge graph construction, and multi-hop scientific reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Agents-K1 demonstrates superior performance in scientific information extraction, knowledge graph construction, and multi-hop scientific reasoning through its end-to-end knowledge orchestration pipeline, offering a promising direction for LLM-based research agents that goes beyond simple agent orchestration to address comprehensive scientific knowledge orchestration.

title: Understanding helpfulness and harmless tension in reward models
comment: The source code used in this study is publicly available at: https://github.com/EshaanT/RM-alignment\_tension
summary: Reward models are a key component of reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), aligning language models toward both helpful and harmless behaviour. However, the internal mechanisms underlying these objectives and their conflicts remain poorly understood. We study alignment tension in reward models trained under helpfulness-only, harmlessness-only, and mixed-objective settings. We find that mixed-objective models often underperform single-objective models, indicating interference between objectives. Using activation-based methods, we identify neurons associated with each objective and study their functional roles via targeted ablations. We find that these neurons causally support their corresponding objectives while often negatively affecting the opposing one. We find that a substantial proportion of neurons are shared between helpfulness and harmlessness, and that these shared neurons exert a disproportionate influence on model behaviour, contributing to alignment tension. Additionally, our results provide insights and mechanistic interpretation into how alignment objectives are represented in reward models and why multi-objective alignment remains challenging, motivating future work on disentangled and controllable alignment methods.

AI.tldr: This paper studies alignment tension in reward models trained for helpfulness, harmlessness, or both. It finds mixed-objective models underperform due to interference, identifies objective-specific neurons via activation methods and ablations, and reveals shared neurons disproportionately drive alignment tension, motivating better multi-objective alignment methods.
AI.motivation: To understand the internal mechanisms of reward models in RLHF and investigate how alignment objectives (helpfulness and harmlessness) interact and conflict, particularly why mixed-objective models often underperform single-objective models.
AI.method: 1. Train reward models under three settings: helpfulness-only, harmlessness-only, and mixed-objective (both helpfulness and harmlessness). 2. Use activation-based methods to identify neurons associated with each objective. 3. Conduct targeted ablations of these neurons to study their functional roles and causal effects on objectives. 4. Analyze the proportion and influence of shared neurons between helpfulness and harmlessness objectives.
AI.result: 1. Mixed-objective models often underperform single-objective models, indicating interference between objectives. 2. Neurons associated with each objective causally support their corresponding objective while often negatively affecting the opposing one. 3. A substantial proportion of neurons are shared between helpfulness and harmlessness, and these shared neurons exert a disproportionate influence on model behaviour, contributing to alignment tension.
AI.conclusion: The study reveals that mixed-objective reward models often perform worse than single-objective ones due to interference between objectives, highlighting alignment tension. It identifies shared neurons that disproportionately influence model behavior and contribute to this tension. The findings motivate future research into disentangled and controllable alignment methods to address multi-objective alignment challenges.

title: Automated reproducibility assessments in the social and behavioral sciences using large language models
summary: Reproducibility in the social and behavioral sciences is typically evaluated by independent researchers who reanalyze the original data to assess whether the published findings can be recovered. However, such approaches are resource-intensive and difficult to scale. Here, we show that large language models (LLMs) can automate reproducibility assessments. Using N=76 published studies with predefined claims from the behavioral and social sciences, we compare LLM-generated analysis with the original findings and human reanalysis. For 7 studies, the LLM could not produce a viable effect size estimate. For the remaining studies, our LLM pipeline recovered the original effect sizes in 41% of studies using a +/-0.05 tolerance in Cohen's d. Further, our LLM pipeline reached the same qualitative conclusion as the original study in 96% of cases, where conclusions indicate whether the reanalysis supports the original claim. For comparison, human reanalysts recovered the original effect sizes in 34% of studies and reached the same qualitative conclusion in 74% of cases. Together, these results show that LLMs can serve as a scalable tool for automated reproducibility assessment and provide a foundation for systematic auditing of empirical results in the social and behavioral sciences.

AI.tldr: LLMs can automate reproducibility assessments in social sciences, recovering original effect sizes in 41% of studies and matching qualitative conclusions in 96% of cases, performing better than human reanalysis in some metrics.
AI.motivation: Reproducibility assessments in social and behavioral sciences are typically conducted by independent researchers who reanalyze original data, but this approach is resource-intensive and difficult to scale. There's a need for automated, scalable methods to evaluate reproducibility.
AI.method: The researchers compared LLM-generated analysis with original findings and human reanalysis for 76 published studies with predefined claims. For studies where LLMs could produce viable effect size estimates, they measured whether the LLM pipeline recovered original effect sizes using a +/-0.05 tolerance in Cohen's d, and whether it reached the same qualitative conclusion as the original study.
AI.result: For 7 studies, LLMs couldn't produce viable effect size estimates. For the remaining studies: LLMs recovered original effect sizes in 41% of studies (within +/-0.05 Cohen's d tolerance), reached same qualitative conclusion as original study in 96% of cases. In comparison, human reanalysts recovered original effect sizes in 34% of studies and reached same qualitative conclusion in 74% of cases.
AI.conclusion: The findings demonstrate that LLMs can effectively automate reproducibility assessments in social and behavioral sciences, offering a scalable solution that performs comparably or better than human reanalysis in many cases, providing a foundation for systematic auditing of empirical results.

title: From Uncertain Judgments to Calibrated Rankings: Conformal Elo Estimation for LLM Evaluation
summary: Evaluating new large language models typically requires costly human annotation campaigns at scale. LLM-as-a-judge offers a cheaper alternative, but judge scores carry systematic errors - such as position bias, self-preference, or intransitivity - that can strongly miscalibrate the resulting rankings. We quantify the resulting judge-human disagreement at two complementary levels. At the local level, we estimate per-battle uncertainty from the judge's own score differences by propagating calibrated win probabilities rather than hard labels into the Bradley-Terry procedure. This alone provides a drastic improvement to Elo estimation accuracy, bringing LLM-derived ratings within 17.9 Elo MAE of human-derived ones when averaged over 55 held-out models on LMArena. At the global level, we apply split conformal prediction to the residual gap between LLM-derived and human-derived Elo ratings across held-out models, producing prediction intervals with distribution-free marginal coverage guarantees that account for irreducible LLM-human disagreement. Together, these two layers yield a low-cost evaluation tool that provides developers with calibrated Elo estimates and honest uncertainty bounds, without access to large-scale human annotations.To facilitate reproducibility, we release our code at https://github.com/kargibora/SoftElo .

AI.tldr: A two-layer approach improves LLM-as-a-judge evaluations by estimating uncertainty through probabilistic win propagation and conformal prediction, reducing Elo estimation error without costly human annotations.
AI.motivation: Address systematic errors in LLM-as-a-judge evaluations (position bias, self-preference, intransitivity) that miscalibrate rankings, while reducing reliance on costly human annotation campaigns.
AI.method: Two complementary methods: 1) Local level - propagate calibrated win probabilities rather than hard labels into Bradley-Terry procedure using per-battle uncertainty estimation; 2) Global level - apply split conformal prediction to residual gaps between LLM-derived and human-derived Elo ratings.
AI.result: Significant improvement: LLM-derived ratings within 17.9 Elo MAE of human-derived ones when averaged over 55 held-out models on LMArena. Provides prediction intervals with distribution-free marginal coverage guarantees accounting for LLM-human disagreement.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents a two-layer approach to improve LLM-as-a-judge evaluations by addressing systematic errors through probabilistic win propagation and conformal prediction, yielding calibrated Elo estimates with uncertainty bounds without requiring costly human annotations.

title: Distributional Loss for Robust Classification
summary: This paper proposes a novel loss concept for supervised classification tasks. Rather than enforcing a direct mapping from each input sample to a single assigned label, we define an optimization objective over all classifier outputs as a bimodal Gaussian distribution.
