This softer target formulation implicitly captures class ambiguity, mitigates overfitting, and encourages the learning of more robust decision boundaries, all without requiring additional label information. Experimental results demonstrate consistent improvements in robustness, with particularly pronounced gains in low-data regimes, while requiring only minimal modifications to standard training pipelines.

AI.tldr: A novel bimodal Gaussian loss function for classification tasks improves robustness and mitigates overfitting by softening target labels without requiring additional label information, especially effective in low-data regimes.
AI.motivation: To address issues in supervised classification tasks by developing a softer target formulation that captures class ambiguity, mitigates overfitting, and encourages robust decision boundaries without requiring additional label information.
AI.method: The method defines an optimization objective over all classifier outputs as a bimodal Gaussian distribution instead of enforcing a direct mapping from inputs to single labels.
AI.result: Experimental results demonstrate consistent improvements in robustness, with particularly pronounced gains in low-data regimes, while requiring only minimal modifications to standard training pipelines.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that the proposed bimodal Gaussian loss function improves robustness, especially in low-data regimes, and can be easily integrated into standard training pipelines without requiring additional label information.

title: ReSET: Accurate Latency-Critical NVFP4 Reasoning via Step-Aware Temperature Scaling
summary: Large reasoning models (LRMs) improve complex problem-solving by generating long intermediate reasoning traces, but this substantially increases inference costs. NVFP4 inference offers a promising approach to reduce both computational and memory costs through hardware-supported low-precision execution. However, directly applying NVFP4 to LRMs introduces two practical limitations: reasoning accuracy degrades under quantization, and existing NVFP4 kernels do not fully realize latency benefits in small-batch autoregressive decoding. In this work, we analyze the effect of NVFP4 quantization on token-level uncertainty during reasoning. We show that quantization increases incorrect sampling at low-entropy symbolic tokens, while causing over-concentration on a small set of tokens in high-uncertainty reasoning steps. Based on this observation, we propose \textbf{ReSET}, a reasoning-step entropy-based temperature-scaling method that estimates step-level uncertainty online and adapts the decoding temperature using both token-level and step-level entropy signals. To address the latency gap, we further design a CUDA-core small-$M$ NVFP4 kernel for latency-critical autoregressive decoding. Across reasoning benchmarks and model scales, ReSET improves NVFP4 reasoning accuracy by up to $\sim\!$2 points over the NVFP4 baseline. Our CUDA-core small-$M$ kernel further improves latency-critical decoding, delivering up to $2.5\!\times$ kernel-level speedup over NVFP4 vLLM and approximately $2\!\times$ end-to-end decoding speedup over BF16. Code is available at https://github.com/aiha-lab/ReSET.

AI.tldr: ReSET improves NVFP4 accuracy for large reasoning models via entropy-based temperature scaling, plus optimized CUDA kernel for faster decoding.
AI.motivation: Large reasoning models improve problem-solving with long reasoning traces but increase inference costs. NVFP4 inference reduces computational/memory costs via low-precision execution, but direct application causes: 1) reasoning accuracy degradation under quantization, 2) existing NVFP4 kernels don't fully realize latency benefits in small-batch autoregressive decoding.
AI.method: 1) Analyze NVFP4 quantization effects on token-level uncertainty during reasoning. 2) Propose ReSET - reasoning-step entropy-based temperature-scaling that estimates step-level uncertainty online and adapts decoding temperature using token-level and step-level entropy signals. 3) Design CUDA-core small-M NVFP4 kernel for latency-critical autoregressive decoding.
AI.result: ReSET improves NVFP4 reasoning accuracy by up to ~2 points over NVFP4 baseline. CUDA-core small-M kernel delivers up to 2.5× kernel-level speedup over NVFP4 vLLM and ~2× end-to-end decoding speedup over BF16.
AI.conclusion: ReSET improves NVFP4 reasoning accuracy by up to ~2 points over baseline and delivers up to 2.5× kernel-level speedup, enabling efficient low-precision execution for large reasoning models.

title: Decoding Insect Song: A Multitask Semisupervised Orthoptera Bioacoustic Classifier
comment: ICML 2026 Workshop on Machine Learning for Audio
summary: Passive acoustic monitoring holds great promise for ecological inference, yet existing automated tools are typically narrowly trained and non-transferable. We address these limitations with PULSE, a semi-supervised, multi-task framework for Orthoptera bioacoustics, combining weakly-supervised species classification, self-supervised learning on unlabelled field audio, and knowledge distillation from a general-purpose bioacoustic model. Our domain-adapted specialist model outperforms a state-of-the-art general model across all metrics (macro F1: 0.21 vs. 0.07; AUC: 0.74 vs. 0.45; AP: 0.32 vs. 0.19), with active learning further raising F1 to 0.34 and AUC to 0.84. Beyond classification, the learned embeddings encode ecologically meaningful structure, exposed through an interactive visualisation tool for ecological discovery.

AI.tldr: PULSE is a semi-supervised, multi-task framework for Orthoptera bioacoustics that outperforms state-of-the-art models and provides ecologically meaningful embeddings for discovery.
AI.motivation: To address limitations in existing automated tools for passive acoustic monitoring which are typically narrowly trained and non-transferable, especially for Orthoptera bioacoustics.
AI.method: A semi-supervised, multi-task framework combining weakly-supervised species classification, self-supervised learning on unlabelled field audio, and knowledge distillation from a general-purpose bioacoustic model. The approach includes domain adaptation and active learning.
AI.result: The domain-adapted specialist model outperforms state-of-the-art general models (macro F1: 0.21 vs. 0.07; AUC: 0.74 vs. 0.45; AP: 0.32 vs. 0.19). Active learning further improves performance to F1: 0.34 and AUC: 0.84. Learned embeddings encode ecologically meaningful structure.
AI.conclusion: PULSE demonstrates a novel framework for bioacoustic analysis that overcomes limitations of existing tools, delivering superior performance while providing ecological insights through learned embeddings and interactive visualization tools.

title: Towards More General Control of Diffusion Models Using Jeffrey Guidance
summary: A key strength of diffusion models lies in their flexibility, since their outputs can be controlled at sampling time through guidance. However, beyond simple cases such as conditional sampling, the target distribution is often left implicit, defined only through a sampling rule or a heuristic energy function. To address this, we propose Jeffrey guidance, a principled framework that extends diffusion-model control to applications beyond what standard guidance can express. It leverages Jeffrey's rule of conditioning to update marginal distributions towards a prescribed target, preserving the conditional structure and minimally perturbing the joint distribution. We first demonstrate Jeffrey guidance by targeting a prescribed embedding distribution. With Inception embeddings as the target, this leads to substantial reductions in FID on both CIFAR-10 and FFHQ. We further apply Jeffrey guidance to fairness on CelebA-HQ, updating an unconditional diffusion model to enforce independence between attributes.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Jeffrey guidance, a principled framework for controlling diffusion models beyond standard guidance methods by using Jeffrey's rule to update marginal distributions toward prescribed targets while preserving conditional structure.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models offer flexibility through guidance during sampling, but current methods are limited to simple cases like conditional sampling, with target distributions often left implicit through heuristic energy functions.
AI.method: The paper introduces Jeffrey guidance, which leverages Jeffrey's rule of conditioning to update marginal distributions toward a prescribed target while preserving conditional structure and minimally perturbing the joint distribution.
AI.result: When targeting Inception embeddings, Jeffrey guidance substantially reduces FID on both CIFAR-10 and FFHQ. It also successfully enforces independence between attributes on CelebA-HQ for fairness applications.
AI.conclusion: Jeffrey guidance provides a principled and flexible framework for diffusion-model control that goes beyond standard guidance methods, offering improvements in generation quality, fairness enforcement, and overall controllability while preserving the model's original structure.

title: To GAN or Not To GAN: Segmentation Analysis on Mars DEM
summary: To better understand Martian Surface, which is needed to enable Rovers navigate Mars with ease, it is necessary to be able to determine the location of mounds. Detecting and studying these morphologies can also help us find evidence of extraterrestrial life, in this case, more specifically, water or signs of life conducive environments. Detection of mounds was done by manually mapping morphological parameters onto Digital Elevation Models. This paper solves the problem by automatically detecting and or predicting mounds on Mars using Neural Network based Semantic Segmentation methodologies. This is done by using supervised semantic segmentation model and generative adversarial approach. A comparison of the approaches shows that adding extra artificially generated data did not improve the result.

AI.tldr: Neural network semantic segmentation can automatically detect Martian mounds; GAN-generated data didn't improve results over supervised segmentation alone.
AI.motivation: To enable rovers to navigate Mars more effectively by understanding Martian surface topography. Detecting mounds can help find evidence of water or signs of life-conducive environments, which is important for astrobiology research.
AI.method: Used neural network-based semantic segmentation methodologies including supervised semantic segmentation models and generative adversarial approaches (GANs) for generating additional training data. Compared both approaches.
AI.result: The comparison showed that adding extra artificially generated data via GANs did not improve mound detection results compared to using only supervised semantic segmentation.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that using supervised semantic segmentation is effective for automatic mound detection on Mars, while adding artificially generated data via GANs did not improve results, suggesting that quality labeled data is more important than quantity of synthetic data for this task.

title: Extracting Governing Equations from Latent Dynamics via Multi-View Contrastive Learning
summary: Identifying latent dynamical systems from noisy, high-dimensional measurements is a central problem at the intersection of representation learning, system identification, and scientific discovery. We present DYSCO, a multi-view temporal contrastive learning algorithm that jointly recovers latent trajectories and the governing dynamics from such observations, by leveraging multiple independent noisy views of the same underlying process to disentangle signal from noise. By parameterizing the dynamics in a structured functional basis, our framework further enables symbolic recovery of the governing equations within an affine gauge. We offer theoretical guarantees for strong identification up to an affine indeterminacy, extending prior identifiability results to the realistic setting of noisy nonlinear observations. Empirically, we demonstrate accurate recovery of both latent trajectories and flow fields across a diverse set of dynamical regimes (e.g., chaotic, oscillatory, and metastable) under both Gaussian and Poisson observation noise, the latter being particularly relevant for neural recordings.

AI.tldr: DYSCO: Multi-view contrastive learning for joint latent trajectory and dynamics recovery from noisy observations, with symbolic equation recovery and guarantees under realistic noise conditions.
AI.motivation: Need to identify latent dynamical systems from noisy, high-dimensional measurements, bridging representation learning, system identification, and scientific discovery.
AI.method: Multi-view temporal contrastive learning algorithm that leverages independent noisy views to disentangle signal from noise, with structured functional basis parameterization for symbolic equation recovery within affine gauge.
AI.result: Theoretical guarantees for strong identification up to affine indeterminacy, empirical demonstration of accurate recovery across diverse dynamical regimes (chaotic, oscillatory, metastable) under Gaussian and Poisson observation noise.
AI.conclusion: DYSCO provides a principled framework for joint latent trajectory recovery and system identification from noisy multi-view measurements, enabling scientific discovery of dynamical systems from high-dimensional observations.

title: Different Layers, Different Manifolds: Module-Wise Weight-Space Geometry in Transformer Optimization
comment: Accepted at WSS @ ICML 2026, code is available at https://github.com/kiratoyoshihara/module-wise-manifold-muon
summary: Weight-space geometry plays a central role in neural network optimization, yet manifold constraints are often applied uniformly across all weight matrices. In this work, we ask whether different transformer modules prefer different manifold geometries. We study Manifold Muon for GPT-2 pretraining and compare layer-wise assignments of Stiefel and DGram constraints across attention and MLP blocks. Our results show a clear asymmetry: constraining attention layers with Stiefel geometry while assigning DGram geometry to MLP layers gives the best performance among the tested configurations, whereas the inverted assignment and all-DGram configuration become unstable under the shared hyperparameter setting. We trace this failure to singular value growth in DGram-constrained attention weights, which can amplify attention logits and induce softmax saturation. These findings suggest that symmetry-aware and geometry-aware optimization for transformers should be module-specific rather than uniform.

AI.tldr: Attention layers prefer Stiefel geometry while MLP layers prefer DGram geometry in transformer optimization, requiring module-specific rather than uniform manifold constraints
AI.motivation: To investigate whether different transformer modules prefer different manifold geometries rather than applying uniform constraints across all weight matrices
AI.method: Applied Manifold Muon to GPT-2 pretraining with layer-wise assignments of Stiefel and DGram constraints across attention and MLP blocks, comparing different configurations
AI.result: Constraining attention layers with Stiefel geometry and MLP layers with DGram geometry yields best performance; inverted assignments and all-DGram configurations become unstable due to singular value growth in DGram-constrained attention weights
AI.conclusion: Layer-specific manifold geometry constraints are essential for transformer optimization, with attention layers preferring Stiefel geometry and MLP layers preferring DGram geometry

title: Once-for-All: Scalable Simultaneous Forecasting via Equilibrium State Estimation
summary: We introduce Equilibrium State Estimation (ESE), a novel paradigm for simultaneous prediction, where multiple interacting systems require separate yet coordinated forecasts. Such scenarios often arise in real-world settings such as economics and healthcare modeling. Unlike existing approaches that predict one system at a time, ESE forecasts all systems in a single pass. It first estimates the equilibrium state across systems, then generates holistic forecasts based on the difference between the current state and the estimated equilibrium. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets, including currency exchange and COVID-19 spread modeling, demonstrate that ESE is at least as accurate as state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods while being significantly faster. In addition, ESE integrates seamlessly with conventional predictors, combining their accuracy with its exceptional efficiency and delivering a 10-70x speedup. With linear-time complexity, ESE scales far better than SOTA methods as the number of systems increases. Moreover, it remains accurate under diverse perturbations, establishing ESE as a fast, generalizable, robust, and scalable multi-prediction method.

AI.tldr: ESE is equilibrium-based simultaneous prediction that forecasts all systems in one pass, matching accuracy while being 10-70x faster.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of existing simultaneous prediction approaches that predict one system at a time for multiple interacting systems.
AI.method: Estimates equilibrium state across systems, then generates holistic forecasts based on difference between current state and equilibrium.
AI.result: At least as accurate as SOTA methods, 10-70x faster, scales better with linear-time complexity, robust under diverse perturbations.
AI.conclusion: ESE establishes a fast, generalizable, robust, and scalable multi-prediction method that outperforms existing approaches.

title: Clipping Makes Distributed and Federated Asynchronous SGD Robust to Stragglers
summary: In modern machine learning, parallelization of training is an important strategy for increasing scale. Asynchronous stochastic gradient descent (ASGD), which maximizes the utilization of available hardware by avoiding waiting for slow workers. However, with constant step sizes, the convergence of ASGD is nonetheless affected negatively by slow workers due to large delays in updates. At the same time, it has been empirically observed in asynchronous training of deep learning models that gradient clipping "stabilizes" training. In this work, we provide a theoretical justification for this behavior, as we show that clipping removes the dependence of the maximum delay in the oracle complexity. We employ a sub-Weibull model of gradient noise which generalizes sub-Gaussian and sub-exponential distributions to more heavy-tailed distributions, motivated by empirical observations in deep learning. We show convergence in expectation, and the first time in asynchronous optimization, convergence with high probability.

AI.tldr: Gradient clipping theoretically stabilizes asynchronous SGD by eliminating delay sensitivity and enables strong convergence guarantees.
AI.motivation: ASGD is crucial for parallel training but suffers convergence issues due to slow workers and large delays, while empirically gradient clipping stabilizes training but lacks theoretical justification.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of ASGD with gradient clipping under a sub-Weibull model for gradient noise, proving convergence properties including high probability convergence.
AI.result: Gradient clipping removes dependence on maximum delay in oracle complexity, enables convergence in expectation and high probability convergence under heavy-tailed gradient noise distributions.
AI.conclusion: Gradient clipping is theoretically justified as an effective technique to stabilize asynchronous SGD training by removing the negative impact of maximum delay on convergence, with the approach showing strong convergence guarantees including high probability convergence.

title: Quantizing Time-Series Models As Dynamical Systems: Trajectory-Based Quantization Sensitivity Score
comment: ICML 2026, Workshop on Forecasting as a New Frontier of Intelligence
summary: We introduce the Trajectory-based Quantization Sensitivity Score (TQS), a metric that reframes post-training quantization (PTQ) through the lens of dynamical-systems stability. By modeling the network's rollout as a discrete-time dynamical system, TQS characterizes how quantization-induced errors propagate and amplify over the rollout horizon. Unlike conventional PTQ methods, where sensitivity analysis is often coupled to the quantization procedure, TQS enables a priori sensitivity estimation decoupled from quantizer selection and bit-width assignment. This separation allows for quantization budget planning even for black-box or compiled networks with fused operators. Building on this, we present TQS-PTQ, a flexible mixed-precision framework that requires no calibration data or costly second-order approximations. Our experiments show that a dynamical-systems perspective provides a robust, high-performing pathway for low-precision deployment in resource-constrained settings.

AI.tldr: TQS metric reframes quantization via dynamical systems, enabling data-free mixed-precision PTQ.
AI.motivation: To reframe post-training quantization through the lens of dynamical-systems stability, enabling a priori sensitivity estimation decoupled from quantizer selection.
AI.method: Developed TQS-PTQ, a flexible mixed-precision framework that requires no calibration data or costly second-order approximations.
AI.result: TQS enables quantization budget planning even for black-box or compiled networks with fused operators. TQS-PTQ shows robust performance in low-precision deployment.
AI.conclusion: The dynamical-systems perspective provides a robust, high-performing pathway for low-precision deployment in resource-constrained settings.

title: Rarity-Gated Context Conditioning for Offline Imitation Learning-Based Maritime Anomaly Detection
summary: Contextual anomaly detection aims to identify abnormal behavior conditional on context variables, but practical deployments often face highly imbalanced context distributions where rare regimes can be critical information. Under such frequency bias, context-conditioned models can produce unstable decisions and excessive false alarms in rare contexts. We propose Rarity-Gated Feature-wise Linear Modulation (RGFiLM), a rarity-aware conditioning module that combines feature-wise modulation (i.e., context-conditioned scaling and shifting of hidden features) with a gate controlled by a data-driven rarity score. The rarity score is estimated from the empirical distribution of context variables and regulates how strongly context modulates intermediate representations: the gate becomes more decisive under rare contexts while remaining conservative under frequent contexts. We evaluate RGFiLM on maritime trajectory anomaly detection using AIS motion sequences with ERA5 environmental context in an environment-sensitive detour scenario. When instantiated in a sequential anomaly scoring pipeline, RGFiLM achieves the best mean F1--False Positive Rate (FPR) trade-off among the compared context-agnostic and context-conditioned methods. These results suggest that explicitly accounting for context rarity is an effective approach for reducing false alarms in context-sensitive anomaly detection.

AI.tldr: Proposes RGFiLM, a rarity-aware conditioning module that uses a gate controlled by context rarity scores to regulate feature modulation, reducing false alarms in contextual anomaly detection with imbalanced context distributions.
AI.motivation: Contextual anomaly detection faces challenges with highly imbalanced context distributions where rare regimes are critical. Under frequency bias, context-conditioned models produce unstable decisions and excessive false alarms in rare contexts.
AI.method: Rarity-Gated Feature-wise Linear Modulation (RGFiLM) which combines feature-wise modulation with a gate controlled by a data-driven rarity score estimated from the empirical distribution of context variables. The rarity score regulates how strongly context modulates intermediate representations.
AI.result: When instantiated in a sequential anomaly scoring pipeline for maritime trajectory anomaly detection using AIS motion sequences with ERA5 environmental context, RGFiLM achieves the best mean F1-False Positive Rate (FPR) trade-off among compared context-agnostic and context-conditioned methods.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly accounting for context rarity is an effective approach for reducing false alarms in context-sensitive anomaly detection.

title: Navigating the Safety-Fidelity Trade-off: Massive-Variate Time Series Forecasting for Power Systems via Probabilistic Scenarios
summary: Probabilistic forecasting models are increasingly deployed on multivariate systems with distinct channel physics and operational constraints, but existing benchmarks evaluate neither property at scale. Public canonical multivariate benchmarks cap out at 2,000 channels, while power-system benchmarks either lack temporal structure or probabilistic evaluation. We introduce PowerPhase, a probabilistic forecasting benchmark built on six transmission grids ranging from 2,000 to 36,964 jointly forecasted channels, more than an order of magnitude beyond popular canonical multivariate benchmarks. Each target trajectory is the output of an AC power-flow solve, and PowerPhase ships with constraint-aware metrics, including Safety_mBrier, NECV, and CVaR-alpha, that complement CRPS and Distortion. Across eight baselines and three seeds, distributional accuracy and constraint satisfaction rank models differently, a trade-off we term safety-fidelity. We further propose PowerForge, a scenario-based quantile forecaster with type-specific decoding heads and a causal bridge between variable groups, which achieves the best average rank on every grid.

AI.tldr: New probabilistic forecasting benchmark for power systems with up to 37K channels and constraint-aware metrics reveals safety-fidelity trade-off, with proposed model PowerForge achieving best performance.
AI.motivation: Existing probabilistic forecasting benchmarks are insufficient for multivariate power systems because they either have too few channels (max 2,000) or lack temporal structure/probabilistic evaluation. There is a need for benchmarks that evaluate both channel physics and operational constraints at scale.
AI.method: The authors introduce PowerPhase, a benchmark with up to 36,964 jointly forecasted channels based on AC power-flow solves. They propose constraint-aware metrics (Safety_mBrier, NECV, CVaR-alpha) and introduce PowerForge, a scenario-based quantile forecaster with type-specific decoding heads and causal bridge between variable groups.
AI.result: PowerPhase benchmark shows that distributional accuracy and constraint satisfaction rank models differently (safety-fidelity trade-off). PowerForge achieves the best average rank on every grid among eight baselines across three seeds.
AI.conclusion: The authors present PowerPhase as a new benchmark for probabilistic forecasting in high-dimensional power systems and PowerForge as a model that achieves best performance on this benchmark while addressing the safety-fidelity trade-off.

title: Enhanced Low-Density Region Exploration in Classifier-Guided Diffusion Models Through Modified Reverse Diffusion Sampling
summary: Diffusion models have emerged as state-of-the-art generative models for high-fidelity image synthesis, particularly in their classifier-free guided and classifier-guided forms. However, standard classifier guidance concentrates probability mass around high-density class mean, leading to poor coverage of rare samples in the tails of the class-conditional distributions. Recent work on diffusion-based tail sampling mitigates this by training an additional low-density-seeking classifier with a synthetic-vs-real discriminator, at the cost of additional networks and training. In parallel, a number of samplers and distillation techniques accelerate or refine diffusion sampling, but do not explicitly address long-tail coverage. We propose a purely sampling-time, density-aware extension of classifier-guided conditional diffusion model that targets low-density regions without any additional training. We have applied guidance at noisy images not on predicted noise like most diffusion models. Starting from a pretrained conditional diffusion model and classifier on ImageNet, we modify the guided reverse dynamics by steering trajectories toward low-confidence regions via the modified classifier gradient, and at each time step, we also guide the sampling process toward the predicted real image. 1st guidance helps explore low-probability samples, and 2nd guidance helps to generate samples to be close to the real data manifold. The proposed sampler consistently improves ADM model recall at 64x64 resolution while maintaining a comparable FID, and with a 256x256 ADM model, we showed the results visually with different combinations of both guidance. We also showed that standard ADM classifier guidance, combined with predicted real image guidance, helps generate high perceptual quality samples with a 256x256 ADM model on ImageNet.

AI.tldr: Sampling-time extension for diffusion models that guides toward low-density regions without extra training, improving rare sample coverage while maintaining quality.
AI.motivation: Standard classifier guidance in diffusion models concentrates probability mass around high-density class means, leading to poor coverage of rare samples in distribution tails. Existing tail sampling methods require additional networks and training, while acceleration/refinement techniques don't address long-tail coverage.
AI.method: Modifies guided reverse dynamics by: 1) steering trajectories toward low-confidence regions via modified classifier gradients, and 2) guiding sampling toward predicted real images at each time step. Uses pretrained ImageNet conditional diffusion models.
AI.result: The proposed sampler consistently improves ADM model recall at 64×64 resolution while maintaining comparable FID. With 256×256 ADM model, visual results demonstrate improved sample diversity and coverage of low-density regions while preserving perceptual quality.
AI.conclusion: The paper proposes a novel sampling-time extension to classifier-guided diffusion models that improves coverage of rare samples without additional training. This approach successfully generates high-quality samples from low-density regions while maintaining competitive FID scores.

title: VideoMDM: Towards 3D Human Motion Generation From 2D Supervision
summary: We introduce VideoMDM, a diffusion-based framework that trains 3D human motion priors directly from accurate 2D poses extracted from monocular videos, without any 3D ground truth. A pretrained 2D-to-3D lifter provides approximate 3D pose sequences that serve as a noisy teacher: these are diffused, denoised by the model in 3D, and supervised in 2D by reprojecting the prediction and comparing against accurate keypoints. We show that, under mild assumptions, a depth-weighted 2D reprojection loss is equivalent in expectation to direct 3D supervision, and we adapt standard 3D motion regularizers - velocity consistency and over-parameterized representation alignment - to this 2D setting. Unlike methods that lift 2D to 3D only at inference, VideoMDM learns a coherent 3D motion manifold during training. On HumanML3D it nearly closes the gap to fully 3D-supervised MDM (FID 0.88 vs 0.54); On real video datasets Fit3D and NBA the method learns to generate motions consistently preferred by humans, with strong quantitative results.

AI.tldr: VideoMDM trains 3D motion priors from 2D video poses without 3D supervision using diffusion and 2D reprojection losses, achieving near-supervised performance.
AI.motivation: To learn 3D human motion priors directly from monocular videos without requiring 3D ground truth data, which is expensive and difficult to obtain.
AI.method: Uses a noisy 3D teacher from a pretrained 2D-to-3D lifter, diffuses the approximate 3D poses, then denoises them in 3D while supervising in 2D via a depth-weighted reprojection loss. Adapts 3D motion regularizers (velocity consistency and over-parameterized representation alignment) to the 2D setting.
AI.result: On HumanML3D, nearly closes the gap to fully 3D-supervised MDM (FID 0.88 vs 0.54). On real video datasets Fit3D and NBA, learns to generate motions consistently preferred by humans with strong quantitative results.
AI.conclusion: VideoMDM demonstrates that high-quality 3D human motion priors can be learned directly from 2D video data without 3D supervision, achieving performance close to fully supervised methods and producing human-preferred motions on real datasets.

title: Positional Encoding in the Context of Memristor-Based Analog Computation for Automatic Speech Recognition
summary: Memristors provide a new chance for resource-efficient computation of neural models for natural language processing by enabling analog execution of vector-matrix-multiplication. Yet, computations on these devices are currently subject to larger distortion, both in weight programming and execution. In this work, we identify large output values of transformed positional encodings to cause major degradation within analog-to-digital conversion (ADC) as part of memristor-based computation. By adjusting the proportion of weight and precision bits of the ADC of specific memristor layers, we reduce the degradation of the execution by ~50% relative, while keeping the estimated energy consumption stable. Additionally, we investigate scenarios where the ADC cannot be modified. In that case the degradation can be reduced by ~30% relative after removing encoding-related linear transformations.

AI.tldr: Optimizing ADC configurations mitigates positional encoding distortion in memristor-based NLP computation
AI.motivation: Memristors enable efficient analog computation for NLP but suffer from distortion in weight programming and execution, particularly due to large positional encoding values degrading ADC performance
AI.method: Analyzed degradation causes and implemented two approaches: 1) Adjusting ADC weight/precision bit proportions for specific memristor layers, 2) Removing encoding-related linear transformations for fixed-ADC scenarios
AI.result: ADC adjustments reduced degradation by ~50% with stable energy consumption; fixed-ADC scenarios saw ~30% improvement by removing encoding linear transformations
AI.conclusion: Adjusting ADC configurations to handle large positional encoding values significantly improves memristor-based computation performance for neural language models, offering practical solutions for analog NLP hardware.

title: Hölder++: Improving the Quality-Coherence Trade-off in Multimodal VAEs
summary: Existing approaches for multimodal variational autoencoders (VAEs) face a trade-off between generative quality and coherence-i.e., they struggle to generate realistic and diverse samples that, at the same time, are semantically consistent across modalities. A recent work shows that using a simple approximation to Hölder pooling as an aggregation method improves coherence over the SOTA MMVAE+, despite assuming a single shared representation across all modalities. Yet, it slightly compromises sample diversity. Inspired by this insight, we propose Hölder++, a novel multimodal VAE that improves the generative quality-coherence trade-off through: (i) the first implementation of Hölder pooling without any approximation for multimodal VAEs; (ii) an extended architecture that models distinct shared and private (i.e., modality-specific) representations (Hölder+); and (iii) hierarchical inference that further enhances the disentanglement between the shared and private representations (Hölder++). Our experiments corroborate that Hölder++ consistently improves the generative quality-coherence trade-off, yields more structured latent spaces, and learns shared representations that are informative for downstream tasks.

AI.tldr: string
AI.motivation: string
AI.method: string
AI.result: string
AI.conclusion: string

title: PolyFlow: Safe and Efficient Polytope-Constrained Flow Matching with Constraint Embedding and Projection-free Update
summary: While flow-based generative models have demonstrated strong performance across a wide range of domains, deploying them in safety-critical physical systems remains challenging due to strict constraint requirements. Existing approaches typically enforce safety through post-hoc corrections, which incur substantial computational overhead and may distort the learned distribution. We propose PolyFlow, a polytope-constrained flow matching framework that embeds constraints directly into the model and flow dynamics. PolyFlow introduces a discrete-time flow formulation and a projection-free architecture, which eliminate the discretization error and guarantee strict satisfaction of arbitrary polyhedral constraints, without the need for expensive iterative solvers. Experimental results show that PolyFlow achieves zero constraint violation while maintaining high distributional fidelity across a range of planning and control tasks. Compared to state-of-the-art constrained generation baselines, PolyFlow significantly reduces inference latency and demonstrates a favorable trade-off between safety, efficiency, and generative quality. Code is available on https://github.com/MJianM/PolyFlow.

AI.tldr: PolyFlow is a polytope-constrained flow matching framework that directly embeds constraints into the model to achieve zero violation with high efficiency and fidelity, outperforming existing constrained generation methods.
AI.motivation: Flow-based generative models have strong performance but face challenges in safety-critical physical systems due to strict constraint requirements. Existing approaches use post-hoc corrections which are computationally expensive and can distort the learned distribution.
AI.method:
1. Polytope-constrained flow matching framework (PolyFlow) 
2. Discrete-time flow formulation
3. Projection-free architecture
4. Direct embedding of constraints into model and flow dynamics
AI.result:
1. Achieves zero constraint violation
2. Maintains high distributional fidelity across planning and control tasks
3. Significantly reduces inference latency compared to state-of-the-art constrained generation baselines
4. Demonstrates favorable trade-off between safety, efficiency, and generative quality
AI.conclusion: PolyFlow proposes an effective constrained flow matching framework that embeds polyhedral constraints directly into the model, achieving zero constraint violation while maintaining high distributional fidelity and reducing inference latency compared to baseline methods.

title: Accelerating Speculative Diffusions via Block Verification
summary: Speculative decoding speeds up LLM inference by using a draft model to generate tokens, with an acceptance-rejection scheme that ensures that the output matches the target distribution. Adapting this to continuous diffusions is difficult because speculative sampling requires drawing from a residual distribution. While straightforward in discrete spaces, efficiently sampling this residual in continuous space is non-trivial. Consequently, existing diffusion adaptations either use computationally inefficient sampling techniques or rely on an alternative scheme. In this work, we introduce a novel scheme that efficiently implements the original speculative sampling mechanism for diffusion models. Our approach offers a critical advantage over current methods: it enables us to adapt block verification from LLMs to diffusions -- which provably improves the acceptance rate of drafts. Furthermore, we formalize and analyze the Free Drafter, a heuristic self-speculative drafter for diffusions that requires no training. By enabling block verification, our Free Drafter yields up to a 6.3% speedup over existing speculative methods with no additional training and negligible overhead beyond the existing parallel verification pass.

AI.tldr: Novel efficient speculative sampling for diffusion models enables block verification and achieves 6.3% speedup with Free Drafter heuristic.
AI.motivation: Existing adaptations of speculative decoding to continuous diffusion models are inefficient or rely on alternative schemes, as sampling from residual distributions in continuous space is non-trivial.
AI.method: Develop a novel speculative sampling scheme for diffusion models that efficiently implements the original mechanism, enabling block verification from LLMs to be adapted to diffusions, and formalize the Free Drafter heuristic.
AI.result: The approach successfully implements speculative sampling for diffusions, enables block verification to improve draft acceptance rates, and achieves up to 6.3% speedup over existing speculative methods.
AI.conclusion: Our speculative sampling scheme enables efficient block verification for diffusion models, achieving speedups of up to 6.3% over existing methods with minimal overhead.

title: How Much Memory Do We Need? Adaptive Memory Gate for Neural Operators
summary: Neural operators have emerged as a powerful data-driven approach for solving time-dependent PDEs. Among recent advances, memory-augmented neural operators explicitly incorporate past states and have achieved remarkable performance under low-resolution observation settings. However, existing approaches apply a fixed memory weight regardless of observation conditions, such as resolution or physical parameters, limiting their adaptability. Our preliminary experiments reveal that optimal memory weight varies with resolution and viscosity, implying that a fixed memory weight cannot simultaneously optimize performance across diverse settings. We propose AMGFNO, which dynamically modulates memory weight through a learnable gate. On the Kuramoto-Sivashinsky and Burgers' equations, AMGFNO achieves 55-79% nRMSE reduction over at low resolution, with the learned gate value automatically decreasing from $\bar{g} \approx 0.7$ to near-zero as resolution increases.

AI.tldr: AMGFNO introduces adaptive memory gating for neural operators that dynamically adjusts memory utilization based on observation conditions, eliminating the need for fixed memory weights.
AI.motivation: Fixed memory weights in existing memory-augmented neural operators limit adaptability across diverse observation conditions (resolution, physical parameters). Optimal memory weight varies with conditions, but current approaches use static weights that cannot simultaneously optimize performance across different settings.
AI.method: AMGFNO dynamically modulates memory weight through a learnable gate mechanism that adjusts based on input observation conditions. The gate function is integrated into the neural operator architecture, enabling the model to automatically determine optimal memory utilization.
AI.result: On Kuramoto-Sivashinsky and Burgers' equations, AMGFNO achieves 55-79% nRMSE reduction at low resolution. The learned gate value automatically decreases from approximately 0.7 to near-zero as resolution increases, demonstrating adaptive behavior.
AI.conclusion: AMGFNO offers a memory-weight adaptation mechanism that eliminates hyperparameter tuning and enables a single model to excel across diverse PDE observation conditions, providing superior accuracy and versatility compared to fixed-weight memory operators.

title: Clustering Node Attributed Networks with Graph Neural Networks and Self Learning
summary: Graph clustering - partitioning the node set of a graph into disjoint subsets that reflect some latent information - is a fundamental problem as it finds applications in a myriad of different scenarios. While this classic problem has been tackled for decades by different communities, a recent variation of the problem driven by real data considers the scenario where nodes have attributes that are also informative. This has triggered novel methods that simultaneously leverage network information (edges) and node information (attributed) in the design of novel clustering algorithms. This work proposes a novel framework that builds on prior works that have applied graph neural networks (GNN) to graph clustering. The proposed framework operates in rounds of self learning in a fully unsupervised setting. In each round, a GNN generates representations for nodes that are used to cluster the nodes. This clustering influences the graph used to generate the node representation in the next round. Moreover, a context graph built in each round using the original graph is used to generate the node representations. Empirical results show that the proposed methodology extracts information from both network edges and node attributes in synthetic data, outperforming algorithms focused solely on the network or attributes when neither are very informative. Multiple rounds of learning also improve the performance and always outperforms a long single round of training (i.e., classic GNN graph clustering). When considering real datasets, empirical results indicate that the proposed methodology is competitive to state-of-the-art methods when cluster sizes are balanced.

AI.tldr: Novel unsupervised graph clustering framework using GNNs with multiple rounds of self-learning, leveraging both edges and attributes through iterative refinement.
AI.motivation: Need for methods that simultaneously leverage both network structure (edges) and node attributes in graph clustering, building upon prior GNN approaches but addressing their limitations.
AI.method: A self-learning framework with multiple rounds where a GNN generates node representations used for clustering, which then influences the graph structure for subsequent rounds, augmented by a context graph built from the original graph.
AI.result: Outperforms algorithms focused only on network or attributes when neither is very informative; multiple rounds improve performance over single-round GNN clustering; competitive with state-of-the-art on real datasets with balanced clusters.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework with multiple self-learning rounds improves upon classic GNN graph clustering and is competitive with state-of-the-art methods on balanced real datasets.

title: Uncertainty Estimation for Molecular Diffusion Models
summary: Diffusion models have seen wide adoption for 3D molecular generation, yet they offer no principled signal of when a generated molecule is likely to be of low quality. We propose a post-hoc method for estimating per-sample uncertainty in pretrained molecular diffusion models. Building on a Laplace approximation of the denoising network, we measure the variability of the noise prediction across the generation trajectory. Empirically, we show that the resulting uncertainty score is informative of sample quality, exhibiting a negative correlation with established sample-level quality metrics. We further study how the proposed uncertainty score can be used to filter generated samples, improving model performance via test-time scaling.

AI.tldr: A post-hoc uncertainty estimation method for molecular diffusion models using Laplace approximation, enabling identification of low-quality samples.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models lack principled signals to identify when generated molecules are likely to be of low quality, limiting their practical utility in molecular generation tasks.
AI.method: Develop a post-hoc method using Laplace approximation of the denoising network to measure noise prediction variability across the generation trajectory.
AI.result: The uncertainty score correlates negatively with established sample-level quality metrics and can be used for test-time filtering to improve model performance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed uncertainty estimation method provides a practical way to identify low-quality generated molecules in diffusion models, enabling test-time filtering that can improve overall model performance.

title: Reinforcement Learning for Neural Model Editing
summary: Editing pretrained neural networks requires specialized algorithms tailored to specific objectives. Designing such algorithms is often time-consuming and demands significant effort. We present an exploratory framework that formulates neural model editing as a reinforcement learning problem, where agents modify models using reward feedback. We introduce two environments: MaskWorld, where agents scale weights multiplicatively, and ShiftWorld, where agents apply additive weight updates. The reward function combines a utility-preservation objective with a task-specific editing objective, enabling agents to learn targeted modifications while maintaining overall model performance. We evaluate the framework on bias mitigation in text classification and machine unlearning in image classification, both of which traditionally rely on specialized algorithms. Our results show that the learned policies reduce forget set accuracy to nearly 0% while preserving over 90% retain set accuracy on the unlearning task. In the bias mitigation setting, the learned policies improve bias-related performance by more than 5% while maintaining general classification utility. Our findings show that neural model editing can be cast as a reinforcement learning problem, allowing editing policies to be learned from reward feedback rather than manually engineered for each task.

AI.tldr: RL framework for neural network editing with MaskWorld and ShiftWorld environments achieves strong results in bias mitigation and machine unlearning by learning editing policies from rewards instead of manual engineering.
AI.motivation: Traditional neural network editing requires specialized algorithms that are time-consuming to design and tailored to specific objectives. The authors aim to create a more general framework that can learn editing policies automatically.
AI.method: The paper introduces an exploratory framework that formulates neural model editing as a reinforcement learning problem. It creates two environments: MaskWorld (multiplicative weight scaling) and ShiftWorld (additive weight updates). The reward function combines utility-preservation with task-specific editing objectives. The method is evaluated on bias mitigation in text classification and machine unlearning in image classification.
AI.result: For machine unlearning, learned policies reduce forget set accuracy to nearly 0% while preserving over 90% retain set accuracy. For bias mitigation, learned policies improve bias-related performance by more than 5% while maintaining general classification utility.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that neural model editing can be effectively formulated as a reinforcement learning problem, allowing editing policies to be learned from reward feedback rather than manually engineered for each task. This approach shows promising results in both bias mitigation and machine unlearning tasks.

title: MaxProof: Scaling Mathematical Proof with Generative-Verifier RL and Population-Level Test-Time Scaling
summary: We present MaxProof, a population-level test-time scaling framework for competition-level mathematical proof in the MiniMax-M3 series. M3 first trains three proof-oriented capabilities -- proof generation, proof verification, and critique-conditioned proof repair -- using a defense-in-depth generative verifier engineered for low false-positive rate. These capabilities are merged into a single released M3 model. At test time, MaxProof treats the model as a generator, verifier, refiner, and ranker, searches over a population of candidate proofs, and returns one final proof through tournament selection. With MaxProof test-time scaling, the M3 model reaches 35/42 on IMO 2025 and 36/42 on USAMO 2026, exceeding the human gold-medal threshold on both.

AI.tldr: MaxProof scales proof capabilities through test-time tournament selection
AI.motivation: Achieve competition-level mathematical proof performance exceeding human gold-medal thresholds
AI.method: Population-level test-time scaling using tournament selection over candidate proofs
AI.result: M3 model reaches 35/42 on IMO 2025 and 36/42 on USAMO 2026
AI.conclusion: MaxProof framework enables competition-level mathematical proofs through test-time scaling

title: SupraBench: A Benchmark for Supramolecular Chemistry
summary: Supramolecular chemistry, which includes the study of non-covalent host-guest assemblies, has advanced various applications. However, designing host-guest systems remains time-consuming, requiring days of dry-lab verification per candidate pair. Although LLMs have emerged as a fast alternative with strong performance on molecular binding tasks, no benchmark currently systematically evaluates LLMs for host-guest reasoning across fundamental supramolecular chemistry tasks, e.g., binding affinity prediction. To this end, we collaborate with domain experts to release the first Supramolecular Benchmark, called SupraBench, to evaluate LLMs in chemistry reasoning. Specifically, we design four fundamental tasks, i.e., binding affinity prediction, top-binder selection, solvent identification, and host-guest description, plus an auxiliary vision-based task for molecular identification. We also release SupraPMC, a curated 16M-token corpus of Supramolecular chemistry articles distilled from Europe PMC, to support the adaptation to the supramolecular domain. We benchmark a broad range of open and proprietary LLMs and find that LLMs leave substantial headroom across all tasks. Domain adaptation pretraining over SupraPMC transfers cleanly to in-distribution regression but trades off against strict letter-format output. Moreover, the difficulty profile differs sharply across task families, revealing distinct failure modes that indicate specific gaps in current supramolecular chemistry reasoning. Our source codes and benchmark datasets are available at https://github.com/Tianyi-Billy-Ma/SupraBench.

AI.tldr: First benchmark (SupraBench) for evaluating LLMs on supramolecular chemistry tasks shows LLMs have significant room for improvement, with domain adaptation helping but creating trade-offs, and different task types revealing distinct reasoning gaps.
AI.motivation: There's currently no systematic benchmark to evaluate LLMs for host-guest reasoning in supramolecular chemistry, despite LLMs showing promise as a fast alternative to time-consuming dry-lab verification for designing host-guest systems.
AI.method: Created SupraBench benchmark with four fundamental tasks (binding affinity prediction, top-binder selection, solvent identification, host-guest description) plus a vision-based molecular identification task, and released SupraPMC corpus (16M tokens) for domain adaptation. Evaluated diverse open and proprietary LLMs across all tasks.
AI.result: LLMs show substantial headroom for improvement across all tasks. Domain adaptation pretraining using SupraPMC transfers well to in-distribution regression but trades off against strict letter-format output requirements. Different task families exhibit sharply different difficulty profiles, revealing distinct failure modes that indicate specific gaps in current supramolecular chemistry reasoning.
AI.conclusion: SupraBench reveals substantial headroom for LLM improvement in supramolecular chemistry reasoning, with domain adaptation pretraining helping in-distribution tasks but creating trade-offs, and different task families showing distinct failure modes that point to specific gaps in current chemical reasoning capabilities.

title: CRAFTIIF: Cross-Resolution Analytic Four-Type Interpretable Isolation Forest for Multivariate Time Series Anomaly Detection
comment: 14 pages, 4 figures, 2 appendices. Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering (TKDE). Code: https://github.com/smitswil/craftiif
summary: Anomaly detection in multivariate time series is challenged by four structurally distinct anomaly types -- point (isolated spikes), distributional (level shifts), temporal (rhythm changes), and collective (inter-sensor correlation breakdowns) -- each requiring different feature representations. Most unsupervised methods target only one or two types and provide limited interpretability. We present CRAFTIIF (Cross-Resolution Analytic Four-Type Interpretable Isolation Forest), a fully unsupervised framework targeting all four types without dataset-specific tuning. CRAFTIIF generates K=500 random analytic wavelet feature draws across four families (Morlet, DOG, Haar, Coiflet), each targeting a specific anomaly type, feeding five structured Isolation Forests -- one per type plus a meta-IF for compound anomalies. An adaptive Otsu/MAD threshold calibrates detection automatically across anomaly rates from 0.1% to 69.2%. Because each IF is trained exclusively on type-specific features, branch firing provides direct anomaly-type attribution by construction, without post-hoc explanation. Evaluated on all 19 datasets of the mTSBench benchmark (Zhou et al., TMLR 2026), CRAFTIIF achieves mean F1=0.228 (all 19 datasets) and F1=0.322 (13 detectable datasets), ranking first among all 25 evaluated methods on VUS-PR (0.463 vs. previous best 0.329, +40.7%). A diagnostic framework -- oracle F1, detectability limits, and branch separation ratios -- identifies 6 of 19 datasets as fundamentally undetectable by any unsupervised method. Ablation over 11 conditions confirms adaptive thresholding (+38% F1), four-branch structure (+20%), and meta-IF (+23%) are each essential. Code: https://github.com/smitswil/craftiif

AI.tldr: CRAFTIIF is an unsupervised anomaly detection framework using wavelet features and structured Isolation Forests to detect all four multivariate time series anomaly types without dataset-specific tuning, achieving state-of-the-art performance with interpretable attribution.
AI.motivation: Current unsupervised anomaly detection methods for multivariate time series typically target only one or two anomaly types, provide limited interpretability, and require dataset-specific tuning. There's a need for a comprehensive framework that can detect all four structurally distinct anomaly types without manual parameter adjustment.
AI.method: Generates 500 random analytic wavelet feature draws across Morlet, DOG, Haar, and Coiflet families, each targeting a specific anomaly type. Five structured Isolation Forests are used (one per type plus a meta-IF for compound anomalies) trained exclusively on type-specific features. Adaptive Otsu/MAD thresholding calibrates detection automatically.
AI.result: Achieved mean F1=0.228 across all 19 datasets and F1=0.322 on 13 detectable datasets in the mTSBench benchmark, ranking first among 25 methods with 40.7% improvement in VUS-PR (0.463 vs. previous best 0.329). Adaptive thresholding improved F1 by 38%, four-branch structure by 20%, and meta-IF by 23%. Identified 6 datasets as fundamentally undetectable by any unsupervised method.
AI.conclusion: CRAFTIIF introduces a novel unsupervised framework that achieves superior performance in detecting all four anomaly types through structured Isolation Forests and automatic thresholding, while also providing interpretable anomaly attribution. It reveals fundamental detectability limits in some benchmarks.

title: A2D2: Fine-Tuning Any-Length Discrete Diffusion for Adaptive Decoding
summary: Discrete diffusion models offer a simple and stable likelihood-based framework for sequence generation, recently extended to any-length settings via token insertion. Principled reward-guided fine-tuning for any-length discrete diffusion, however, remains largely unexplored. We introduce Fine-Tuning Any-Length Discrete Diffusion for Adaptive Decoding (A2D2), a unified framework for reward-guided fine-tuning of any-length discrete diffusion models via joint optimization of the insertion and unmasking policies together with a quality-based inference schedule. We derive the Radon-Nikodym derivative for the joint insertion-unmasking path measures, enabling theoretically guaranteed convergence to the intractable reward-tilted sequence distribution without requiring target samples. Building on this, we establish unmasking and insertion quality as tractable approaches for minimizing decoding error and introduce the Adaptive Joint Decoding (AJD) loss, which provably yields the optimal path measure that generates the reward-tilted distribution. Empirically, A2D2 improves reward optimization while enhancing generation flexibility and accuracy over prior fixed-length fine-tuning and inference-time guidance methods.

AI.tldr: A2D2 framework enables reward-guided fine-tuning for any-length discrete diffusion via joint optimization of insertion/unmasking policies with adaptive scheduling, outperforming previous methods.
AI.motivation: Reward-guided fine-tuning for any-length discrete diffusion models remains largely unexplored despite the framework's potential for flexible sequence generation.
AI.method: Derivation of Radon-Nikodym derivative for joint insertion-unmasking path measures, introduction of Adaptive Joint Decoding (AJD) loss that provably yields optimal path measure, and joint optimization of insertion/unmasking policies with quality-based inference schedule.
AI.result: Empirically, A2D2 improves reward optimization while enhancing generation flexibility and accuracy over prior fixed-length fine-tuning and inference-time guidance methods.
AI.conclusion: A2D2 provides a theoretically grounded framework for reward-guided fine-tuning of any-length discrete diffusion models through joint optimization of insertion/unmasking policies and inference scheduling, outperforming previous fixed-length and inference-time guidance approaches.

title: Adjusted Cup-Product Neural Layer
summary: Many important observables in physics and geometry are cup products of cochains. The adjusted cup product neural layer has been introduced in this paper. It is a neural primitive that hard wires the cup product with an adjustment term from higher gauge theory. This creates a readout that is gauge invariant by design. Their main theoretical result shows that on a closed cycle the output relies entirely on the adjustment coefficient. Setting this coefficient to zero removes the output completely regardless of other parameters. Thus the adjustment is the only source of gauge invariant signal. They prove this observable is a nonzero quadratic form and is exactly invariant under one and two gauge transformations.

AI.tldr: Introduces adjusted cup product neural layer with gauge-invariant readout; adjustment coefficient is sole source of gauge invariant signal.
AI.motivation: Many important observables in physics and geometry are cup products of cochains, and there's a need to create gauge-invariant observables through neural network architectures.
AI.method: Introduces an adjusted cup product neural layer that hard wires the cup product with an adjustment term from higher gauge theory, creating a gauge-invariant readout by design.
AI.result: The main theoretical result shows that on a closed cycle, the output depends entirely on the adjustment coefficient. Setting it to zero removes the output completely, proving the adjustment is the only source of gauge invariant signal.
AI.conclusion: The paper demonstrates that the adjustment coefficient is crucial for creating gauge-invariant observables in neural networks, with the theoretical proof showing it's a nonzero quadratic form that remains invariant under gauge transformations.

title: Existence Precedes Value: Joint Modeling of Observational Existence and Evolving States in Time Series Forecasting
summary: Real-world time series are often highly incomplete and irregular due to sensor dormancy, transmission delays, and event-driven sampling, making reliable forecasting fundamentally challenging. Existing methods have evolved from impute-then-forecast pipelines to continuous-time models such as Neural ODEs and continuous-time graph networks. While these approaches improve the modeling of historical irregularity, they still rely on an implicit oracle assumption at inference time: the timestamps of future valid observations are presumed to be known in advance. This assumption limits practical relevance, since in many real systems the more fundamental question is not only what the future value will be, but also whether a valid observation will occur at all. In this paper, we propose Timeflies, a unified framework that reformulates forecasting as a joint problem of future observability inference and value estimation. To explicitly model the interaction between observation dynamics and state evolution, Timeflies adopts an observation stream and a value stream, coupled through three dedicated modules for reliability-aware embedding, observation-guided dependency modeling, and joint prediction. We further construct Shadow, a benchmark that combines natural missingness from public datasets with real-world industrial data, and introduce the Observation-Value Joint Entropy (OVJE) metric to comprehensively evaluate this coupled predictability. Extensive experiments show that Timeflies consistently outperforms existing methods, highlighting the importance of explicitly modeling future observability in time series forecasting with missing values. Code and dataset are available in https://github.com/ant-intl/Timeflies.

AI.tldr: Timeflies jointly predicts future observability and values for irregular time series using dual-stream architecture
AI.motivation: Existing methods rely on an unrealistic assumption that future observation timestamps are known in advance, limiting practical relevance for real-world applications where both observability and values need prediction
AI.method: A dual-stream architecture with observation stream and value stream coupled through reliability-aware embedding, observation-guided dependency modeling, and joint prediction modules
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate Timeflies consistently outperforms existing methods, showing the importance of explicit future observability modeling
AI.conclusion: Timeflies offers a unified framework for forecasting irregular time series by jointly predicting future observability and values, and experimental results show it consistently outperforms existing methods

title: Learning with Simulators: No Regret in a Computationally Bounded World
summary: Understanding the minimal assumptions necessary for generalization is the fundamental question in learning theory. Unfortunately, most results rely heavily on independence (or some proxy thereof) of the data-generating process, while results for strongly dependent data are far more limited. Towards addressing this gap, we introduce the framework of simulatable processes, where the learner has access to a simulator that approximates the distribution generating the data (which may be an arbitrarily complex and dependent process). Surprisingly, given access to such a simulator, we show that we can recover the same learning guarantees as in the classical setting with independent data, namely, error bounds that depend on the VC dimension. Further, we use this framework to study the power of conditional sampling and show strict statistical and computational advantages in this setting. As a highlight of our framework, we exhibit a single algorithm that simultaneously learns any given VC class under all processes samplable in bounded polynomial time, with regret controlled by the time-bounded Kolmogorov complexity of the process. This provides a significant conceptual broadening of the classical PAC model.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces simulatable processes, a framework for learning with dependent data where learners have simulator access, enabling classical VC-based guarantees without independence assumptions.
AI.motivation: Most learning theory results rely heavily on independence assumptions, while results for strongly dependent data are limited. The authors aim to address this gap by developing a framework that can handle arbitrarily complex and dependent data-generating processes.
AI.method: Introducing the framework of simulatable processes where learners have access to a simulator approximating the data-generating distribution. Developing algorithms that leverage this simulator access to recover classical learning guarantees.
AI.result:
1. Showed that with access to a simulator approximating the distribution, we can recover the same learning guarantees as in classical independent data settings (error bounds depending on VC dimension).
2. Demonstrated the power of conditional sampling in this framework, showing strict statistical and computational advantages.
3. Developed a single algorithm that simultaneously learns any given VC class under all processes samplable in bounded polynomial time, with regret controlled by the time-bounded Kolmogorov complexity of the process.
AI.conclusion: The paper introduces the simulatable processes framework as a significant conceptual broadening of classical PAC learning, enabling generalization guarantees under arbitrarily complex and dependent data-generating processes without requiring independence assumptions.

title: Simplex-Constrained Sparse Bagging: Transitioning from Uniform Priors to Sparse Posteriors in Ensemble Learning
summary: We present Simplex-Constrained Sparse Bagging (SCSB), a mathematically rigorous framework for post-training compression and probability calibration of bootstrap-based bagging ensembles. Standard bagging ensembles (such as Random Forests, Bagged SVMs, and Bagged Neural Networks) assign uniform voting power to all constituent estimators. However, this naive uniform prior ignores the varying local competence of base estimators and contributes to model overconfidence. We formulate ensemble pruning and calibration as a joint optimization problem over the probability simplex by minimizing the Out-Of-Bag (OOB) loss. To induce sparsity, we address the theoretical "L1-simplex paradox" -- the mathematical reality that the L1 norm is constant on the simplex and fails to prune -- by introducing a concave quadratic penalty. SCSB is model-agnostic and achieves up to 96% ensemble compression, yielding linear inference speedups and superior probability calibration (lowered Expected Calibration Error) while preserving or enhancing generalization accuracy.

AI.tldr: SCSB framework compresses bagging ensembles via simplex-constrained sparse optimization, improving calibration and speed without sacrificing accuracy.
AI.motivation: Standard bagging ensembles use uniform voting weights which ignore varying local competence of base estimators and lead to model overconfidence
AI.method: Formulates ensemble pruning and calibration as joint optimization over probability simplex with concave quadratic penalty to overcome L1-simplex paradox
AI.result: Achieves up to 96% ensemble compression, linear inference speedups, superior probability calibration (lowered ECE), while preserving or enhancing generalization accuracy
AI.conclusion: SCSB provides a mathematically rigorous approach to ensemble compression and calibration that offers practical benefits in speed and accuracy.

title: Beyond the Commitment Boundary: Probing Epiphenomenal Chain-of-Thought in Large Reasoning Models
summary: Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning is the dominant paradigm for inference-time scaling in language models, yet the causal influence of individual steps on the final answer poorly understood. We estimate each step's causal importance via early exit and use this measure to study how answers form across the reasoning traces of several model families. Across diverse tasks, we find that reasoning typically crosses a \emph{commitment boundary} -- a sharp transition from transient intermediate guesses to a stable, high-confidence answer. This transition often happens in a single step, well before the model's reasoning block ends, and is followed by \emph{epiphenomenal} CoT steps that leave the final answer probability unaltered. Using attention probes, we show that answer-formation stages can be linearly decoded from intermediate reasoning steps with high accuracy and generalize robustly to unseen reasoning tasks. We exploit this signal to early-exit reasoning blocks at the commitment boundary, reducing the length of CoTs up to 55\% on average with negligible impact on model performance.

AI.tldr: CoT reasoning exhibits a 'commitment boundary' where answers stabilize quickly; many subsequent steps are unnecessary. This enables early exiting to shorten CoTs by ~55% without performance loss.
AI.motivation: To understand the causal influence of individual Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning steps on final answers and identify patterns in how answers form across reasoning traces.
AI.method: Used early exit to estimate causal importance of individual CoT steps, analyzed attention probes to decode answer-formation stages linearly, and applied these insights to implement early-exit strategies at the commitment boundary.
AI.result: Found that reasoning typically crosses a commitment boundary - a sharp transition from transient intermediate guesses to stable high-confidence answers, often in a single step well before reasoning ends. Subsequent steps are epiphenomenal. Showed answer-formation stages can be linearly decoded from intermediate steps with high accuracy and generalize to unseen tasks. Achieved up to 55% reduction in CoT length through early exit at commitment boundary with negligible performance impact.
AI.conclusion: The study uncovers the commitment boundary phenomenon in Chain-of-Thought reasoning and demonstrates that many reasoning steps are epiphenomenal, enabling significant reduction in CoT length through early exit strategies without compromising performance.

title: The Stable Recovery Manifold: Geometric Principles Governing Recoverability in Continual Learning
summary: Catastrophic forgetting is often viewed as the destruction of previously learned knowledge during sequential learning. Building on the Accessibility Collapse framework, we investigate the geometric structure of recoverability in continual learning. Using Split CIFAR-100 and a sequentially trained ResNet-18, we analyze recoverability, representational drift, and recovery complexity across ten tasks. We introduce Recovery Subspace Dimensionality (k_t), a measure of the minimum number of singular directions required to preserve 90 percent of full probe performance. Contrary to our Recoverability Diffusion hypothesis, recovery dimensionality remains stable throughout training (mean k_t = 8.0) despite substantial representational drift. Principal-angle drift strongly predicts recoverability (r = -0.862), and a simple geometric model explains 82.2 percent of recoverability variance. These findings support the Stable Recovery Manifold hypothesis, suggesting that forgotten knowledge remains compactly decodable despite representational reorganization. The results indicate that catastrophic forgetting is primarily an accessibility and manifold-alignment problem rather than information destruction.

AI.tldr: Catastrophic forgetting is not about destroying learned knowledge but about accessibility - forgotten information remains compactly decodable despite representational reorganization, as shown through geometric analysis of recoverability in continual learning.
AI.motivation: To investigate the geometric structure of recoverability in continual learning using the Accessibility Collapse framework, challenging the common view that catastrophic forgetting represents destruction of previously learned knowledge.
AI.method: Analyzed recoverability, representational drift, and recovery complexity across ten tasks using Split CIFAR-100 and a sequentially trained ResNet-18. Introduced Recovery Subspace Dimensionality (k_t) to measure minimum singular directions needed to preserve 90% of full probe performance. Developed a geometric model to explain recoverability variance.
AI.result: Recovery dimensionality remained stable throughout training (mean k_t = 8.0) despite substantial representational drift. Principal-angle drift strongly predicted recoverability (r = -0.862), and the geometric model explained 82.2% of recoverability variance.
AI.conclusion: Catastrophic forgetting in continual learning is primarily an accessibility and manifold-alignment problem rather than information destruction. The forgotten knowledge remains compactly decodable despite representational reorganization, supporting the Stable Recovery Manifold hypothesis.

title: Dense Supervision, Sparse Updates: On the Sparsity and Geometry of On-Policy Distillation
summary: On-policy distillation (\textsc{OPD}) has recently become a prominent post-training recipe as it combines two desirable ingredients: on-policy student trajectories and dense teacher supervision, yet how this hybrid changes a model's parameters remains unclear. Across several language and vision-language model pairs and use cases, our analysis yields two main findings. On sparsity, \textsc{OPD}-style updates are small and coordinate-sparse. They are distributed across layers and are usually FFN-heavy. This sparse structure is operationally useful: training only the discovered subnetwork recovers nearly the same performance as full \textsc{OPD}. However, the sparsity-inducing SGD optimizer underperforms AdamW in our optimizer ablation, likely because dense teacher supervision preserves heterogeneous coordinate-wise gradient scales where AdamW's adaptive scaling remains useful. On geometry, the updates are numerically full-rank but spectrally concentrated; they lie mostly away from the principal singular subspaces of the source weights and fall disproportionately on coordinates where the source weights are close to zero. These findings suggest that dense teacher supervision does not turn \textsc{OPD} into ordinary dense parameter rewriting; instead, \textsc{OPD} retains important geometric signatures of on-policy post-training.

AI.tldr: OPD produces sparse, structured updates with geometric patterns distinct from dense parameter rewriting.
AI.motivation: To understand how on-policy distillation (OPD) - combining on-policy student trajectories and dense teacher supervision - actually changes model parameters, given its prominence as a post-training method.
AI.method: Analysis of OPD-style updates across language and vision-language model pairs, examining sparsity patterns, layer distribution, coordinate characteristics, and comparing optimizers.
AI.result: 1) OPD updates are small and coordinate-sparse, distributed across layers (FFN-heavy). Training only the discovered subnetwork recovers nearly same performance. 2) Updates are numerically full-rank but spectrally concentrated, lying away from principal singular subspaces and disproportionately on coordinates where source weights are close to zero.
AI.conclusion: OPD retains important geometric signatures of on-policy post-training rather than becoming ordinary dense parameter rewriting.

title: Understanding Truncated Positional Encodings for Graph Neural Networks
summary: Positional encodings (PEs) enhance the power of graph neural networks (GNNs), both theoretically and empirically. Two of the most popular families of PEs - spectral (e.g., Laplacian eigenspaces, effective resistance) and walk-based (polynomials of the adjacency matrix) - are theoretically equivalent in expressive power, with expressivity between the 1-WL and 3-WL tests. However, this equivalence assumes the GNN uses the "complete" version of these PEs, which requires $O(n^3)$ time and space complexity. Instead, practitioners commonly use truncated variants of these encodings, such as the first $k$ eigenspaces or powers of the adjacency matrix. However, the theoretical properties of these truncated PEs are unknown. In this work, we initiate the study of these truncated PEs. Theoretically, we show that, under truncation, several families of PEs are fundamentally different in expressive power. As a corollary, we show that truncated spectral PEs are no longer stronger than the 1-WL test. We also study a family of spectral PEs, the $k$-harmonic distances, to highlight the differences in expressive power of even closely related truncated PEs. Finally, we experimentally show that a mix of truncated PEs is preferable to any single family on real-world datasets.

AI.tldr: Truncated positional encodings have different expressive power than complete versions; spectral truncated PEs lose advantage over 1-WL; mixing truncated PEs works best.
AI.motivation: Practitioners commonly use truncated variants of positional encodings due to computational constraints, but the theoretical properties of these truncated PEs are unknown.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of truncated positional encodings including spectral, walk-based, and k-harmonic distances. Experimental validation on real-world datasets comparing different truncated PE families.
AI.result: 1. Under truncation, several families of PEs are fundamentally different in expressive power. 2. Truncated spectral PEs are no longer stronger than the 1-WL test. 3. Mix of truncated PEs performs better than any single family on real-world datasets.
AI.conclusion: Truncated positional encodings need careful selection and mixing for optimal performance on real-world datasets, as different families have fundamentally different expressive powers under truncation.
